However, comparison of the submitted revised RPCI showing only a total balance of P3,233,958.26, as against the total Inventory Ledger balance of P4,492,143.28, resulted in a material discrepancy of P1,258,185.02, suggesting that the Inventory Committee failed to conduct actual and complete physical count of all properties of the Municipality. The GSO-Designate also admitted that no PCs were maintained for real properties such as Land, Land Improvements, Buildings, Infrastructure Assets, Building and Other Structures due to absence of available documents indicating the accountable officers to the properties and their actual locations. Verification of records and interview with the Accounting Unit staff revealed that the PPELC for the real property accounts was already updated, however, the PPELC for Equipment, Furniture and Fixtures, Books and Other PPE accounts was still being updated since the said accounts still included most of the PPEs reclassified as Semi-Expendable items in CY 2024. The accounting unit staff assigned to monitor the inventory balances admitted that they only maintain an inventory monitoring report showing the acquisition, usage/consumption and unused balances of inventories instead of the SLCs. The RPCPPE as of December 31, 2024 included tangible items totaling P3,298,412.00, that did not meet the P50,000.00 capitalization threshold. Ten (10) unserviceable properties valued at P717,215.80 have not been disposed of. GSO-Designate prepared some IIRUPs and submissions but some items remained outstanding and discrepancies unresolved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to demand from the various NGOs/POs the submission of the liquidation documents and/or return of the total amount granted to properly account and settle the Due from NGOs/POs account.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Liquidation documents for the financial assistance granted to various associations had not been submitted to the Audit Team. Deficiencies noted included use of Reimbursement Expense Receipts (RERs) for transactions exceeding P1,000.00, lack of original sales invoices/delivery receipts, signs of tampering in sales invoices, revised or unsupported procurements, absence of Fund Utilization Reports certified by associations’ accountants, and lack of pictures/inspection certificates. Weak internal controls and lack of enforcement of documentary requirements were cited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (i) direct the concerned officials to take immediate action once defects or problems are encountered in the implementation of projects that hamper its completion within the stipulated period to avoid tying up of possible government funds in receivables, which may increase the risk of possible loss in case of non-collection and to avoid possible adversities that may cause additional expenditures to the agency; (ii) instruct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letter to the concerned contractor for the refund of the unrecouped advances to contractors for the abandoned projects, otherwise; and (iii) seek legal advice to facilitate the collection of unrecouped mobilization fees and/or the filing of appropriate legal action against the contractor.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: There is an on-going negotiation with the contractor since CY 2022 for the possible course of action to be taken to continue the implementation of the abandoned projects; however, no development was reported as at yearend. Management had yet to provide written comments/justifications on the matter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments and conduct regular monitoring of the status on the individual subsidiary ledger accounts composing the Due to NGAs account to avoid misrepresentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: There are long outstanding balances such as P4,800,000.00 pertaining to three PPAs abandoned by the contractor with mobilization fees paid and not recouped; P6,620,933.70 pertains to Municipality’s Share from Tobacco Excise Tax with portions aged up to over three years; other balances include project balances and unexpended savings not yet remitted to source agencies. Municipality requested DBM for reprogramming of unexpended balances. Management committed to analyze and effect necessary adjustments but had yet to provide full resolution.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, through the MSWMB, to: (i) fast track the finalization of the Solid Waste Management Plan and have it approved by the NSWMC to ensure that PPAs included in the plan are in accordance with the National Solid Waste Management Framework; and (ii) henceforth, hasten the implementation of all Solid Waste Management-related programs/project/activities to enforce proper sorting, segregation, composting, recycling and disposal of waste materials to efficiently address solid waste concerns within the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The municipality was still in the process of consolidating gathered data/information required in the preparation of Chapter IV (Waste Amount and Composition Survey) of the SWMP. After finalization of WACS, revision/updating of several components/chapters of the existing SWMP will be undertaken. The project for RCA/SWM at Brgy. Sto. Niño amounting to P200,000.00 was reprogrammed to another project; garbage collection remained limited to twice a month around Municipal Compound; no approved ten-year SWMP yet; hence, full compliance with RA 9003 not yet achieved.

observations.managementAction: The Mun. Accountant conducted analysis of existence of dormant fund transfers, recorded corrections in the books, and made a follow-up on the submission of reports. The GSO is still collecting data. Coordination with the GSO is still in progress. The Mun. Accountant ensured proper accounting and reporting of PPE transactions.
observations.reason: Determination of all items to be derecognized is still in progress. The GSO is still collecting data.

observations.managementAction: Printing of LRNLCs is still in progress. Reconciliation with relevant records remains unresolved in CY 2024.
observations.reason: Reconciliation with relevant records remains unresolved in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended, and Management agreed to:

13. Require the Municipal Treasurer/MGSO to timely submit the SSMI together with the RIS, and other documents to the Municipal Accountant to record the issuances in the books;

14. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen monitoring procedures on outstanding inventories and regularly communicate with the Municipal Treasurer to submit SSMI supported with proper documents to timely record the issuances in the books; and

15. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories semestrally and submit the RPCI to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer for reconciliation in their records, copy furnish COA.

16. We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to: (a) analyze the unreconciled balances of the Due from and Due to Other Funds account, identify the reconciling items and prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry to reflect the correct account balances in the Statement of Financial Position; and (b) strictly monitor the reciprocal accounts by conducting regular reconciliation thereof, to eliminate and avoid future discrepancies, if any.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require:

a. The Municipal Accountant to:

17. Apply the criteria on capitalization of PPE as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2022-004 to ensure proper classification of assets in the books; and

18. Draw a JEV to effect necessary adjustments in the books of accounts in CY 2024 as follows:

Debit: 
Accumulated Depreciation
P8,812,622.52
Prior Period Adjustment
P1,893,054.58

Credit:      
Property, Plant and Equipment 
P10,707,907.10

b.	The Municipal Treasurer to: 

19. Provide data to the Municipal Accountant relevant to items reclassified as semi-expendable property including information of its condition;

20. Update existing PPE and Inventory records and prepare and maintain the additional necessary forms/reports/ registries for purposes of record-keeping, and monitoring over issued semi-expendable properties provided in Section 4 of the Circular; 

21. Cancel the PAR of the semi-expendable property and issue ICS to the actual custodian of the items; and

22. Moving forward, issue ICS to actual custodians of items to establish monitoring, control and accountability over them.

We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

41. Require the business owners to submit a sworn statement of gross sales/ receipts/Audited FS if applicable, and assessment should be based thereon; and

42. Conduct examination on books of accounts, if in the validation of the personnel of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office that there will be under declaration of gross receipts by taxpayers, to establish effective enforcement and collection of taxes, fees and charges as provided in the Ordinance.

43. We recommended, and Management agreed that, thru the GFPS, to expedite the completion of the GAD Database and ensure that it contain SDD and other gender related information to facilitate the conduct of gender analysis, identify gender issues and determine gender-responsive PPAs for prioritization in programming and budgeting in the GAD Agenda and annual GPB, respectively

44. We recommended, and Management agreed that the GFPS formulate a GAD Agenda for the remaining term of the LCE in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and be made as basis in the preparation of the annual GPB.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to apply the capitalization threshold in
recognizing PPE accounts and reclassify to semi-expendable properties the PPE that
are below the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 per item/unit in conformity with
Item 3.7 of COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024.

We recommended and the Municipality agreed to create an Inventory Committee composed of
adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee, created for the one-time
cleansing of the PPE account balances, agreed to prepare a Physical
Inventory Plan and execute the Plan in accordance with COA Circular No.
2020-006.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to undertake the
procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts, and that the
Municipal Mayor file the request for authority to write-off the dormant
account as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We recommended and Management agreed to demand from the
accountable officers (AOs) with outstanding cash advances as of yearend
to submit immediately to the Accounting Office the required liquidation
reports and/or refund the unused amounts to the Cashier.

We recommended and Management agreed to cause the withholding of the
salaries of the AOs in case they fail to liquidate their outstanding cash
advances, pursuant to Item 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended and the Accountant agreed to course through the
inventory account all regular purchases and record issuances of supplies
and materials as they take place pursuant to Section 114, Chapter 7 of the
NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended and the Accountant agreed to record materials received,
issued and the balance both in quantity and amount through the
maintenance of Supplies Ledger Card in accordance with Appendices 7 of
the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended and the Supply Officer agreed to record all receipts and
issues of supplies and the balance in quantity through the maintenance of
Stock Card in accordance with Appendix 36 of the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I.

We recommended and Management agreed to require that physical count
of inventory items by type semestrally be conducted and reported in the
RPCI to be reconciled quarterly with the SLC and/or SC and control
accounts and any discrepancy/ies be immediately verified and adjusted.

We recommended and the Local School Board agreed to submit the SEF
utilization reports, quarterly and annually, to the concerned offices within
the prescribed period for timely evaluation and monitoring of its utilization
in compliance with the pertinent provisions in Item 6.1 of the DepEd, DBM,
and the DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended and the LSB, through the MLGU Focal Person/Uploader,
agreed to post the SEF utilization reports in at least three (3) conspicuous
public places, as well as on the MLGU's official website once it has been
established by management, not later than the 20th day after the end of
each quarter for full transparency and accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed to stop charging expenses not
related to disaster risk reduction and management to the Local Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Fund to maximize the use of the fund
under Section 5 of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and process
claims only with complete documentation.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Ensure that the National Food Authority (NFA) is considered as the
primary source for rice procurement under Section 1 of Executive Order
No. 51. For future procurement activities involving rice, the NFA should
be prioritize as the primary supplier unless a valid exemption is
obtained with proper documentation; and
b. In cases where procurement from sources other than NFA is deemed
necessary, the BAC conduct and document a thorough cost-benefit
analysis and market survey to justify the procurement decision which
include price comparisons and quality assessments to demonstrate
that the chosen supplier offers the most advantageous terms for the
government.

We recommended and Management agreed to furnish the Audit Team with
a copy of the contracts entered into by the MLGU and each of all the
documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation within five
working days from execution thereof, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-
001.

We recommended and the MSWD Office agreed to prepare the Agency
Action Plan and Accomplishment Report, ensuring that PPAS integrated
into other departments or special accounts are identified and incorporated
accordingly.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Accounting and
Budget Office to promptly establish a Responsibility Center then assign
RC codes or, if impractical to establish a separate RC, shall maintain
subsidiary ledgers/registries to record all GAD-related expenses and
financial transactions, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2021-008, in
order to facilitate the monitoring, accounting and preparation of the GAD-
related reports.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the persons
responsible for the audit suspensions and those liable for the
disallowances and charges to settle their accounts.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to properly recognize the semi-expendable items issued during the year and in the prior years, to present fairly the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management:
ensure that budget allocation for specific MOOE account is carefully studied and projected based on historical data and anticipated needs during budget preparation to have a reliable measurement base for fund utilization; and
in cases where the budget allocation for a specific MOOE is found to be insufficient, consider the applicability of fund augmentation, following the provisions of Section 336 of RA 7160 to have a flexible and adaptable budget; and
moving forward, ensure that all MOOE transactions are properly classified in the specific or appropriate MOOE account and use the Other MOE account to record expenses not falling under any of the specific MOOE accounts, for the fair presentation of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management:
direct the GSO to expedite the updating of all inventory records to facilitate the Inventory Committee’s conduct of the physical count of all PPE, and subsequently submit the corresponding inventory report to the Audit Team; and
require the GSO and Accounting Office to conduct reconciliation of records to ensure consistency and accuracy between the PCs maintained by the GSO and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Office, to address any discrepancy, if there’s any, to ultimately ensure the fair presentation of the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management require the Accounting Office, in coordination with the GSO, to exert best effort to locate relevant documents supporting the balances without details that would facilitate the reconstruction of their respective SLs, in order to establish the reliability and fair presentation of these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal GSO and the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of Sections 114 and 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs, particularly on the recognition of inventory and related inventory expense, to ensure reliable financial reporting, and strengthen internal control over the receipt, issuance, and utilization of inventories.

We recommended that the Management:
instruct the BAC to ensure the completeness of the required details in the RFQs to facilitate proper evaluation of the validity and propriety of the transactions of the LGU; and
refrain from referring to brand names in the specifications provided in the PRs and RFQs, in conformity with the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act RA 9184 to ensure fair competition in the procurement activities of the LGU.

We recommended that the Management:
avoid the practice of disbursing through reimbursement and ensure that all payments are made directly to the suppliers or service providers, to facilitate the proper withholding and remittance of applicable taxes; and
strictly adheres to the appropriate modes of procurement and consistently comply with the procedures prescribed under the Procurement Law and its IRR, to uphold the principles of transparency and accountability, while ensuring that the Agency obtains the most advantageous price and terms in all its procurement undertakings.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and concerned procurement staff to ensure that all CSE are procured through PS-DBM, as mandated, in faithful observance of the related procurement rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Management:
moving forward, ensure the preparation of an amended AIP that incorporates all additional development projects proposed to be funded from savings and realignments of the 20% DF; and
cause the preparation and enactment of a Supplemental Budget, based on the amended AIP, to reflect the necessary realignments and re-appropriations of funds and ensure that the revised AIP and the corresponding Supplemental Budget are submitted to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan, for proper review and approval prior to the implementation of the new projects.

We recommended that the Management ensure that the Utilization Report for the 20% DF accurately reflects all projects implemented, including new projects funded from savings and realignments, with the corresponding actual expenditures, to uphold transparency, maintain accountability, and provide stakeholders with a reliable information on the utilization of fund.

We recommended that Management direct the Offices concerned to submit the lacking documentary requirements for all land acquisitions to establish the legality and validity of the transactions and remind all concerned officials to refrain from processing and approving disbursements without the complete documentary requirements outlined in the aforementioned Circular.

We recommended that the Management exert all necessary efforts to expedite the transfer of land titles to the name of the LGU, in order to safeguard the Municipality’s interests and to ensure full compliance with the applicable legal and regulatory requirements.

We recommended that the Management direct the MDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the monthly submission requirements and ensure that the required reports are submitted on time to the COA Auditor and furnished to the OCD and DILG-LGOO, to facilitate timely oversight and promote transparency, accountability and effective monitoring of the LDRRMF utilization.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately revert the balance of LDRRMF, which remained unutilized for five (5) years, to the General Fund, in accordance with Item 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, and conduct regular fund aging analysis and monitoring to ensure timely utilization or reversion of trust fund balances, thereby promoting transparency, accountability, and optimal use of public resources.

We recommended that the Management:
instruct the Offices concerned to submit the necessary supporting documents for the payments of hazard pay and henceforth comply with the documentary requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001, to establish the validity, propriety and legality of such disbursements; and
strictly follow the provisions of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2016 and DBM-DSWD Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2018, particularly on the conditions for entitlement and the prescribed computation of hazard pay, to ensure accurate and lawful payment of the benefit and to avoid the risk of overpayment and improper utilization of public funds.

We recommended that Management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan for the immediate enactment of a resolution prescribing comprehensive policies and guidelines on the granting of donations or financial assistance, establishing a well-defined basis for determining eligibility and the corresponding amount to be granted, to ensure equitable, efficient, and transparent delivery of aid to its constituents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed through the (a) Municipal Assessor’s Office, Accounting Office, and Treasurer’s Office to ensure strict compliance with their respective duties and responsibilities to accurately assess and efficiently collect Real Property Taxes; and (b) Municipal Treasurer’s Office to intensify efforts to enhance the RPT collection rate by implementing more proactive and strategic collection measures.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) continue the close monitoring of the granting and liquidation of cash advances and ensure strict adherence to the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002; (b) conduct regular review of the subsidiary ledgers to identify and rectify all incorrectly classified transactions; and (c) observe the proper application of the Revised Chart of Accounts as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-009 to ensure the accuracy and reliability of financial records and reports.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) create an Inventory Committee (IC); and (b) instruct the (i) IC to conduct the physical count of inventories at least every six months and prepare the RPCI and RPCSP for the inventories counted; (ii) Municipal Accountant to (1) utilize the perpetual inventory method and maintain the necessary records in accounting for inventories; and (2) coordinate with the MTO for the regular reconciliation of inventory balances; and (iii) Municipal Treasurer to maintain the required records and reports for inventories.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) create an Inventory, Appraisal, and Disposal Committees; (b) instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality; (ii) submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team on or before January 31 of each year; and (iii) reconcile the inventory reports with the accounting records; (c) Acting Municipal Treasurer to (i) maintain PC and PAR for the proper control and monitoring of the LGU’s property and equipment; (ii) ensure that all vehicles in active use are registered with the LTO and covered with property insurance; and (iii) submit to the Audit Team copies of OR/CR of all motor vehicles and heavy equipment for verification of its ownership; (d) Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELC; (e) Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to (i) consider adopting the one-time cleansing option for PPE accounts; and (ii) in coordination with the accountable officers, prepare IIRUP to report all unserviceable properties; and (f) Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable PPEs in accordance with the prescribed guidelines and procedures.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the implementing and concerned offices to ensure continued adherence to the terms and conditions of current and future fund transfers from National Government Agencies to maximize fund utilization and achieve intended program outcomes.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the (a) Municipal Accounting Office to sustain its compliance with IPSAS 39 in accounting for employee benefits; and (b) Human Resource Management Office to continue to maintain and update the Municipality’s employees records.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) ensure continuous utilization of the iBLPS and maintain effective collection strategies to sustain and further improve revenue collection performance; (b) strictly impose fines on businesses found operating without securing or renewing their business permits, in accordance with Section 9.01 of the Municipal Revenue Code; (c) implement stricter measures to ensure that all business establishments operating within the locality possess valid business permits; (d) verify the status of closed or non-operational business establishments and, where applicable, apply the procedures for termination or retirement of business in accordance with Item (h) of Section 2D.04 of the Revenue Code; and (e) exercise the authority granted under Section 171 of RA No. 7160 for more accurate tax assessments.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to sustain its commendable efforts in the implementation of development projects, while ensuring continued compliance with Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160 and Item 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2024; and (b) submit all outstanding procurement-related documents as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to support the regularity and validity of the payments made.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the HRMO to (a) conduct a comprehensive study to develop an Organizational Structure and Staffing Pattern Plan, in order to determine the ideal number and composition of plantilla positions based on the municipality’s operational needs; (b) formulate policies and guidelines to assess the necessity and propriety of hiring JO and CoS personnel, taking into consideration the functions and capacity of existing regular employees; (c) implement a more robust internal control system for recruitment and selection processes to ensure transparency, accuracy of applicant information, and compliance with employment standards; and (d) ensure that the actual tasks performed by JOs and CoS personnel are consistent with the duties and responsibilities specified in their respective contracts.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the (a) LDRRM Office to (i) sustain its effective implementation of PPAs; and (ii) ensure the timely and accurate submission of monthly utilization reports to oversight agencies; and (b) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to (i) cease the transfer of unexpended/unobligated LDRRMF-CO to the STF and ensure proper reversion to the GF; and (ii) properly charge STF-related disbursements to the TF at the time of payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continue its efforts to enhance gender mainstreaming to ensure full compliance with relevant rules and regulations; (b) create a multi-year GAD Agenda as basis for the annual GAD Plans and Budget by using the templates provided in PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2018-04 dated September 19, 2018; (c) nominate the GFPS members for seminars/trainings on gender analysis to equip them with the knowledge and skills necessary for applying the HGDG tool; and (d) administer the HGDG test when attributing costs to GAD PPAs to ensure accurate and appropriate cost attribution.
We recommended and the LCE agreed through the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Offices to sustain the timely withholding and remittance of taxes in accordance with pertinent regulations.

We commended Management for the sustained observance of the GSIS Act of 1997, and recommended to which they agreed to continue the withholding and timely remittance of all premium contributions and loan amortizations of its employees as well as the government’s share to the fund.
We recommended and the LCE agreed through the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Offices to continue adhering to the requirement of R.A. No. 9679 or the Home Development Mutual Fund Law of 2009.
We recommended and the LCE agreed through the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Offices to continue the very good practice of promptly remitting all collections due to PhilHealth.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Concerned officials and employees to strictly follow the procedures for the
one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances prescribed in COA Circular
No. 2020-006, to wit:
 Inventory Committee to properly accomplish the RPCPPE containing all
the required details as prescribed under Section 124 of the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume I, while the City Accountant to reconcile the books with
the CGSO records to verify the cause and amount of discrepancies and
make the necessary adjustment, if any;
 City Accountant to draw an adjusting entry to reclassify the identified
semi-expendable properties worth P113,100.00 to its appropriate
accounts and ensure that there are no items below the P50,000.00
capitalization threshold are to be recorded as PPE in consonance with
COA Circular No.2022-004;
b. City Accountant to review the Lapsing Schedule and provide all the
necessary details of each PPE items pursuant to Section 111 (1) of PD
No. 1445; and
c. CGSO to obtain the TCTs of all City-owned parcels of land to establish
absolute ownership and mitigate possible legal risks in compliance with
Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant to properly record the tangible items which meet the definition
and recognition criteria of PPE but with cost below P50,000.00 under the
appropriate semi-expendable property account upon purchase and thereafter,
recognize as expense upon issuance to end-user; and
b. City Accountant and City General Service Officer to maintain the Semi-
Expendable Property Ledger Cards (SPLCs) and Semi-Expendable Property
Cards, respectively, to properly monitor the issuance of the semi-expendable
property to end-users.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineering Office to regularly coordinate with the CAO and provide copy
of the monitoring report and/or copy of the Certificate of Final Inspection and
Acceptance to ensure timely recording of the completed projects from the CIP
accounts to its appropriate PPE accounts; and
b. City Accountant to immediately reclassify the CIP accounts to the appropriate
PPE accounts as soon as the project is completed and accepted and recognize
the corresponding depreciation expense.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct a thorough inventory of all supplies and
materials twice a year and submit a report thereon to the Auditor in proper form,
summarized according to the type of inventory and properly and completely
accomplished in all particulars, in accordance with Section 124 of the MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. City Accountant and the City General Services Officer to maintain complete and
updated SLCs and SCs for each type of inventories for proper monitoring and
conduct periodic reconciliation of their records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to ensure that the due and demandable portion of Loans Payable – Domestic are reclassified from non-current liabilities to current liabilities, pursuant to Paragraphs 27 and 80 of the IPSAS 1, for fair presentation of the accounts in the FS; and
b. Municipal Treasurer make representation with the LBP to account for the noted variance in the loan accounts and thereafter, notify the Municipal Accountant to effect appropriate adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Account to recognize the Accounts Payable amounting to P682,814.88 in conformity with Paragraph 7 of IPSAS 1, and to make the necessary journal entries to reclassify the completed CIP – Infrastructure Assets account to its appropriate PPE account, taking into consideration the accumulated depreciation for the period elapsed, in the manner provided under Paragraph 7 of the IPSAS 1 and Section 50, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly post all the transactions in the general ledgers as basis for the preparation of the trial balances and henceforth, record the RPT/SET collections and recognize the income from RPT/SET using the provided sample journal entries, in compliance with Section 22, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular No. 2015-009:
Account Title Debit Credit
Cash – Local Treasury XX
Discount on Real Property Tax – Basic XX
Real Property Tax – Basic Receivables XX
To record the receipt of prompt payment for RPT
Deferred Real Property Tax – Basic XX
Real Property Tax – Basic XX
To recognize the income from RPT
Cash – Local Treasury XX
Discount on Special Education Tax XX
Special Education Tax Receivables XX
To record the receipt of prompt payment for SET
Deferred Special Education Tax XX
Special Education Tax XX
To recognize the income from SET

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to ensure proper classification of income accounts in strict adherence to COA Circular No. 2015-009 to fairly present the year-end balances of the same in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary journal entries to adjust the Government Equity and Accumulated Amortization – Computer Software accounts and henceforth, ensure that all Computer Software assets purchased by the Municipality and the corresponding amortization, if applicable, are recognized in the manner provided Paragraph 99 of IPSAS 31, to ensure the fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to record the appropriate Other Receivable account to recognize the overpayment made to the supplier amounting to P11,283.13 with the corresponding credit to Cash in Bank – Local Currency Current Account, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001, to book up the reconciling item; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to demand a refund from the supplier for the duplication of payment in the amount of P11,283.13, in compliance with Section 2 of the PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-003; to avoid issuance of Notice of Disallowance based on overpayment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and MGSO to coordinate and immediately verify, analyze, and reconcile their records, in compliance with Paragraph 27 of the IPSAS 1 and Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to identify the causes of the errors/deficiencies noted and make adjustments if necessary; and
b. Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the PPELCs for each class of PPEs, in compliance with Section 13 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II, to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the Municipality’s PPE accounts.
We also reiterated our prior year’s recommendations for the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:
a. MGSO to exert extra effort to locate the details and supporting documents and determine the person/s accountable for the missing PPE items and prepare the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs for items described as "non-existing" or "missing" in the RPCPPE as required under Sections 6.3.1 and 7.3 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, otherwise, comply the procedures provided under Section 7.11 of the same Circular if accountability could not be established despite exhaustion of all diligent efforts; and
b. MGSO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee, to strictly comply with the disposition, derecognition and disposal procedures of all missing and unserviceable PPEs, as required under COA Circular No. 2020-006, to ascertain the existence and accountability of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the SLCs for each commodity/stock, in compliance with Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, as basis for the reconciliation with the SCs maintained by the MGSO; and
b. MGSO to prepare the RPCI in the prescribed format specifically showing the stock numbers, unit values, balances of the inventory items per stock cards and the resulting variance when compared with the actual count, as required under Section 56 and Appendix 48 of the same Manual, Volume II, to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the Municipality’s Inventory accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to formulate and adopt more aggressive collection strategies; otherwise, consider availing the remedies provided under Sections 176 and 256 of RA No. 7160 to enforce the collection and reduction of delinquent real property taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to be more assertive in notifying the recipients of the payroll to claim their pay and immediately sign the payroll sheets, and henceforth, strictly comply with Items 5.1.1 and 5.1.3 of the COA Circular No. 97-002, for the immediate liquidation of the cash advances for payroll and travel fully supported by documentary requirements, and to refrain from granting additional cash advances unless the liquidation of the previous cash advance is submitted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MGSO and Municipal Engineer to verify the status of the projects under the Guaranty/Deposit Payable accounts aged more than one year from the date of retention of money due to them and establish whether the terms and conditions of the contract are faithfully adhered to by the concerned suppliers; and
b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to require the concerned suppliers, after the lapse of the warranty period, to secure the necessary documents needed to process the refund of their guaranty deposit, in compliance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009, Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 6.2 of Annex E of the same Revised IRR, to return the suppliers and contractors’ retention money.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. MGSO to prepare and issue the ICS for semi-expendable property totaling P129,370.00, and to likewise prepare and maintain the SPC, RegSPI, and RSPI, in compliance with Section 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004, for the establishment of proper control and accountability over the semi-expendable property; and
b. Municipal Accountant to maintain the SPLC, in compliance with Section 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004, to record the acquisition, description, custody, impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history, and other information about the property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that DVs are duly numbered and properly prepared with complete details, in compliance with Section 32 and Annex 24 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II, to ensure utmost transparency and traceability of the documents, especially disbursement of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to monitor and refrain from granting cash advances to the AOs beyond the maximum cash accountability; otherwise, consider increasing their fidelity bond based on the highest amount held in custody and disbursed in the preceding year, in accordance with Section 305 (f) of RA No. 7160 and Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 to protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the loans and insurance applications of the employees and ensure that payroll deductions are in accordance with the order of preference provided by RA No. 11975 and that the employees’ net take-home pay shall not be less than the minimum amount of P5,000 to ensure employee’s work efficiency and effectiveness.

We strongly recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to support the balances of PPE and Accumulated Depreciation with reliable/correct lapsing schedules in consonance with Section 111 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance with Paragraph 71 of IPSAS 17 by computing for the depreciation of the PPEs costing P25,044,193.78 and thereafter, draw the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reflect the correct balances of the PPEs in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to draw JEVs to reclassify the misclassified PPEs costing P10,889,851.29 to their proper PPE accounts, to ensure the correct presentation of the affected account balances in the financial statements, in accordance with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw JEVs to adjust the net understatement of Depreciation Expense and Accumulated Depreciation in the amount of P2,595,203.53 to accurately present the PPE balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant the certified list of the names of the taxpayers and the corresponding amount of taxes due and collectible, in the manner prescribed in Section of 20 of MNGAS, Volume I, for LGUs; and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the discrepancy between their records and make the necessary adjustments to come up with a reliable RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the misclassified intangible asset recorded under ICT Equipment by debiting Computer Software and Accumulated Depreciation – ICT Equipment accounts in the amounts of P25,471,500.00 and P66,444.58, respectively, and crediting ICT Equipment and Prior Period Adjustment accounts by same respective amounts to ensure the correct presentation of the affected account balances in the financial statements, in accordance with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct that:
a. Inventory Committee to prepare a complete inventory of all supplies and materials at least once a year by type of inventory and submit a report thereon to the Auditor in proper form, summarized according to the type of inventory and properly accomplished in all particulars, in accordance with Sections 194 and 206 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated SLCs for each type of Inventories for proper monitoring and conduct periodic reconciliation of accounting and property records, in compliance with Section 202 of the same Manual.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, at the earliest and practicable period, initiate the one-time cleansing of PPE balances of the Municipality by following the guidelines and procedures on the six activities prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, to eliminate the noted discrepancy amounting to P17,995,663.08 as of December 31, 2024 to come up with reliable PPE balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to determine the details of the noted difference of P7,090,544.05 for the CIB-LCCA between their respective records, and subsequently prepare the necessary adjustments to present the correct balance of the CIB-LCCA, duly supported by certified reconciliation statements of Accounting and Treasury’s records; and
b. conduct periodic reconciliation of their records for particularly for the CIB-LCCA at least quarterly as required under Section 181(c) of the GAAM, Volume I and Section 63(F) of the LTOM.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare and submit immediately the lacking BRS for Bank Account Numbers LBP 2392-1003-10 and LBP 2392-1002-99 under General Fund and LBP 2392-1002-80 under the Trust Fund to determine the reconciling items for adjustment in the books of accounts in consonance with PD No. 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011; and
b. Effect the immediate adjustment of valid reconciling items in the books of accounts, as reflected in the BRS for LBP 2392-1003-02 under the SEF, with net amount of P161,235.46, to ensure the accuracy of the account balances appearing in the Municipality’s financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Municipality’s records with the DSWD and prepare the necessary adjusting entry to revert to the Due to NGAs account the unsupported Liquidation Reports for fund transfers; and
b. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to submit the required Liquidation Reports for fund transfers from DSWD within the reglementary period in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to take up the unexpired portion of insurance as at year-end by debiting Prepaid Insurance and crediting Prior Year Adjustment both accounts in the amount of P2,667,630.06 to reflect the correct balances of affected accounts; and
b. Henceforth, strictly observe the application of accrual basis in the recognition of expenses to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements in compliance with PPSAS 1 and Section 4.b of GAM for LGUs, Volume I.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in identifying/ locating the related documents for the remaining P3,980,871.91 variance found in the BRS and thereafter, draw journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustments and correction in the books for a fair presentation of the balance of the Cash in Bank account in the financial statements; and
b. Instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to identify the cause/s of the P3,267,358.24 differences between their respective records and thereafter, make the necessary adjustments to reconcile their books.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant to reconcile the variance between balances of the RPT/SET Receivable as against balances of Deferred RPT/SET accounts, thereafter, prepare the necessary journal entry vouchers to correct the account balances.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Acting GSO to submit to the Audit Team the duly approved Physical Inventory Plan (PIP), containing at least, the specific assignments/duties of the members, the cut-off date, and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targetter completion of the physical inventory;
b. Monitor compliance by the Inventory Committee, the Acting GSO and the Acting Municipal Accountant with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the procedural guidelines for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, including the reconciliation of the inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records; and
c. Require the Acting Municipal Accountant to identify the cause/s of the unreconciled amounts in the balances of the PPE accounts in the General ledger and Lapsing Schedule and effect the necessary adjustments in the recorded depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to reclassify the P6,867,826.10 semi-expandable items and effect the necessary adjustments in the books in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in reconciling the difference of P13,062,685.96 noted between the Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds accounts. Thereafter, prepare the necessary adjusting entries to balance the two accounts and ensure the fair presentation of the Municipality’s financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO)/Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) and Electronic Remittance File (ERF) Handler to make representation with the GSIS on the reconciliation and/or settlement of the unpaid premium remittance aggregating to P242,341.58 of the 99 concerned municipal employees. Then, require the HRMO, Acting Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the above subject accounts, and remit whatever balance due to the agencies concerned.

Also, we strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant to allocate enough time to verify the details of the negative or abnormal balance in the accounts and thereafter, make the necessary adjustments to present an accurate and reliable balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Treasurer and Acting Municipal Accountant remit to the BIR all taxes withheld within the prescribed period. Otherwise, any surcharges, interests and penalties that may be incurred shall be the personal liability of the municipal officials concerned who are responsible for the non-remittance of the taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the above subject accounts, and remit whatever balance due to the agencies concerned to avoid incurrence of penalties, surcharges and inconveniences to the employees concerned. Strictly remit in full the withheld premium contributions and loan amortizations to the respective government agencies in accordance with the cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to adopt a complete ATM payroll system for the Municipality to eliminate the risk of loss or misapplication of funds aside from promoting greater convenience, transparency, and accountability in consonance with the provisions of Section 2.1 of IRR of Executive Order No. 170, S. 2022, Section 124 of PD No. 1445 and DBM Circular No. 2008-8 dated October 23, 2008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Accountant and the BAC to submit the lacking documentary requirements relative to the procurement of land charged to 20 percent Development Fund amounting to P18,000,000.00 to establish the regularity, propriety and validity of the disbursements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GFPS to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related PPAs as planned, in order to attain the objective for which funds are provided for, in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2011-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to properly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursements of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund based on the LDRRMFIP;
b. Instruct the MDRRMO Head to justify the implementation of the programs, projects and activities charged against the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund amounting to P19,122.81, which were not included in the LDRRMFIP and are not in line with the purpose of the fund; and
c. Refrain from approving disbursements that are not among the PPAs chargeable to Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund to ensure proper utilization of the said fund and to avoid disallowance in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to draw the journal entry reflecting the net discrepancy of

immediately effect all identified reconciling items to come up with the accurate
and reliable amount presented in the year-end financial statements.

We reiterated partly our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-
Municipal Accountant to prepare a complete and accurate lapsing schedule for all

We strongly reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the OIC-Municipal

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
revert the payables without valid documentation and proper authority totaling

1445. Thereafter, draw the necessary journal entry voucher to revert these long-
outstanding obligations to the Government Equity account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant

9184 to prevent the accumulation of Guarantee/Security Deposits Payable in the
books of accounts of RPG.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
to:

P970,890.00 and P5,772,519.58, respectively;
b. ensure compliance with the PD No. 1445 and GAM for LGUs Volume III to avoid
similar errors in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to
ensure that the appropriate funds are allocated to the correct expense accounts during

account names, and OIC-Municipal Accountant to verify the appropriateness of the

Furthermore, ensure compliance with the PD No. 1445 and GAM for LGUs, Volume
III to avoid similar errors in the future.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:

b. Municipal Accountant to verify and evaluate the accuracy of the reconciling items reflected in the BRS and effect the necessary adjustment in the books to come up with the adjusted balance of the CIB-LCCA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify to the CIP account the cost incurred for infrastructure projects while still under construction by debiting CIP-Buildings and Other Structures in the amount of P88,590,040.53 and crediting Buildings, Hospital and Health Centers, and Other Structures in the amounts of P11,982,302.23, P7,476,457.64 and P69,131,280.66, respectively.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

b. Municipal Accountant to identify the causes of the unreconciled amounts per PPE accounts in the General ledger and Lapsing Schedule and thereafter effect the necessary adjustments in the recorded depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation; and
c. Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal General Service Officer (GSO) and the Property Officer to update the lapsing schedule, property cards and the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) once the relevant data are complete and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the reported PPE and related accumulated depreciation to fairly present the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the discrepancy between their records and make the necessary adjustments to come up with a reliable RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET account balances; and

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to properly record the transactions in the Municipality’s books of account in order to present fairly the balance of the affected accounts by debiting Due to GSIS in the amount of P11,208,348.86 and crediting CIB-LCCA by same amount.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct the required periodic reconciliation of their respective records, in order to identify/establish the details of the variance of P37,307,511.54, P57,320,685.58 and (P42,157.68), and thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments on their books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to reverse the entry made in the erroneous provision of depreciation for the on-going infrastructure projects by debiting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation of recorded assets and by crediting Prior Period Adjustment both accounts in amount of P3,767,865.32.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to undertake appropriate actions to determine the status of claims for all accounts payable in order to effect immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of their respective fund pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445, specifically, the automatic reversion of accounts payable without valid claims which remained unsettled for more than three years and recorded as payables since CY 2010, totaling P145,783,934.29.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to come up with the details of the P30,682,941.30 balance of the Guarantee/Security Deposits Payable account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the Disbursing Officers in compliance with Item No. 4.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to avoid the risk of loss of not being fully indemnified in case of defalcations, shortages, unrelieved losses.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to allocate enough time to verify the details of the negative or abnormal balance in the accounts and thereafter, make the necessary adjustments to present an accurate and reliable balances in the financial statements.

We strongly reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to reconcile the above subject accounts, and remit whatever balance due to the agencies concerned to avoid incurrence of penalties, surcharges and inconveniences to the employees concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt an intensified income collection campaign to attain targeted income;

c. Direct the concerned Municipal finance officials to exercise proper coordination and to strictly monitor the balance of allotment for obligation, in order to further prevent incurrence of obligation in excess of allotment and overdraft in disbursements against obligations incurred.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDC to:

b. Ensure that all priority development PPAs for inclusion in the 20 percent DF of the Municipality are properly planned and are procurement-implementation-ready.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GFPS to:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-MDRRMO to:

b. Submit justification on the implemented PPAs not included in the LDRRMFIP totaling P27,544,696.74 that were charged to the unexpended balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund of previous years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct that:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

 Effect the necessary adjustments in the recorded depreciation expense and

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting records with the

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require
the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra effort to identify

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal

We reiterated our recommendation with modifications that the Municipal Mayor

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, in

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Administrator to create an Inventory Committee to properly

b. General Services Officer to actively assist the members of the Inventory

c. Inventory Committee for the preparation of the New Physical Inventory
Plan and submit it to the Audit Team, in accordance with the COA Circular

smooth conduct of One-Time Cleansing of non-existing/missing PPE of the
Municipality; and

d. Municipal Accountant to draw necessary Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs)
to properly account for the results of the PPE One-Time Cleansing

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal mayor direct the:

a. General Services Officer to maintain Local Road Inventory, Road Map, and

2015-008;

b. Municipal Accountant to maintain and keep required records and reports

c. Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and General

d. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all local
road assets, prepare Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network
System and submit to the Audit Team immediately.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require immediate action from the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer on the net book reconciling items totaling P12,443,982.24 reflected in
the BRS to fully identify the nature of such items, and cause appropriate
adjustments so as to reflect correct and accurate CIB-LCCA, in adherence to the
provisions of Sections 111(2) and 74 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 3.2 and 3.3 of
COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Immediately adopt the perpetual inventory method by initially recording
all inventory purchases as assets, and recognizing expenses only upon
issuance or consumption; and
• Maintain and regularly update SLC for each inventory item to ensure
accurate tracking of inventory movements and reliable support for
inventory balances in the financial records.

b. General Services Officer to:
• Immediately implement measures to secure both the storage and delivery
areas by installing appropriate security features, such as durable locks,
physical barriers, and monitoring systems, to safeguard government
assets. Management should also regularly assess and monitor these
facilities to ensure continuous adherence to asset protection policies;
• Immediately secure the fidelity bond of the Supply Custodian in
compliance with Section 101(2) of PD No. 1445 and Treasury Circular No.
02-2009; and
• Ensure that the Supply Custodian formally reports to and functions under
the supervision of the designated GSO/Supply Officer.

c. Municipal Administrator to:
• Create an Inventory Committee, as authorized by the Local Chief
Executive, to properly oversee the physical inventory-taking and the
reconciliation of inventory records with accounting records to ensure the
accuracy, existence and completeness of properties;
• Issue appropriate office orders or memoranda clearly defining the roles,
responsibilities, and reporting arrangements between the Supply Officer
and the Supply Custodian; and
• Monitor the actions of all concerned departments to ensure that audit
recommendations are prioritized and implemented promptly, and
consistently remind concerned officials and employees of the laws, rules,
and regulations pertaining to the management of inventories.

d. Inventory Committee to:
• Conduct a complete and timely physical count of all inventories at the
end of every semester, and prepare and submit the corresponding Report
of Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI); and
• Ensure the timely notification and participation of a COA representative
during future physical inventory activities to enhance the credibility of
the inventory results and to comply with applicable rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant
to reconcile the corresponding advances accounts and draw a Journal Entry
Voucher (JEV) to record Advances to SDO for each CA granted to an SDO, as
required under Section 91 Volume I or the Recording Cash Advances for Special
Purpose/Time-bound Undertakings of the GAM for LGUs and thereafter ensure
that an Advance Account is properly set up in the General/Subsidiary Ledger
before recording any liquidation and thereafter ensure that an Advance Account is
properly set up in the General/Subsidiary Ledger before recording any
liquidation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
▪ Strictly enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to AOs at year-end and the
immediate refund of unspent amounts; and
▪ Send demand letters to all AOs with outstanding CAs and submit their
names to the Human Resource Management Office for appropriate action
including the immediate withholding of salaries and other unpaid claims
due them.

b. Municipal Treasurer to instruct all AOs with unliquidated CA to immediately
liquidate their accounts and for future grants to liquidate such CA at year-
end.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor implement a sound
internal control in accordance with the abovementioned laws, rules and
regulations and may adopt the following measures:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and Human Resource
Management Officer to work together with PVB – Marikina to ensure the swift
and seamless opening of payroll accounts for JO personnel; and

b. Require each JO personnel to open a payroll account with the PVB
immediately after assumption of office and before payment of first salary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit financial reports and
DVs together with the SDs within the reglementary period as prescribed in COA
Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2006-009 to the Audit Team to facilitate prompt
validation and review. Otherwise, appropriate administrative disciplinary action
must be initiated against the officials and employees who shall continuously fail to
comply with the requirements without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of
Suspension for the unsubmitted vouchers; and

b. Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to evaluate the current
manpower complement of respective Offices of the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer and consider requesting the creation and/or filling of
additional plantilla positions for said offices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

a. Closely monitor the timely renewal of fidelity bond of the accountable officers
in compliance with Item No. 4.10 of the same Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to
ensure that all accountable officers are covered by valid fidelity bond; and

b. Refrain from granting cash advance to disbursing officer until her fidelity bond
was renewed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to
undertake the proper disposition and/or procedures for one-time cleansing of
dormant Accounts Receivables in the amount of P21,940,921.13 before requesting
COA approval for the write-off/derecognition of these dormant accounts from the
books, pursuant to COA No. 2023-008.

We recommend the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare
and submit the Quarterly Schedules of Dormant Accounts in compliance with Item
No. 10.1 of Circular No. 2023-008 to ensure timely review and evaluation of the
dormant accounts and the immediate action, thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal the Disposal
Committee to:

a. Immediately convene and proceed with the appraisal and disposal process in
accordance with Revised Manual on Disposal of Government Properties to
prevent further deterioration; and

b. Ensure strict adherence to COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-01 or Revised
Manual on Disposal of Government Properties, to safeguard government
assets, promote operational efficiency, and prevent further loss in asset value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

a. MPDO to:
▪ Monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development
programs, projects and activities in accordance with AIP; and
▪ Assess and formulate budget appropriately in order to implement more
PPAs to ensure that the attainment of the desired socio-economic
development and its environmental outcomes.

b. Municipal Engineer to implement and manage development projects, ensuring
projects stay on track and are within planned timeline and within budget;

c. Municipal Budget Officer to enhance collaboration with the MPDO and
Municipal Engineer to ensure that the budget reflect realistic projections
based on the capacity of the Municipality;

d. MPDO, the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile
the amounts reported in the Statement of Appropriation, Obligations, and
Balances (SAOB) and Accomplishment Report and submit to the Audit Team
immediately; and

e. Municipal Administrator to constantly remind concerned officials and
employees on the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing
development projects under the 20 percent Development Funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant,
Municipal Treasurer and Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to
coordinate with each other and conduct a thorough reconciliation of balances,
ensuring the accuracy of billing statements, payroll deductions, and remittances,
while also facilitating the timely remittance of outstanding balances to prevent
penalties, surcharges, and inconveniences to officials and employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a. The GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to maximize the utilization of the GAD
funds through the implementation of GAD-related PAPs as planned, in order to
attain the objective for which funds are provided for, in compliance with PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2011-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
JMC No. 2016-01;

b. The Municipal Budget Officer to strengthen collaboration with the GAD Focal
Person to ensure that the budget reflects a more realistic and practical
allocation of resources, taking into account both financial constraints and
gender and development priorities; and

c. The Municipal Administrator to constantly remind concerned officials and
employees on the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing GAD
programs, projects and activities.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Acting
Municipal Mayor to strictly direct the:

a. The Inventory Committee to-
• Prepare, in coordination with General Services Office (GSO), Office of the
Municipal Accountant (OMA), and submit the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) for
approval of the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the conduct of inventory is properly
and adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;
• Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least ten (10) calendar days before
the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
• Use temporary control stickers in the conduct of physical inventory and attach the
same to the prominent and secured portion of the inventoried PPE for proper and
easy identification;
• Prepare the List of PPEs Found at Station to facilitate the recognition of PPE items
not recorded in the books to establish reasonable basis to consider the same as
property of the Municipality;
• Prepare the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs to facilitate the conduct of required
procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without established
accountability; and
• Strictly implement COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of inventory of PPE
for one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

b. The GSO to-
• Use pre-numbered property tags or control stickers to all PPE containing the
information required under Section 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and to
attach the same to the prominent and secured portions of the inventoried PPE for
proper and easy identification;
• Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with Property
Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs) as prescribed under Section 5.6 of COA
Circular No. 2020-006;
• Renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever there
is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability;
• Ensure that all PPE acquisitions and disposal are timely and completely recorded
in the property card;
• Ensure submission of RPCPPE on or before January 31 of the following year in
compliance with the prescribed guidelines; and
• Keep a complete and updated record of all PPE items.

c. The OMA to:
• Record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation with GSO to
establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts, with the
presence of valid supporting documents;
• Prepare and maintain PPE Ledger Card for each PPE items and continually
update the same to account for additions to and subtractions from PPE; and
• Strictly comply with COA Circular No. and 2020-006, and Section 491, GAAM,
Vol. I.

d. The OMA, GSO and IC to, henceforth, coordinate and conduct reconciliation of the
results of physical inventory and GSO records/RCPPE; identify and address the
nature and causes of variances; and hold responsible/ accountable the designated
supply officers/custodians pursuant to Section 491 of GAAM, Vol. I, and other
existing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. General Services Officer to submit regularly to the Municipal Accountant the
accomplished Monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued duly supported
with Requisition and Issue Slip for every issuance, to facilitate timely recording
of issuances in the books by the latter and to provide accurate data/information
for effective decision making.

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure proper and accurate computation of weighted
average cost by recording of all purchases and issuances of supplies and
materials on a real time basis for fair presentation of inventory accounts in the
financial statements; and

c. Conduct the physical inventory of supplies and materials in a manner that
promotes detection of possible issues such as identification of unrecorded
inventories found at station and losses during the physical count and
undertake proper reconciliation procedures between the Report on the
Physical Count of Inventories and accounting records to be able to support the
validity, existence, completeness and accuracy of the reported balance of
inventory accounts.

We reiterated our prior audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Cause the reversal of year-end accruals and prior years’ balances for which the

P18,198,627.99; and

b. Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of the
affected accounts for the fair presentation in the financial statements, copy
furnish the Office of the Auditor for evaluation;

• Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with PARs as prescribed

• Renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever

• Ensure that all PPE acquisitions and disposal are timely and completely

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to draw journal entry vouchers to adjust the staled
checks by debiting CIB-LCCA in the amount of P5,599,640.62 and crediting

b. Municipal Treasurer to send a written notice to the payees of the existence
of the check before the same turned stale and shall be reported and

(For unrecorded collections)

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

crediting appropriate Income account by same amount to reflect the
correct balances of said accounts as of December 31, 2024 and provide

the recognized income accounts are subject for reclassification after

and

b. Municipal Treasurer to establish clear and/or enhance the existing

control and monitor what kind of collections it pertains to properly utilize
the BRS. Also, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant of the monitoring

of accounts.

(For other book reconciling items)

We reiterated our audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor

a. Conduct thorough analysis and prepare journal entries for the identified

the recorded series of transactions that occurred to determine the root
cause and draw adjusting entries, if warranted, to correct the account

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

b. Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of the
affected accounts for the fair presentation in the financial statements, copy
furnish the Office of the Auditor for evaluation;

We reiterated our audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the

a. Inform the identified suppliers/contractors who have valid outstanding claims
against the Municipality to file their requests for refund particularly those

cause the release of their guaranty/security deposits; and
b. Revert to the unappropriated surplus those identified as long outstanding
wherein the obligation to pay had prescribed or could no longer be established
due to the lack of details or wherein the guaranty/security deposit had been
forfeited.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Submit the financial reports and other documents including the identified

009 and to ensure completeness of the submitted original DVs and their
