a. Inventory Committee to complete the procedures for One-time cleansing of
PPE of the Municipality in accordance with the COA Circular No. 2020-006;
b. Municipal Accountant to re-classify as semi-expendable property the PPE with
total carrying amount of P6,042,686.06 but with acquisition cost of less than
P50,000.00 per unit COA Circular No. 2022-004;
c. Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of inventory taking of all PPE
and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team in compliance with Section 124,
Chapter 7 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
d. Municipal Accountant to maintain records and reports for Local Road
Networks and disclose the total road network system in the Notes to the
Financial Statements incompliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare the SLs maintained for Accounts Payable to show the details of the
transactions;
b. Submit to the Audit Team accurate/reliable Aging of Accounts Payable;
c. Prepare Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust errors in recording transactions
totaling P54,682.99 by debiting Accounts Payable and crediting appropriate
liability accounts; and
d. Strictly adhere on the provision of Section 111 of PD No. 1445 on keeping the
accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to plan/strategize on how to conduct and
complete the physical inventory of PPE and prepare the PIP for submission to
COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of
inventory taking activities;
b. Require the OIC-GSO and the Municipal Accountant to investigate the cause of
the variance to update their respective records and submit the same to the
Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the PIP; and
c. Henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make the necessary journal entry to revert the said obligations without valid
claim to Government Equity by debiting Accounts Payable and crediting the
Prior Period Adjustment account so that the Municipal Government can use
the available funds for other priority projects that would uplift the living
condition of its constituents; and
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provision of PD No. 1177 on the proper
recognition of Accounts Payable.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to continue
the conduct of a thorough review and reconciliation of the reported
transactions in the Cash - Local Treasury account;
b. Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjustment/s in the books to
reflect the correct balance of the Cash- Local Treasury account; and
c. Henceforth, concerned municipal officials to strictly comply with Section
181(c) of GAAM, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the
unreleased checks totaling P80,839.28 to revert it back to the Cash in Bank-
LCCA account;
b. Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Accounting Office the
schedule of unreleased checks every end of the year to serve as basis in the
adjustment to revert the same to the Cash in Bank- LCCA account and set up
the liability account; and
c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No.
96-011.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make the necessary journal entry for the reclassification of the ongoing
projects by debiting Construction in Progress-Infrastructure Assets and
Construction in Progress-Building and Other Structures accounts amounting
to P4,868,708.82 and P63,935,421.92, respectively, and crediting Road
Networks of P2,188,357.31, Flood Control Systems of P2,680,351.31 and
Buildings of P63,935,421.92.
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the proper
recording of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Immediately submit the aforesaid paid disbursement vouchers together with
their supporting documents to the Auditor’s Office pursuant to the above-cited
regulations, for post-audit. Failure on the part of the concerned official to
submit the above-mentioned documents shall automatically cause the
suspension of payment of her salary until she has complied with the
requirements pursuant to the above-mentioned regulations; and
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provision of COA Circular No. 2009-006 on
the submission of financial transaction documents and reports of the
Municipality to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the DSWD IV-A Accounting Office to
facilitate the preparation and submission the liquidation reports of the fund
transfers from the said agency;
b. Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with Municipal Accountant to remit to the
SAs all the unused balance of funds pursuant to the cited regulations, so that
the amount could be immediately used for other projects/ programs of the
National Government and to relieve the Municipality from any
accountability/liability for said unused funds; and
c. Henceforth, observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 in
implementing projects funded by Source Agencies to ensure that funds are
utilized and liquidated on time and the delivery of services and benefits to the
public will be facilitated.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents to
substantiate the disbursements made and to facilitate post audit of the same.
Otherwise, the transactions shall be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Engineering Office and all other offices of the Municipal
Government to formulate their respective PPMPs for their different PPAs and
submit the same to the Municipal Budget Office and forwarded to the BAC
Secretariat for consolidation into an APP; and
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 7.3.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA
No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal HRMO to:
a. Assess the existing organization structure and staffing pattern of the
Municipal Government so that the ideal number of plantilla items/positions
could be established and develop a written policy guideline on the hiring of
individuals under JO, clearly defining the conditions whereby employment of
such JO workers may be availed of;
b. Consider those qualified JO workers for appointment to vacant regular
plantilla positions in the Municipal Government; and
c. Henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 76 and 77 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the OIC-General Services Officer (GSO) and the Municipal Accountant
to conduct regular reconciliation of their respective records/reports to achieve
an accurate balance of the account as reported in the Financial Statements
and Property Records and prepare all the necessary activities, documents and
reports in order to take advantage of the opportunity for the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balance as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain and update the PPELCs, and if
the GAAMS cannot provide, prepare PPELC manually; and OIC-GSO to issue
PAR for all PPE items to the concerned accountable officers and maintain and
update the related Property Cards (PCs) as necessary pursuant to the
provisions of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1;
c. Create a Property Unit under the GSO or designate a Property Officer with a
plantilla position, which shall be supervised by the General Services Officer, to
focus on property management;
d. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and OIC-
General Services Officer with complete description and cost segregation of
road components for road projects as basis in the preparation of
records/reports and disclosure of total road network system in the Notes to
Financial Statement pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008;
e. direct the Municipal Accountant to re-classify as semi-expendable property the
PPE with acquisition cost of less than P50,000.00 per unit in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2022-004; and
f. Instruct the OIC-General Services Officer to prepare and submit to the Audit
Team the IIRUP and WMR, whichever is applicable, and the Municipal
Appraisal and Disposal Committee to cause the disposal of the identified
unserviceable properties to prevent its further deterioration through proper
disposal procedures and applicable mode of disposition as required by the
COA and DBM JC No. 2024-01 dated January 30, 2024.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Review and analyze all payables that remained outstanding at year-end,
including the beginning balance totaling P30,672,737.13, by gathering the
necessary documents to substantiate the existence of valid claims, in
compliance with Section 46 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1177;
b. If warranted, seek authority, from the Sangguniang Bayan, to revert to the
unappropriated surplus the payable accounts having no valid claims which
were outstanding for more than two years and hereinafter, observe the
prescribed period for the retention of Accounts Payable in the books of
accounts in accordance with Section 98 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445;
c. Make necessary adjusting journal entry reversing liabilities which are not
legitimate obligations of the Municipality; and
d. Exert due care in the recording of transactions with complete documentation
and establish the accurate amount of the Accounts Payable to achieve fair
presentation of that account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require immediate action from the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer on the net book reconciling
items totaling P18,155,557.59 reflected in the BRS to fully identify the nature of
such items, and cause appropriate adjustments so as to reflect correct and
accurate CIB-LCCA, in adherence to the provisions of Sections 3.2 and 3.3 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS I.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Re-evaluate the necessity for the granting of PCF to the 33 PCF Custodians to
establish adequate control over the fund;
b. Impose that PCF shall be strictly for petty operating expenses to cover
recurring expenses for one month duly supported by an estimate of costs in
accordance with COA Circulars No. 97-002 and 2023-004; and
c. Assess whether there is a need to reduce the said fund based on historical
and/or projected monthly expenses of the Municipality and return the excess
funds to the General Fund, if necessary, to mitigate the risk of loss or misuse
and unnecessary tying up of funds that could have been used for other
purposes.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to evaluate the existing Contract to Sell
(CTS) issued by the Municipality thru the former administration for amendment or
cancellation in view of the beneficiaries’ failure to settle their arrears subject to
approval of the Sangguniang Bayan, if warranted. Otherwise, instruct the
Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the OIC-Housing and People’s
Development Office (HPDO) to enforce the collection of the amounts due from the
beneficiaries, including the down payment and arrears, in compliance with the
binding CTS entered into by the Municipality.

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer investigate the noted discrepancy of P362,000.00,
reconcile their respective records and thereafter, monitor the collections therein,
in compliance with Section 2 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the concerned Offices to send representative to public procurement
training program for capacity building on the preparation of PPMPs;
b. Instruct all implementing units to prepare and submit, on a timely manner,
their respective PPMPs prior to the preparation of the consolidated APP in the
prescribed format of the GPPB, which include, but not limited to, supplies,
materials, equipment and infrastructure projects to be implemented during the
current year/succeeding years, and submit to the Municipal Budget Office for
evaluation;
c. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to furnish a copy of the procuring entity’s
budget proposal, as well as the corresponding PPMPs of all implementing units
or end-user Offices, to the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat for
its review and consolidation into the proposed APP, consistent with the
procuring entity’s budget proposal, pursuant to Section 7.3.4 of the same IRR;
and
d. Direct the BAC, thru its BAC Secretariat, to finalize the modes of procurement
under the proposed APP, in accordance with Section 7 of the 2016 Revised IRR
of RA No. 9184.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require immediate action from its
Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) to make representation with the GSIS on the
updating and/or settlement of the delinquent loan accounts of 46 municipal
employees totaling P1,661,536.56 in accordance with the IRR of RA No. 8291, to
avoid further accumulation of penalties and surcharges by the concerned
municipal employees and the unnecessary tying up of funds of the GSIS.

We recommended Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer to prepare and submit to the MDRRMC the
proposed LDRRMFIP incorporating the programs and projects to be charged to
the Special Trust Fund (STF) in accordance with Section 4.4 of COA Circular No.
2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-MDRRMO, in
coordination with other concerned offices, to ensure full utilization of the MDRRM
Fund by implementing the identified PPAs in compliance with Republic Act (RA)
No. 10121 that is aimed to strengthen the disaster resilience of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the General Services Officer in coordination with the Municipal
Accountant to list all insurable properties in the PIF with the latest appraised
values/valuation, appraisal date and other information as required under COA
Circular No. 2018-002 and submit the PIF to the Supervising Auditor/Audit
Team Leader and the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year;
b. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to prioritize the inclusion in the
Municipality’s annual budget the amount of premiums for the general
insurance covering all insurable properties;
c. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the payment of the insurance
premiums to the GSIS; and
d. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 656 as amended by
Presidential Decree (PD) No. 245 and COA Circular No. 2018-002, to protect the
interest of the Municipality and be indemnified in the event of damage or loss
due to casualty or fortuitous events.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the payroll system
to eliminate the risk of loss or misapplication of funds aside from promoting
greater convenience, transparency, and accountability in consonance with the
provisions of Section 124 of PD No. 1445, DBM Circular No. 2008-08 and the
IRR of EO No. 170, S. 2022; and
b. Municipal HRMO to formulate policy requiring each JO personnel to open a
payroll account with the servicing bank immediately upon assumption of
office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation
of GAD-related PAPs as planned, in order to attain the objective for which funds
are provided for, in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2011-01, as
amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

a. The IC to-
• Prepare, in coordination with General Services Office (GSO), Office of the
Municipal Accountant (OMA), and submit the PIP for approval of the
Municipal Mayor to ensure that the conduct of inventory is properly and
adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;
• Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least 10 calendar days before
the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
• Use temporary control stickers in the conduct of physical inventory and
attach the same to the prominent and secured portion of the inventoried PPE
for proper and easy identification;
• Prepare the List of PPEs Found at Station to facilitate the recognition of PPE
items not recorded in the books to establish reasonable basis to consider
the same as property of the Municipality;
• Prepare the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs to facilitate the conduct of
required procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs
without established accountability; and
• Strictly implement COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of inventory of
PPE for one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

b. The GSO to-
• Use pre-numbered property tags or control stickers to all PPE containing
the information required under Section 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and
to attach the same to the prominent and secured portions of the inventoried
PPE for proper and easy identification;
• Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with PARs as prescribed
under Section 5.6 of COA Circular No. 2020-006;
• Renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever
there is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability.

c. The OMA to record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation with
GSO to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts, with
the presence of valid supporting documents; and

d. The OMA, GSO and IC to, henceforth, coordinate and conduct the necessary
procedures on the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE account balances
of government agencies to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to
existence, condition and accountability with reference to COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to
gather documents and conduct thorough analysis of reconciling items totaling
P163,607.75 reflected in the bank reconciliation, and thereafter, prepare journal
entries for the identified valid book reconciling items which should be done before
closing the books to ensure the accuracy of the cash at the end of each reporting
period and copy furnish the Audit Team of the drawn journal entries for validation
and evaluation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Cause the reversal of year-end accruals and prior years’ balances for
which the Municipality has no valid obligations as of December 31, 2023
totaling P42,250,959.63;
• Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of
the affected accounts for the fair presentation in the financial statements,
copy furnish the Office of the Auditor for evaluation;
• Strictly observe the application of accrual basis and monitor all the
obligations due the suppliers/creditors by merely recording all valid claims
or liabilities in the books of accounts in order to avoid/prevent conflict
claims to be filed by the latter; and strict compliance with the accounting
standard to ensure accrual of the liabilities to fairly present the account
balance of the affected account at the end of the reporting period; and

b. General Services Officer to copy furnish the Municipal Accountant regularly
with the accomplished Acceptance and Inspection Report duly supported with
purchase order/contract and delivery receipt every after completed delivery of
procured items to facilitate timely recording by the latter in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prepare an updated and detailed schedule of the guaranty deposits payable
account for monitoring;

b. Inform the identified suppliers/contractors who have valid outstanding claims
against the MGSM to file their requests for refund and thereafter cause the
release of their guaranty/security deposits; and

c. Revert to the unappropriated surplus those identified as long outstanding
wherein the obligation to pay had prescribed or could no longer be established
due to the lack of details or wherein the guaranty/security deposit had been
forfeited.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Strictly enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to AOs within the
reglementary period and the immediate refund of unspent amounts;
• Refrain from granting new/additional CAs to AOs with outstanding CAs;
• Continue sending demand letters to all AOs with outstanding CAs and
submit their names to the Municipal Legal Office for appropriate action
including the immediate withholding of salaries and other unpaid claims
due them; and

b. Municipal Administrator to put in place an effective mechanism to constantly
remind all AOs of the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing the
handling of cash advance every time they are granted with the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that cash advance is granted to bonded SDO only to avoid the risk of loss
of not being fully indemnified in case of defalcations, shortages, unrelieved losses.

We recommended that the Municipality continue to further strengthen its effort and
initiative in maximizing the utilization of National Government support funds to
harmonize and aid in the delivery of basic services to the public.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Chairman/Secretariat to
furnish the Auditor with copies of approved contracts/POs together with the
complete supporting documents within five working days from the execution or
issuance in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated
February 12, 2009.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the HRMO to:

a. Adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and
other payroll deductions such that monthly net take-home pay of any employee
does not fall below the prescribed ceiling of P5,000.00;

b. Direct those in charge of payroll preparation to exercise diligence in
monitoring net take home pay of employees to comply with the requirements
of existing law. Remedial measures should be done to rectify the situation of
the 15 employees receiving below the required net take home pay which
includes not allowing them to apply for the new loan unless their net take-home
pay will not fall below P5,000.00; and

c. Strictly implement Section 56 of the General Provisions of RA No. 11936 on the
mandatory monthly net take home pay of P5,000.00.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Submit the lacking documents required for payments of salaries/wages of JO
workers totaling P155,433,791.34 to avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit;
• Ensure that all CAs for the salaries/wages of JO workers and the
corresponding liquidation reports are supported with complete documentation
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023 before giving
certifications on the disbursements and recording as fully liquidated,
respectively; and
• Ensure that disbursement vouchers and liquidation reports submitted to COA
are coupled with complete supporting documents.

b. HRMO to establish clear guidelines providing complete list of documents
required from JO workers for the processing of their claims pursuant to the
documentary requirements in COA Circular No. 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023
including the deadline of submission for the timely preparation of payroll and
accurate computations thereof.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Office (MPDO) to:

a. Monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development
programs. Projects and activities in accordance with Annual Investment
Program (AIP); and

b. Assess and update AIP to ensure that only those considered crucial to the
efficient discharge of government functions for the attainment of the desired
socio-economic development and its environmental outcomes are included in
the AIP.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor through GFPS to:

a. Maximize the use of GAD Fund and implement the planned PPAs to fully
support the goal of achieving women’s empowerment and gender equality and
instituting an enabling environment at the local level that ensures gender-
responsive governance; and

b. Propose a training program on the HGDG in the GPB for the incoming year and
seek the assistance of a gender analysis specialist/expert in the use and/or
administration of the HGDG.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor through GFPS to:

a. Strictly monitor the implementation of PPAs in the GPB in terms of its success,
to further review the relevance of planned PPAs and to ensure that all
implemented PPAs reflected in the GAD AR were incorporated in the approved
GPB for effective planning and gender-responsive; and

b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the guidelines set forth in the PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the BAC and MDRRMO to:

a. Maximize the use of LDRRMF of the MGSM and fully implement the planned
PPAs to attain the MGSM’s preparedness, response, and recovery from the
impacts of natural and man-made disasters efficiently and effectively; and

b. Review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan indicating the PPAs
to be charged against the LDRRMF special trust fund pursuant to section 5.1.15
of the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2002.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the GSO to:

a. Ensure that all insurable properties of the Municipality are insured and likewise
make the necessary steps for the proper implementation of the Section 5 of RA
No. 656; and

b. Prepare the PIF based on the RPCPPE prepared in accordance with the
provisions of the COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Review the amount appearing as unremitted and cause the immediate
remittance to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth;
• Ensure that the remittances correspond to the exact amount withheld;
• Remit the withheld amounts of mandatory counterpart and premium
contributions of the MGSM, as well as loan repayments, within the
prescribed period to avoid penalties and to guarantee that Municipal
personnel/employees enjoy the full benefits from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and
PhilHealth; and
• Strictly comply with Section 6(b) of RA No. 8291 or the GSIS Act, Rule VII
of the IRR of RA 9679 or the HDMF Law or Pag-IBIG Fund, and Sections 19
(b) and 183 under Title III, Rule III, Revised IRR of the National Health
Insurance Act of 1995, together with the guidelines and instructions set
forth therein to avoid payment of unnecessary penalties on the unremitted
amounts.

b. Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to:
• Instruct the AAO to update immediately data/information of new
recruits/newly promoted personnel so that withheld deductions shall be
duly accepted/billed by the systems of GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth; and
• Issue a memorandum to concerned employees to immediately
update/report changes regarding member’s information with government
agencies to avoid delays in the remittance or non-remittance of
contributions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Require the Acting Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to verify the
composition of the Due to GSIS account and immediately remit to the GSIS the
amount due for loan repayments/amortizations; and

b. Make an appropriate action to reconcile the 50 delinquent loan accounts of 29
Municipal employees totaling P2,628,609.04 with the GSIS in accordance with the
Revised IRR of RA No. 8291, to avoid further possible accumulation of penalties and
surcharges.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant:

a. To immediately remit to the BIR the withheld taxes amounting to P146,998.51 and
remit succeeding withheld taxes within 10 days following the month in which
the withholding was made to avoid unnecessary payment of penalties and/or
surcharges by the withholding officer of the Municipality; and

b. Strictly comply with Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated March
20, 2007 and its guidelines and instructions set forth to avoid payment of
interest and penalty on the unremitted amounts.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Conduct thorough analysis and prepare journal entries for the identified valid
book reconciling items reflected in the bank reconciliation and the same
should be done before closing the books to ensure the accuracy of the cash at
the end of each reporting period and copy furnish this Office for the drawn
journal entries for validation and evaluation; and

b. Gather documents for analysis and reconciliation and to trace the accounting
entries made in previous years starting from the point the subsidiary ledgers
on the PNB account balance has become abnormal and the recorded series of
transactions that occurred to determine the root cause and draw adjusting
entries, if warranted, to correct the account balance.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

a. IC to:
• Prepare, in coordination with GSO, OMA, and submit the PIP for approval of
the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the conduct of inventory is properly and
adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;
• Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least ten (10) calendar days
before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities;
• Use temporary control stickers in the conduct of physical inventory and
attach the same to the prominent and secured portion of the inventoried PPE
for proper and easy identification;
• Prepare the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs to facilitate the conduct of
required procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs
without established accountability; and
• Strictly implement COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of inventory of
PPE for one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

b. GSO to:
• Use pre-numbered property tags or control stickers to all PPE containing
the information required under Section 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and
to attach the same to the prominent and secured portions of the inventoried
PPE for proper and easy identification;
• Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with PARs as prescribed
under Section 5.6 of COA Circular No. 2020-006;
• Renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever
there is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability.

c. OMA to record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation with GSO
to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts, with the
presence of valid supporting documents; and

d. OMA, GSO and IC to, henceforth, coordinate and conduct the necessary
procedures on the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE account balances
of government agencies to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to
existence, condition and accountability with reference to COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of
the affected accounts for the fair presentation in the financial statements,
copy furnish the Office of the Auditor for evaluation;
• Strictly observe the application of accrual basis and monitor all the
obligations due the suppliers/creditors by merely recording all valid claims
or liabilities in the books of accounts in order to avoid/prevent conflict
claims to be filed by the latter; and strict compliance with the accounting
standard to ensure accrual of the liabilities to fairly present the account
balance of the affected account at the end of the reporting period; and

b. General Services Officer to copy furnish the Municipal Accountant regularly
with the accomplished Acceptance and Inspection Report duly supported with
PO/contract and delivery receipt every after completed delivery of procured
items to facilitate timely recording by the latter in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prepare an updated and detailed schedule of the guaranty deposits payable
account for monitoring;

b. Inform the identified suppliers/contractors who have valid outstanding claims
against the MGT to file their requests for refund and thereafter cause the
release of their guaranty/security deposits; and

c. Revert to the unappropriated surplus those identified as long outstanding
wherein the obligation to pay had prescribed or could no longer be established
due to the lack of details or wherein the guaranty/security deposit had been
forfeited.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
strictly comply with the guidelines provided in the RA No. 11032 and its IRR and the
corresponding MOA entered into between by the MGT and BFP to remit
immediately within two working days after the transaction is made in order to
refrain from precluding both contracting parties to act promptly and take necessary
action on any discrepancy noted in the report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee
Chairman/Secretariat to furnish the Auditor with copies of approved contracts/POs
together with the complete supporting documents within five working days from the
execution or issuance in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-
001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the HRMO to:

a. Conduct a thorough assessment and evaluation of existing manpower
complement to re-assign JO workers whose functions are form part of the job
description of the agency's existing regular employees to designation or work
appropriate for JO workers as defined in COA-DBM JC No. 2, s. 2020; or remove
subject JO workers if determined unnecessary and excessive to avoid
inefficient bureaucracy and wasteful government expenditure; and

b. Fill-up vacant plantilla positions to minimize the hiring of JO workers.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the GSO to:

a. Ensure that all insurable properties of the Municipality are insured and likewise
make the necessary steps for the proper implementation of the Section 5 of RA
No. 656; and

b. Prepare the PIF based on the RPCPPE prepared in accordance with the
provisions of the COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the BAC and MDRRMO to:

a. Maximize the use of LDRRMF of the MGT and fully implement the planned PPAs
to attain the MGT’s preparedness, response, and recovery from the impacts of
natural and man-made disasters efficiently and effectively; and

b. Review and update annually the LDRRMFIP indicating the PPAs to be charged
against the LDRRMF special trust fund pursuant to section 5.1.15 of the COA
Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2002.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
• Review the amount appearing as unremitted and cause the immediate
remittance to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth;
• Ensure that the remittances correspond to the exact amount withheld;
• Remit the withheld amounts of mandatory counterpart and premium
contributions of the MGT, as well as loan repayments, within the
prescribed period to avoid penalties and to guarantee that Municipal
personnel/employees enjoy the full benefits from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and
PhilHealth; and
• Strictly comply with Section 6(b) of RA No. 8291 or the GSIS Act, Rule VII
of the IRR of RA 9679 or the HDMF Law or Pag-IBIG Fund, and Sections 19
(b) and 183 under Title III, Rule III, Revised IRR of the National Health
Insurance Act of 1995, together with the guidelines and instructions set
forth therein to avoid payment of unnecessary penalties on the unremitted
amounts.

b. HRMO to:
• Instruct the AAO to update immediately data/information of new
recruits/newly promoted personnel so that withheld deductions shall be
duly accepted/billed by the systems of GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth; and
• Issue a memorandum to concerned employees to immediately
update/report changes regarding member’s information with government
agencies to avoid delays in the remittance or non-remittance of
contributions.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Immediately remit to the Bureau of Internal Revenue the withheld taxes amounting to
P532,815.05 and remit succeeding withheld taxes within ten (10) days following the
month in which the withholding was made to avoid unnecessary payment of penalties
and/or surcharges by the withholding officer of the Municipality; and

b. Strictly comply with Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated March 20,
2007 and its guidelines and instructions set forth to avoid payment of interest and
penalty on the unremitted amounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, at the earliest and practicable period,
initiate the one-time cleansing of PPE balances of the Municipality by following
42
the guidelines and procedures on the six activities prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2020-006, to eliminate the noted discrepancy amounting to P3,538,718,381.98
as of December 31, 2023 to come up with reliable PPE balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the recorded PPE with acquisition
cost below the capitalization threshold of P50,000.00 by debiting Prior Period
Adjustment and corresponding Accumulated Depreciation of PPE accounts in the
amounts of P11,389,508.82 and P69,652,363.78, respectively, and crediting the
corresponding PPE accounts by P81,041,872.60 in consonance with Sections 4.1
44
and 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 and Sections 3.6 and 3.7 of COA Circular No.
2024-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit immediately the
BRS for Bank Account Number 3112-1000-90 under General Fund to
determine reconciling items for adjustment in the books of accounts; make
personal representation with the LBP to obtain the pertinent documents for
debit memoranda as basis for recording the same; and exert diligent effort to
support and locate the documents for other reconciling items from prior
years; and
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer of its timely submission of necessary
supporting documents as basis in the recording of reconciling items in the
books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Treasurer to maintain a separate Trust Fund for 15 percent
share of the OBO from the collection of building permit fees and other
charges; and
b. Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting entries to transfer the 15 percent
share of the OBO amounting P534,664.37 under the Due to NGAs under
General Fund to Trust Liabilities account under the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to submit justification for the non-
utilization of fund transfer amounting to P2,970,000.00, otherwise, direct the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with each other to
facilitate the immediate reversion of said fund due to the expiration of the term of
the intended project to the DOH-CHD CALABARZON and due to unjustified
reasons in consonance with the entered MOA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Demand the liquidation of outstanding cash advances totaling P55,841,828.83
and file appropriate charges against the accountable officers if they refused to
settle their cash advances; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the guidelines on the
granting and liquidation of cash advance pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002
dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
refrain from processing additional cash advances to any official and employee
without liquidating first their prior cash advances in consonance with Section 89
of PD No. 1445 and Item 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10,
1997.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately request and secure authority from the SB to cause immediate
reversion of the Accounts Payable totaling P27,185,125.92 to the unappropriated
surplus of the General Fund so that the Municipality can use the available funds
for other priority projects to provide better service to its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Immediately submit the aforesaid paid DVs, JEVs and their supporting
documents to the Auditor’s Office pursuant to the above-cited regulations, for
post-audit, to avoid suspension of the subject transactions. Failure on the part
of the concerned official to submit the above-mentioned documents shall
automatically cause the suspension of payment of his salary until he has
complied with the requirements pursuant to the above-mentioned regulations;
and
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provision of COA Circular No. 2009-006
dated September 15, 2009 on the submission of financial transaction
documents and reports of the Municipality to the Audit Team.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the OIC-Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct
regular reconciliation of their respective records/reports to achieve an
accurate balance of the account as reported in the Financial Statements and
Property Records and prepare all the necessary activities, documents and
reports in order to take advantage of the opportunity for the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balance as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020;

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain and update the PPE Ledger
Cards (PPELCs) for the unrecorded PPE; and OIC-Supply Officer to issue PAR
for all PPE items to the concerned accountable officers and maintain and
update the related Property Cards (PCs) as necessary;

c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain and keep required records and
reports for Local Road Networks and disclose the total road network system in
the Notes to the Financial Statements as required and the OIC-Supply Officer
to render a Report on Local Road Network, road map and keep a complete
Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components;

d. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to assist the Municipal Accountant and OIC-
Supply Officer with data needed such as complete description and cost
segregation of road components for road projects and assists and provide the
latter all information/data in preparing the records/reports as required;

e. Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all
local road assets, prepare Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network
System and submit to the Audit Team immediately;

f. Create a Municipal Disposal Committee in accordance with COA and DBM JC
No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024;

g. Instruct the OIC-Supply Officer to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the
IIRUP and WMR, whichever is applicable, and the Municipal Disposal
Committee to cause the disposal of the identified unserviceable properties to
prevent its further deterioration through proper disposal procedures and
applicable mode of disposition as required by the COA and DBM JC No. 2024-
01 dated January 30, 2024; and henceforth;

h. Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant, nonetheless, issue the corresponding
demand letter or billing statement to enforce collection thereon and to exhaust all
possible means to enforce payment by the debtors, and for documentation of the
succeeding actions to be taken. If after all remedies have been exhausted and
collection still fail, the Management may opt to avail writing-off of the account subject to
the provisions stated in Annex 8 of COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023,
if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Government Department Head (MGDH) - Municipal Human Resource
Management Office to:

a. Request a copy of billing statements or its equivalent records from the respective
agencies to facilitate the reconciliation of account balances;
b. Trace to prior years’ accounting entries to ascertain the cause of negative
balances in the Due to Pag-IBIG and GSIS accounts; and

c. Review and analyze records pertaining to the unreconciled balances in the
Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) of the Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth accounts
and immediately adjust the unreconciled and abnormal account balances to
correct and update SL balances in accordance with the provisions in Sections
123 and 124 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the
Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the implementation of the identified
priority development projects to achieve its full implementation based on the
timeline set; and

b. direct the concerned officials to ensure that the projects under the 20 percent
Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation
ready in accordance with Item No. 3.2.3 of the Joint Memorandum Circular No.
1 dated November 4, 2020 to assure strict compliance on the proper utilization
of the fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to:

a. Process the release of the guarantee deposits in a total amount of
P1,295,223.39 subject to the attested Certification of the Procurement Office that the
delivered goods were free from manufacturing defects; and

b. Process the release of the guarantee deposits after the lapse of the warranty
period and within reasonable time allowed under Section 62.1 of the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to present fairly the liability account in the
financial statements as of a given period.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require immediate action from its
Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) to make representation with the GSIS on the
updating and/or settlement of the delinquent loan accounts of 29 municipal
employees totaling P1,547,056.17 in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No.
8291, to avoid further accumulation of penalties and surcharges by the concerned
municipal employees and the unnecessary tying up of funds of the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MGDH-HRMO to:

a. Adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and other
payroll deductions such that monthly net take-home pay of any employee does not
fall below the prescribed ceiling of P5,000.00;

b. Direct those in charge of payroll preparation to exercise diligence in monitoring net
take home pay of employees to comply with the requirements of existing law.
Remedial measures should be done to rectify the situation of the 15 employees
receiving below the required net take home pay which includes not allowing them to
apply for the new loan unless their net take-home pay will not fall below P5,000.00;
and

c. Stop the current practice and strictly implement Section 56 of the General
Provisions of RA No. 11936 on the mandatory monthly net take home pay of
P5,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation
of GAD-related PAPs as planned, in order to attain the objective for which funds are
provided for, in compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2011-01, as
amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the:
Inventory Committee to –
Prepare the PIP, and submit the same to the Municipal Mayor for approval to ensure that the conduct of inventory-taking is properly and adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;
Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities;
Prepare a detailed and properly-filled consolidated RPCPPE and submit to the Municipal General Service Officer (MGSO) and Municipal Accountant for reconciliation of PPE items with their corresponding records and investigate discrepancies thereof; and
Strictly conduct the inventory taking in accordance with the procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006.
Municipal Treasurer as the Designated MGSO to –
Request the disposal of unserviceable properties by filling-up of the IIRUP and proceed with disposal procedures either thru sale, donation, or destruction following Section 379 of RA 7160 and the Manual on the Disposal of Government Property per COA Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Joint Circular 2024-01.
Submit to the Municipal Accountant the duly approved IIRUP and disposal documents for recording in the books of accounts, after the completion of the disposal process.
Municipal Accountant to record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts, supported with appropriate documents.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the following:
Municipal Accountant
To adjust the entries made by reversing the previously recorded accounts. The following entries given hereunder:
Accounts Payable 			2,493,438.87
Other Payables	                          164,584.00
Prior Period Errors				2,658,022.87
Prior Period Errors			2,658,022.87
Government Equity				2,658,022.87
Strictly follow the accrual basis of accounting in recording transactions and ensure that only those obligated covered by Obligation Request /CAFOA but delivered items and services rendered are recognize and recorded as assets/expense and payable as at year-end. For obligated and undelivered items, disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements shall be made.
In coordination with the Municipal Treasurer/MGSO to review the transactions and determine the payables without purchase orders and deliveries to date and prepare a certification thereof.
Municipal Budget Officer to:
Refrain from obligating transactions on the basis only of PRs and ensure that Obligation Requests/CAFOA are supported by POs and/or contracts.
Upon receipt of the certifications from the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer/MGSO, to revert to the general fund (GF) the accounts payable and other payables.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Treasurer/MGSO to timely submit the SSMI together with the RIS, and other documents to the Municipal Accountant to record the issuances in the books;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen monitoring procedures on outstanding inventories and regularly communicate with the Municipal Treasurer to submit SSMI supported with proper documents to timely record the issuances in the books; and
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories semestrally and submit the RPCI to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer for reconciliation in their records, copy furnish COA.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to: (a) analyze the unreconciled balances of the Due from and Due to Other Funds account, identify the reconciling items and prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry to reflect the correct account balances in the Statement of Financial Position; and (b) strictly monitor the reciprocal accounts by conducting regular reconciliation thereof, to eliminate and avoid future discrepancies, if any.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to require:
The Municipal Accountant to:
Apply the criteria on capitalization of PPE as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2022-004 to ensure proper classification of assets in the books.
Draw a JEV to effect necessary adjustments in the books of accounts in CY 2024 as follows:
Debit: Accumulated Depreciation	 P8,812,622.52
Prior Period Adjustment 		 P1,893,054.58
Credit:      Property, Plant and Equipment 	P10,707,907.10
The Municipal Treasurer to:
Provide data to the Municipal Accountant relevant to items reclassified as semi-expendable property including information of its condition;
Update existing PPE and Inventory records and prepare and maintain the additional necessary forms/reports/registries for purposes of record-keeping, and monitoring over issued semi-expendable properties provided in Section 4 of the Circular;
Cancel the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) of the semi-expendable property and issue ICS to the actual custodian of the items; and
Moving forward, issue ICS to actual custodians of items to establish monitoring, control and accountability over them.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recalculate the accumulated depreciation, disregarding the residual value on its computation as outlined in item no. 14 of COA Circular No. 2015-008, in order to derive the true value of annual depreciation related to the components of Local Road Networks.
Adjust the affected accounts related the above-mentioned understatement, we suggest the following adjusting entries:
Dr: Government Equity			P 113,880.72
Cr: Accumulated Depreciation-Road Networks 	P 113,880.72

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct:
The Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV supported by appropriate documents to record and adjust the reconciling items in the books and ensure that reconciling items are timely adjusted to generate accurate financial reporting as of a given period.
The Municipal Treasurer to:
Send written notices to the payees to confirm the existence of the stale checks and advise the payees to submit the stale check for replacement following the procedure prescribed in Section 59 of Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs Volume I; and
Regularly monitor unclaimed checks and practice the sending of written notice to the payee/s at least one month before the check becomes stale.
Regularly submit a List of Stale Checks to the Municipal Accountant for proper action in the books of accounts.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct:
The BAC to:
Stop preparing a consolidated PR, as well as the preparation of POs, AIR, CAFOA and DVs.
Stop preparing two (2) different ITBs, instead adopt a strategy to ensure that similar items/nature of goods to be procured be consolidated in the ITB with reference to approve PRs or may opt to form these PRs into Lots in the ITB.
Strictly conduct the procurement of goods in accordance with the RIRR of RA 9184.
The MGSO, BAC and other department heads to come up with an efficient strategy in preparing and processing of PRs for the procurement of goods in accordance with RIRR of RA 9184 while ensuring that goods of similar nature or items and needed at about the same time are procured thru public bidding save for any exceptions.

Recommendations
We recommended, and the management agreed to direct the BAC to:
Implement a rigorous document verification process during post-qualification to validate the authenticity, veracity, and validity of all submitted documents. This process should be conducted using non-discretionary criteria, as outlined in the bidding documents, to ensure fairness and transparency.
Ensure prompt and written notification to the concerned bidder, detailing the grounds for disqualification upon determination of a bidder's failure to meet the post-qualification criteria. This notification should be issued in accordance with Section 34.5 of the RIRR to uphold transparency and accountability.
Refrain from recommending to the Head of Procuring Entity for the award of a contract when there has been no proper conduct of the post-qualification process during the procurement of goods. This ensures a fair and competitive bidding process among all other bidders and adherence to the non-negotiable bidding requirements.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the:
Municipal Engineer to immediately conduct regular review and evaluation of unimplemented projects and expedite the preparation of necessary documents to facilitate the request for procurement and implementation of these projects.
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to re-assess the projects funded under the 20 per cent current appropriations as to viability, feasibility, and relevance, and endorse the results thereof to the Municipal Development Council for appropriate action.
Municipal Agriculturist to ensure that for programs and projects that falls under the agricultural development has to be implementable ready in order to optimize the benefits that can be obtained therefrom.
Ensure that identification of projects to be funded out of the 20 per cent DF are well planned, and procurement-and-implementation ready to avoid implementation issues such as lot/area for implementation

Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to transfer the unexpended current balance of the LDDRMF in the GF to TF as at year-end and not merely as set-up of obligation, moving forward.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Require the business owners to submit a sworn statement of gross sales/receipts/Audited Financial Statements if applicable, and assessment should be based thereon.
Conduct examination on books of accounts if in the validation of the personnel of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office that there will be under declaration of gross receipts by taxpayers to establish effective enforcement and collection of taxes, fees and charges as provided in the Ordinance.

Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed that thru the GFPS to expedite the completion of the GAD Database and ensure that it contain SDD and other gender related information to facilitate in the conduct of gender analysis, identify gender issues and determine gender-responsive PPAs for prioritization in programming and budgeting in the GAD Agenda and annual GPB, respectively in accordance with Annex B, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-0; and

Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed that the GFPS to formulate a GAD Agenda for the remaining term of the LCE in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and be made as basis in the preparation of the annual GPB.

We recommended that management exert effort to determine the composition of the unidentified prior years’ balance to determine if the same were fully depreciated and/no longer existing and make the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended and the LCE
agreed to
(a) continuously implement programs, projects, and activities that will assist its constituents in mitigating the adverse effects of the pandemic; (b) continue the LGU’s COVID-19 immunization program until the desired local herd immunity is achieved; and (c) instruct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the MHO to explore other methods to determine and recognize in the books the full cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year end and to henceforth record future receipts and issuances of the same.
observations.managementAction:
The LCE, along with the Municipal Health
Office and COVID-19
task force,
consistently executes
initiatives to aid
residents in alleviating
the pandemic's
adverse impacts.
MHO and Vaccination
Team are progressing
with the LGU's
COVID-19
immunization
program.
The Municipal
Accountant prepared
the journal entry
voucher to adjust the
recognition of
inventory costs at
year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE
agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer to continuously
comply with the withholding and
full remittance of the taxes
withheld within the prescribed
period.
observations.managementAction: All taxes withheld by the LGU are remitted in full within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Municipal
for its continuous compliance with the GSIS law, and advised, for which the LCE concurred, to continue the withholding and timely remittance of all premium contributions and loan amortizations of its employee members as well as the government’s share to the fund.
observations.managementAction: All withheld contributions are remitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for its sustained satisfactory observance of the National Health Insurance Act, and advised, for which the LCE concurred, to continue the withholding and timely remittance of all PHIC contributions of its employee members.
observations.managementAction: All withheld contributions are remitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Municipal
Accounting Office and the
Municipal Treasurer’s Office for
their continuous compliance on
proper deductions and remittances of PAG-IBIG Fund, and recommended, for which the LCE agreed, to continue comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679.
observations.managementAction: All withheld contributions are remitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:
a. City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the unreleased checks to revert it back to the Cash in Bank account; and henceforth,
b. strictly comply with the guidelines provided in Section 112 of PD No. 1445 and Paragraph 27 of the IPSAS.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant already prepared the necessary adjusting journal entries for the unreleased checks in CY 2023 per JEV No. 018-23-01-100 dated February 28, 2023.
observations.reason: The City Government installed control measures such as conduct of inventory of checks on hand as at year-end by the City Treasurer’s Office to ensure strict compliance with the mentioned guidelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a. the City Accountant to submit to the City Legal Officer the cases of the accountable officers who have already resigned, and whose location cannot be traced for appropriate legal remedies; 
b. the City Legal Officer to recommend the appropriate action to be done to recover the unliquidated cash advances; and henceforth,
c. strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant had forwarded an endorsement letter to the City Legal Office seeking assistance on the cases of the accountable officers who have already resigned and whose location cannot be traced. The City Legal Office issued their recommendation thru a Memorandum to recover the unliquidated cash advances. The City Accounting Office submitted the cases to the City Legal Office. As of December 31, 2023, cash advances granted to various AOs in CY 2023 totaling P30,687,479.37 remained unliquidated as at year-end aging from two to 138 days.
observations.reason: Some AOs encountered difficulty in liquidating their cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant as regards the discrepancy of P466,706.16 between the recorded balances of the reciprocal accounts;
b. prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the said accounts in the City’s financial statements; and henceforth,
c. strictly comply with Sections 111 and 112 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The City coordinated with the Provincial Accountant and they already determined the cause of the discrepancy of P466,706.16 between the recorded balances of the reciprocal accounts. The City Accountant prepared the necessary adjusting journal entries per JEV No. 2307-223 dated January 12, 2023 and JEV Nos. 2302-570, 2302-571 and 2302-572 dated February 1, 2023 were prepared by the City Accountant except for the noted discrepancy that pertains to the unaccounted balance on the part of the Provincial Government of Rizal.
observations.reason: The discrepancy pertains to an unaccounted balance on the part of the Provincial Government of Rizal.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Planning and Development Officer to revisit its policy on project implementation, taking into consideration the target period of implementation; and henceforth, implementing office for the immediate implementation of the PPAs; and
b. City Accountant to coordinate with the City Property Officer as regards the documents supporting the issuance of the plastic crates and other supplies for the Briquetting Equipment, to serve as basis in the proper recognition of the same in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: City Planning and Development Office closely coordinated with concerned implementing office and as of December 31, 2023, the project was fully implemented. The City Accounting Office prepared the necessary adjusting journal entry per JEV No. 0013-03-23-300 dated March 31, 2023 to properly recognized the issuance of the plastic crates and other supplies in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the affected account in the City’s financial statements; 
b. ensure that all supplies and materials received from other government agencies/sourced agencies are properly and completely accounted in the books; and henceforth,
c. strictly comply with Section 63 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 120 and 121, Chapter 7 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City prepared the needed adjusting journal entries. The City Accounting Office ensured strict compliance on the recording of supplies and materials received from other government agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. instruct the concerned City officials to properly plan the implementation of all PPAs funded out of the 20 percent DF to avoid delays, and ensure 100 percent accomplishment within the target period; 
b. direct the City Planning and Development Officer and the City Engineer to closely monitor and vigorously pursue the timely and full implementation of all the programs and activities embodied in the 20 percent Development Fund; and
c. strictly observe the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20 percent DF under Sections 3.1 and 4 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 01 dated November 4, 2020.
observations.managementAction: Assessment of existing process on the planning and implementation of the PPAs was conducted and resulted in optimal utilization of the current appropriation of the 20 percent DF. The City Planning and Development Officer and the City Engineer closely monitored the implementation of the PPAs funded under the 20 percent DF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer to fast track the process of identifying the projects recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure that are existing, and reassess the value based on the guidelines mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008; 
b. City Accountant to record in the books of accounts the value of the road network projects to be identified by the City Engineering Office; and 
c. Concerned city officials, to henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2015-008.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer has identified the projects recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. The City Accountant prepared the necessary adjusting journal entry per JEV No. 070-23-04-100 dated May 3, 2023 to record the value of the Road Network projects identified by the City Engineering Office. The City Government ensures strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Effect the derecognition of P1,448,138.25 representing the demolished portion of the infrastructure structures; and
b. Coordinate with the City Engineer in order to identify the costs and to reconcile the PPE records as regards the PPEs that have been replaced due to repair, renovation, and record the necessary adjustments, to comply with Paragraph 85 of IPSAS 17.
observations.managementAction: The City prepared JEV No. 019-23-01-100 dated February 28, 2023 to record the value of the demolished portion of the infrastructure assets. The City coordinated with the City Engineer to reconcile the PPE records and to identify the demolished part of the infrastructure for necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Recognize expense when incurred and report the same in the financial statements in the period to which they relate; and
b. Follow the guidelines of IPSAS 23, specifically on the recognition of revenue from non-exchange transactions, in this case, the receipt of grants/fund transfers from the National Government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant recognized expense and revenue in the Trust Fund books of the Municipality for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Refrain granting cash advances to elected officials for purposes other than their own official traveling expenses; and henceforth,
b. Strictly observe the guidelines on the granting and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No.97-002.
observations.managementAction: An Office Memorandum was issued by the Municipal Mayor instructing the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting cash advances to elected officials for purposes other than official traveling expenses. Strict compliance to the guidelines on granting and liquidation of cash advances is being observed by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to:
a. Ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% DF thru efficient implementation of all priority development projects, programs and activities that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic, and environmental development of the Municipality; and
b. Properly plan the timely procurement and implementation of all projects identified priority development projects under the 20% DF to guarantee the prompt accomplishment of the same.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator had already included all PPA’s chargeable to 20% DF in the yearly preparation of the Annual Investment Plan for CY 2023 which all said PPAs were all implemented during the year. Also, the subject PPAs under continuing appropriations was being implemented in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct GAD- Focal Person to:
a. Ensure compliance in the five percent allocation requirement of the GAD Budget mentioned in the above JMC, as amended; and
b. Prepare and submit GAD – AR in the prescribed format, regularly within the prescribed period for timely evaluation of the gender-responsiveness of the GAD-related PPAs implemented by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2023, the Municipality appropriated P33,239,500.00 or 5.79 percent of its final budget of P574,000,000.00 for its GAD-related PPAs. The Municipal GAD Focal Person submitted GAD AR in the prescribed format in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:
a. Prepare the required reports on time and provide the assigned Supervising Auditor and/or Audit Team Leader with an updated list of all ongoing PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year, for audit validation and verification; and
b. Strictly adhere to COA Circular No.2013-004 for the timely submission of the notices of posting of PPAs signboard and the required Monthly Monitoring Report.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer had already prepared the required reports and provided the Audit Team with an updated list of all ongoing PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year; The Municipality adhered with COA Circular No. 2013-004.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to:
a. Assess the existing organization structure and staffing pattern of the Municipality so that the ideal number of plantilla items/positions could be established and develop a written policy guideline on the hiring of individuals under JO/COS, clearly defining the conditions whereby the employment of such JO/COS personnel may be availed of; and
b. Consider those qualified JO/COS personnel for appointment to vacant regular plantilla positions in the agency.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality conducted already the assessment of the existing organizational structure and staffing pattern of the Municipality and submitted a copy of their written policy guidelines on hiring personnel through JO and COS for CY 2024 to the previous Audit Team. 31 and 18 JOs were promoted to permanent position in CY 2022 and 2023, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Inventory Committee to prove the existence of the Municipality’s properties, complete the inventory count and prepare and submit to the Auditor RPCPPE in the prescribed form. Prioritize the reconciliation of the results of the inventory count with the property and accounting records. Any discrepancies found should be investigated, and the necessary adjustment should be made where the error was committed;
b. GSO to update the Property Cards for all PPE accounts and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land to substantiate the correctness of the general ledger balances for PPE accounts; and
c. Concerned Municipal Officials to submit a request for relief from property accountability and all the required documents for the flooded PPE to the Commission on Audit to facilitate approval thereof so the cost of the said items be dropped from the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Inventory Committee has completed the RPCPPE for CY 2021 and the same was submitted to COA on January 31, 2022. GSO updated the Property Cards for all PPE accounts and the OIC-Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary adjusting journal entries to present the correct balances of the said accounts. Necessary documents are being processed and will be submitted to COA once necessary documents for the deceased, resigned, and separated employees were notarized.
observations.reason: Not yet conducted reconciliation with the accounting records. Notarized affidavits and other necessary documents from deceased, resigned, and separated employees were not yet secured, thus hindering completion of necessary documents for the request for relief.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the OIC-Municipal Accountant to remit all the unused balance of funds to the BTR/Source agencies so that the amount could be immediately used for other projects/programs of the National Government and to relieve the Municipality from any accountability/liability for the unused funds; and
b. Henceforth, observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 in implementing projects funded by source agencies to ensure that the funds are utilized and liquidated on time and the delivery of services and benefits to the public will be facilitated.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer remitted the unused balance of funds duly to BTR/source agencies. The Municipality observed/adhered the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWMC to coordinate with the NSWMC for the guidelines in conduction the Waste Analysis Characterization Study (WACS) to immediately address the increasing volume of wastes within the locality for environmental sanitation and public health.
observations.managementAction: The updated guidelines on the WACS and its related manual have already been approved by the NSWMC and an MOA with TESDA and Coca-Cola Philippines was entered to conduct Solid Waste Training; WACS output updated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Treasurer and other officials concerned to undertake an aggressive Tax Collection Campaign for timely collection and develop strategies to improve their tax collection efficiency. Avail legal remedies such as administrative and judicial actions to enforce collections from delinquent real property taxpayers, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is currently conducting an aggressive Tax Collection Campaign for CY 2023.
observations.reason: The Municipality is conducting Tax Collection Campaign and reported increased collections (as of December 31, 2023).

observations.managementAction:
The Management created the Inventory Committee under Executive Order No. 41, series of 2023 and submitted their Physical Inventory Plan for the “One-time Cleansing” of the Municipality’s PPE Account balances to address the net discrepancy between the book balances of PPE and actual Physical Inventory.
In addition, the management submitted a Lapsing Schedule for various PPE accounts.
observations.reason: One-time cleansing has not been undertaken. Reconciliation of records were conducted but variances still exist between records. Accounts reconciliation and complete one-time cleansing procedures were not fully taken up.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant discontinue the practice of directly closing the expense account to its related liability account in the Trust Fund books and to follow the guidelines of IPSAS 23, specifically on the recognition of revenue from non-exchange transactions, in this case, the receipt of funds from other government agencies.
observations.managementAction: The Utilized amount of fund transfer from National Government Agencies for specific purpose were already recognize as income in the Trust Fund book.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to conduct thorough review and reconciliation of all reported transactions related to the Cash Local Treasury in all funds;
