observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO coordinated with other offices and agencies to track the implementation of PPAs and requested for schedule of trainings and risk assessment; and budgets were requested for the implementation of various LDRRM related PPAs.
observations.reason: Not Implemented for some PPAs; the unimplemented PPAs were carried over.

observations.recommendation: a. Immediately fill up the remaining mandatory vacant positions at the LDRRMO to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the PPAs funded under the LDRRMF; and b. Ensure the effective implementation of the LDRRMF pursuant to Section No. 12(b) of RA No. 10121.
observations.managementAction: Vacant plantilla were already posted in the Civil Service Commission and Personnel Selection Board (PSB) conducted screening for the qualified contenders.

observations.recommendation: b. Prepare the annual GAD-AR following the form prescribed in Annex E of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-001.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person provided the quantitative data on the actual results of GAD PPAs and ensured thorough review of the GAD-AR before submission to DILG and COA.

observations.recommendation: b. OIC-Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the OIC-Municipal Treasurer, to remit to the BTR/Source Agencies all the unused balance of funds pursuant to the cited regulations, so that the amount could be immediately used for other projects/programs of the National Government and to relieve the Municipality from any accountability/liability for the said unused funds. Should there be a need for the unspent funds, the Municipality should request from the source agencies for authority/approval to use the excess amounts for other purposes or projects.
observations.managementAction: Management coordinated with the Source Agency for the implementation of the projects.

observations.recommendation: b. Dispose the unserviceable PPEs in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 to avoid further deterioration; and c. Instruct the GSO to submit the documents such as list of unserviceable documents, pictures of disposed equipment, deed of donation if donated to other agency or the Official Receipt from sale of the unserviceable PPE item/s to the Accounting Office, as basis of the latter to drop the unserviceable properties from books of accounts in accordance with the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: An IIRUP was already submitted by Management to the Audit Team. Disposal of other unserviceable PPEs other than motor vehicles was still in progress.

observations.recommendation: b. Determine the cause/s of the delay in the submission of the accounts of the Municipality to the Audit Team and find solutions thereto.
observations.managementAction: Immediate submission of financial reports and supporting documents were undertaken by concerned offices and the Municipal Accountant. Management implemented internal control measures for the timely recording of transactions.

observations.recommendation: a. Publicize and open the vacant positions to all qualified applicants for the Municipal Treasurer who will take charge of the disbursement of all local government funds and such other funds which custody may be entrusted to him/her by law or other competent authority; and b. Fill up the Municipal Accountant who will perform both the accounting and internal audit services of the Municipality to help ensure the proper stewardship of public funds.
observations.managementAction: Vacant plantilla positions were already posted and the Personnel Selection Board (PSB) conducted screening and filling up of the required positions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of directly closing the expense account to its related liability account in the books and to follow the guidelines of IPSAS 23, specifically on the recognition of revenue from non-exchange transactions, in this case, the receipt of grants from the National Government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office already recognize revenue upon utilization of funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the noted difference amounting to P342,238,529.24 between their records; and henceforth; b. Municipal Accountant, OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Assessor to conduct a periodic reconciliation of the balances of Basic and Special Education Tax dues and RPT and SET Receivable accounts to immediately detect and rectify any discrepancies that may arise.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer reconciled the noted difference amounting to P342,238,529.24 as shown in the reconciliation report dated March 2, 2023 and which was submitted to Auditor’s Office on March 30, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record the receipt and issuance of the procured and issuance of the procured inventoriable items of the Municipality in conformity with the generally accepted accounting principles and mentioned pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2023, the Municipal Accounting Office carried out the recording of receipt and issuance of the procured inventoriable items in conformity with the pertinent rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to: a. asses the existing organization structure and the staffing pattern of the Municipality so that the ideal number of plantilla items/positions could be established and develop a written policy guideline on the hiring of the individuals under the JO/Contract of Service; b. consider those qualified JO personnel for appointment to casual or vacant regular plantilla positions in the agency.
observations.managementAction: The HRMO already drafted the “Policy Guidelines for the Hiring and Rehiring of Personnel under Job Order/Contract of Services” of the Municipality and submitted its copy to Auditor’s Office on March 2024. It outlines the general guidelines, the provisions for the need for hiring/rehiring, conditions for disqualifications, and applicable funding and payment of rates of services, and other requirements. Qualified JO personnel were considered for appointment in 54 casual positions. Also, the 105 vacant plantilla positions in CY 2022 was decreased to 80 in CY 2023 as qualified personnel were considered to occupy said items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Budget Officer to replenish the LDRRMF from the General Fund in the amount of P11,712,613.20, which was expended for transactions not directly related to the four thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management. Otherwise, the same will be disallowed in audit; and b. Municipal Budget Officer and OIC-MDRRMO to strictly observe the guidelines in the utilization of the LDRRMF provided under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and ensure that only expenditures directly related to disaster risk management are charge against the fund.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer already coordinated with Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to approve the replenishment/payment of LDRRMF amounting to P11,712,613.20 on the target date on May 2024 through a supplemental budget. The Municipal Budget Officer ensures that LDRRM funds were strictly expended according to the four thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management.
observations.reason: Replenishment of fund is subject yet for approval of SB.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the refund of the Gratuity Pay charged against the LDRRMF amounting to P284,000.00. Otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer refund/paid the amount of P284,000.00 under OR no. 21231360A this dated April 23, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System to: a. Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs in accordance with the DILG endorsed GPB to ensure that the PPAs are undertaken within the target period; and b. Closely coordinate with the Implementing offices to ensure that the GAD PPAs are implemented within the appropriated budget.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal GAD Coordinator committed to strictly monitor the utilization of GAD PPAs on quarterly basis and to apply more concrete GAD monitoring and evaluation system. The Municipal Budget Officer and GAD Coordinator closely monitor the budget per PPA. In CY 2023, no GAD PPAs were implemented beyond the appropriated budget.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 15, Page Nos. 66 to 69.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the: b. Municipal Accountant to subsequently prepare the required adjusting journal entries to present the correct balances of the said accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements; and c. Municipal Accountant to conduct a periodic reconciliation of accounts Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs in the Municipality’s books with the corresponding reciprocal accounts in the books of the Province.
observations.managementAction: As per reconciliation of the Municipal Accounting Office and the Provincial Accounting Office, the Provincial Accountant acknowledged that the discrepancy is for adjustment in the books of the Provincial Office, hence no adjustment is necessary in the books of the Municipality as supported by letter dated January 30, 2023. The Municipal Accountant and Provincial Accountant conducted already regular periodic reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare necessary journal entry to record all the real property owned by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 2023-08-3110 dated August 15, 2023 was made by the Municipal Accountant to recognize the land totaling P32,232,620.00 in the books of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to determine the details of the discrepancies between the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from/Due to LGU’s recorded in the Municipality and Province’s record so that appropriate adjustment can be made for fair presentation of the affected accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements; and
b. henceforth, conduct periodic reconciliation of Municipality’s account with the Provincial accounting records to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies between the two records.
observations.managementAction: Coordination with the Provincial Accounting Office to reconcile has been made for the balances as of June 30 and September 21, 2023. Certification Not made by the former dated May 8, 2023 for the balance of Due to LGU account as of December 31, 2023.
observations.reason: Verification by the Audit Team showed discrepancy of P104,770.97 between the records of the Provincial Accountant and the Municipal Accountant based on the Certification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: adhere to the above-cited regulations that all payments of the Municipality should be made by check directly payable to the suppliers and ensure that only small amounts should be paid in cash through the petty cash fund.
observations.managementAction: An inter-office Memorandum on the reimbursement policy was issued by the Municipal Mayor dated July 19, 2023 to all officials and employees to implement the no reimbursement policy exceeding the threshold amount of P1,000.00 effective immediately.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. request for authority to the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of the same after analyzing and determining the payables without valid documents and those that have been outstanding for two years or more;
b. exert effort to locate and retrieve the relevant documents pertaining to the transaction to establish its validity and legality; and
c. prepare necessary journal entries to correct the noted erroneous recording of the transaction.
observations.managementAction: Letter request dated August 14, 2023 by the Municipal Accountant to the Municipal Mayor for the reversal of payables has been made resulting to Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No. 160 s. 2023 dated Sept. 4, 2023. The old balances were included in the request for reversion to Government Equity account. Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) Nos. 101-2023-09-03599 and 221-2023-09-00772 both dated September 19, 2023 were made by the Municipal Accountant to take-up the reversion to Government Equity account. Also, JEV No. TF-2023-08-423 dated August 17, 2023 was made by the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors made in the subject account.

The Municipal Mayor has issued a letter dated July 19, 2023 addressed to the Department Heads to submit their proposed organization structure for consolidation. Policy guidelines in hiring job order personnel and contract of service was submitted to the Audit Team on May 22, 2024. 15 qualified job order personnel were already appointed as 10 casual employees and five regular employees.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2023, there were 277 JO personnel in the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to exercise caution in recording the transaction and make sure that it conforms with the generally accepted accounting principles and is in accordance with pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant commits to implement the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Housing to submit the following: a. distribution list for goods distributed to the homeowners in the resettlement sites; b. pertinent documents evidencing extension on the validity of the 2020 RGAF, otherwise, return the unexpended balance of the said fund to the source agency; and c. a written explanation why the Municipality directly paid the honorarium to the community workers instead of transferring the funds to the barangay concerned as provided in the MOA.
observations.managementAction: The distribution list was submitted to the Audit Team. The expended balance of the fund has been returned to the Bureau of Treasury per Official Receipt (OR) No. 7065766 dated April 12, 2023 totaling P31,960.00. Written explanation has been submitted in a letter dated March 28, 2023 stating that the hiring of community workers were endorsed to the Teresa LGU in coordination with St. Therese Housing Home Owners Association and Barangay Dalig to achieve a more efficient process.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to update all identified projects with lacking/insufficient information in the signboard in compliance with mentioned regulation. Thereafter, make sure that all PPAs undertaken by the Municipality comply with the notification of all information on these PPAs for full disclosure of all transactions involving public interest.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Advisory to all contractors has been made by the Municipal Engineering Office to adhere on the required posting of information in the signboard. Thereafter, make sure signboard to fully disclose all public transactions impacting the public interest and to recognize the public’s right to information on issued public concern.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO) to prepare a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMFIP including the list of projects and activities to be funded out of the previous years unexpended LDRRMF in accordance with the above-mentioned regulations. Likewise, we also recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to properly incorporate all activities in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) as approved by the LDRRM Council.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMFIP for CY 2023-2025 was submitted to the Audit Team on February 12, 2024 which included the planned utilization of the Special Trust Fund out of the unexpended LDRRMF in the previous years. Likewise, the AIP for CY 2023 included activities embodied in the LDRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal person to: a. ensure compliance with the JMC on the five percent allocation requirement for GAD Budget of the Municipality; and b. regularly monitor and ensure the efficient implementation of GAD PPAs in accordance with the DILG endorsed GPB.
observations.managementAction: The CY 2023 Annual GAD Budget allocated five percent to GAD. Certificate of Review and Endorsement (CRE) for the GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2023 was issued by the DILG dated November 2, 2023. The Municipal Advisory to the GAD focal person reiterated modifications in Observation page no. of Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:                            

Exert effort to identify and verify the reconciling items and proceed with the recording and adjusting of the same in the books of accounts of the Municipality, especially on the items with notable amount;

Commit to perform a periodic reconciliation of the Treasurer’s Cashbook and of the Accounting Ledgers, to aid in the identification of material differences between their records, and arrive at a reliable Construction in Progress (CIP) account balance 

Set-up control policies in order to prevent  the accumulation of unadjusted reconciling items in the BRS.

We recommend that Management:

Create an Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of local road networks and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network not later January 31, of each year.

Require the Municipal Accountant to present in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosure for local road networks, and to prepare and maintain Local Road Network Ledger Cards for each local road; and 

Require the Municipal General Services Officer to prepare the annual Report of Local Road Network Property Cards for each local road.

The Municipal Treasurer  furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and at the amount due and collectible for the year which will serve as basis of the latter for the booking up of the RPT and SET Receivable accounts.

We further recommend that the Municipal Treasurer update/complete its records related to RPT (RPT)and (SET) in order to establish periodic reconciliation of balances with the books of accounts maintain by the Municipal Accountant to come up with a more accurate and proper balance of RPT and SET Receivable accounts.

To instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify and record payables to its appropriate account in order to present fairly the Accounts Payable as well as the Due to Officers and Employees account in the financial statements.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant fully observe and adhere to the disclosure requirements  in the preparation of the Notes to Financial Statements particularly the Reconciliation of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts and Statement of Financial Performance to provide users of the same relevant and adequate information and a clearer understanding of the financial statements.

The Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to concerned Accountable Officers requiring the immediate liquidation of CAs and effect the necessary sanctions for non-liquidation of the same.

To refrain/stop from preparing approved payrolls and drawing cash advance related therewith without identifying the names of the payees.

To refrain/stop drawing cash advance for official travel under the name of other official /employees to properly pinpoint responsibility/accountability.

We recommended that fuel expenses with the nature of financial assistance be charged to the General Fund appropriation   and return back to the BGCM fund the said amount with corresponding adjusting entries. Otherwise, refund the same.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2020 | The BGCM Fund was already fully liquidated. A Supplemental Budget from the General Fund will be prepared equivalent to the amount charged to BGCM fund  and to be remitted to the National Treasury as soon as possible.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the Municipal Accountant analyze the Receivables accounts, Due from NGAs, Due from GOCCs, Due from NGOs/POs, and Other Receivables amounting to P48,237.99, P2,161,652.37, P990.92 and P12,854.05, respectively, which has been dormant for 13 years and make the necessary verification against previous financial reports and schedules to determine the details of these accounts and confirm their balances, if warranted.  Otherwise, submit request of authority to COA for write off the accounts following the procedures set under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert the Due to Officers and Employees and Notes Payable which remained dormant for more than ten years and no actual claim has been filed in the amount of P4,786.47 and P1,096.48, respectively, pursuant to Section 98 of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant reasoned the accounts have been long outstanding since no subsidiary ledger maintained by her predecessor.  However, she committed to comply with our recommendations and will submit request of authority to COA for write-off of these dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Reclassify those cash advances granted to those officers and employees who are already dead, resigned, no longer connected with the LGU and those whereabouts are not known to “Other Receivables” account in the total amount of P4,172,123.84;

Require the Municipal Accountant issue demand letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and demand the immediate settlement thereof; and

Require for write-off of dormant accounts where collection is nil following the procedures set under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with our recommendations.  The Municipal Accountant will reclassify the long outstanding cash advances and issue demand letter to officials and employees to settle their unliquidated cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Inventory Team/Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of property, plant and equipment in order to finalize the preparation of the RPCPPE pursuant to Section 66 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System; and 

Hire job order personnel, if necessary, to assist in the conduct of physical count of PPE for a speedy accomplishment and submission of the RPCPPE.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to complete the conduct physical count of all PPE and will hire job order personnel to assist on the conduct of physical count and prepare necessary documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Submit the lacking documents as proof that they actually conducted the prescribed procedures on negotiated procurement; and 

Strictly comply with the provisions of the RA 9184 and its IRR for all its procurement and the required transparency of government procurements in order to avoid sanctions and penalties and disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to submit the lacking documents and strictly comply with the provisions of the RA 9184 and its IRR.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following to the Management:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly signed certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible every beginning of the year for recording purposes.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV to record the RPT/SET receivables based on the certified list from the Office of the Treasurer and ensure that the recorded receivables are based on actual reference documents.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management direct the Accounting Office, the General Service Office and the Assessor’s Office to coordinate with each other to reconcile the records of both offices, and provide complete description of the property to reflect the true and correct balance of the Land account.
We further recommended that management identify the exact location of all the lots owned by the LGU and secure the property by putting fence. Facilitate the titling or transfer of title of the parcels of land owned by the LGU to avoid possible risk/disputes or claims of outside parties.

We recommended that management exert effort to determine the composition of the unidentified prior years’ balance in order to determine if the same were fully depreciated and/no longer existing and make the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:

properly classify and record collections from the sale of bid documents to their appropriate account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 for fair presentation of accounts in the financial position of the LGU; and
consider the following adjusting journal entry to reclassify the collection of bid documents from the Guarantee/Deposit Payable account to the Trust Liabilities account or income account whichever is applicable.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts taking into consideration the  aforementioned pro-forma entry in Item 1.3 for fair presentation of accounts in the LGU’s financial statement; and
Municipal Accountant likewise coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer upon recognition of stale check for the latter to send written notice to the payees in order to verify its existence/status and accordingly its replacement upon submission of the stale checks.
Municipal Treasurer that for succeeding checks that become staled, a written notice should be sent to the payee at least one month before a check becomes stale to avoid its accumulation.

We recommended that management require the identified officials and employees to file an application for bonding with the Bureau of Treasury, strictly monitor the expiration of bond coverage and apply for renewal thereof prior to its expiration as required in Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019 with due consideration on the processing time from the application to the approval of the bond.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to make a correcting entry on those completed projects that were recorded as outright expenses in the amount of P1,362,509.66 and record the same in the corresponding Property, Plant and Equipment accounts.

We recommended that the Management submit the lacking supporting documents to strictly ensure the validity, propriety, necessity and regularity on the purchase of the two lots in the amount of P18,000,000.00.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to disclose adequate information as to breakdown and relevant details of the Inter-Agency Payables in the Notes to Financial Statements for a more comprehensive information on the financial condition of the LGU.

We recommended that the Management:
require the MHO and the concerned departments to henceforth furnish the Accounting Office with complete documents relative to the donated COVID-19 vaccines, medicines and other donations-in kind for proper accounting thereof; and
perform an inventory of all the undistributed/undisposed vaccines, if there is any, and recognize as inventories in the books of accounts of the Municipality for fair presentation of account balances in the financial statements in keeping with Paragraphs 27 and 96 of IPSAS 1 and 23, respectively and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that Management instruct the Inventory Team/Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of property, plant and equipment in order to finalize the preparation of the RPCPPE pursuant to Section 66 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System.

We recommend that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify those cash advances granted to those officers and employees who are already dead, resigned, no longer connected with the LGU and those whereabouts are not known to “Other Receivables” account in the total amount of P4,172,123.84;
Proposed Entry:
Other Receivables		P4,172,123.84
Advances to Officers and Employees		P4,172,123.84
Require the Municipal Accountant to issue demand letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and demand the immediate settlement thereof; and
Request for write-off of dormant accounts where collection is nil following the procedures set under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Management direct the LDRRM Officer henceforth prepare the LDRRMFIP using the prescribed format provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years, which were already transferred to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Management:
Examine the reasons for the increase in the number of job order personnel and the need to hire such personnel, especially those performing clerical and administrative functions.  Hiring of personnel under job order contract should only be resorted to as the need arises and deployed to offices that actually are in need of their services; and
Formulate Policy Guidelines in hiring contractual/job order personnel to ensure that the hiring of additional manpower is necessary and indispensable in the delivery of vital services needed in the implementation of the various programs and activities of the Municipality and will result in the optimum utilization of financial resources.
We recommended that the Management strictly use funds for the specific purpose for which they have been appropriated pursuant to Section 336 of RA 7160 or the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended and the General Services Officer (GSO) designate/ Municipal Treasurer agreed to:

 cause the accomplishment of transfer documents of properties donated to  concerned agencies and submit copy thereof  to the accounting office for  derecognition in the books.

cancel the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) and issue Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to inventories/ properties costing below the ₱15,000.00 threshold.

We further recommended that the accounting office effect the Proposed Adjusting Journal Entries as shown in Annex E to correct balances of affected accounts.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2021 | GSO designate already submitted transfer documents to the accounting office.


Cancelled PAR, issued ICS and record items below ₱15,000.00 as inventories.


Recommendation well taken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that:

the Municipal Treasurer  initiate the process of disposing the unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines under NBC 425 dated January 28, 1992  to avoid  further deterioration.

the Municipal Accountant record the eventual disposal of unserviceable properties  to reflect the actual properties owned which are essential or useful to the LGU.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2021 | Unserviceable properties were disposed in October 2022.



Entry was made in October 2022 recognizing the proceeds of the disposal totaling ₱60,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the provision of Property Number and other vital information on all government properties in accordance with COA Circular 80-124 dated June 18, 1980 and COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2021 | There are still some properties which were not tagged because the office concerned lacks manpower to complete the tagging.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the LDRRMO, in coordination with the LDRRMC, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 in the preparation of LDRRMF Investment Plan ensuring that the projects and activities listed therein consist of the current and prior years’ LDRRMF and to incorporate the same plan in the AIP of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2021 | The unexpended balance in prior years was already incorporated in the Supplemental Investment Plans of CY 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the LDRRMO prepare and submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the prescribed format  in the COA-Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 with the utilization portion accord with the PPAs indicated in the LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2021 | Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund is submitted monthly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to support transactions with complete documentation and to require the schools concerned to submit liquidation documents such as: pictures before and after the construction and statement of work accomplished, etc.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2021 | The subject documents were already submitted to COA.

Recommendation well taken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to adopt policies and internal controls to regulate loan accommodations and other payroll deductions to ensure strict compliance with the minimum monthly net take home pay of ₱5,000.00 pursuant to Section 49 of the General Provisions of RA No. 11518.
observations.managementAction: Management commented not to approve loan applications of employees whose net pay will fall below ₱5,000.00 if loan deductions will take effect and not to grant additional loans to those whose net pay is already below ₱5,000.00.
observations.reason: There are still employees whose net pay is below ₱5,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose the related party transaction in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 20 paragraph 27 to ensure transparency and for the users to be able to fully assess the financial position and performance of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant committed to disclose all related party transactions in the Notes to Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to record the reversion of stale checks in the General Fund amounting to P54,545.00 pursuant to Section 59, Chapter  3 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant informed that the adjusting entries were already made for the reversion of stale dated checks and committed to consistently comply with the provision of Section 59, Chapter  3 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Liquidating Officer religiously reconcile the recorded amounts reflected in her cashbook to avoid the possibility of occurrence of shortage or overage in compliance with Section 6.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Liquidating Officer monthly reconciles its book to Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Field Collectors to remit their collections daily to the Liquidating Officer, who will prepare the Report of Collections and submit the same to the Municipal Treasurer;

Require the Liquidating Officer to not issue new accountable forms to Field Collectors without remitting the previous accountable forms issued to avoid accumulation of collections in their hands; and

Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the collections daily and intact to avoid possibility of theft, mishandling and misuse of the amount collected.
observations.managementAction: Collections deposited intact to the authorized government depository bank (AGDB).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Liquidating Officer certify as to the correctness and completeness of the recorded transactions at month-end after the last entry in her cashbook.
observations.managementAction: The Liquidating officer certifies her Cashbook monthly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer include checks in the preparation of the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms, pursuant to Sections 51, Chapter 2 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume II.
observations.managementAction: During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer committed to include checks in the preparation of Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management increase the fidelity bond of the Liquidating Officer and ensure that her bond is sufficient to cover her accountabilities in compliance with Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009. in the event that there is a loss and in order to comply with Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009.
observations.managementAction: Already applied for the increase of fidelity bond

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strengthen and improve the existing controls on the disbursement of funds by adopting the ADA disbursement system wherein payment of salaries and wages are directly deposited to the respective ATM bank account of personnel, thus eliminating the risk of loss through theft or robbery and ultimately provide safeguard of funds pursuant to Section 2 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Management submit the lacking documents to support the payment of the project, Improvement/Rehabilitation of COVID-19 Quarantine Facility, in the amount of P118,521.82 and ensure that all payments to contractors for the implementation of infrastructure projects be supported with the documentary requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009 -001 dated February 12, 2009 to ensure propriety and validity of the transaction.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC to submit justification in writing for allowing the documents submitted in the bid such as the Joint Venture Agreement containing a scanned signature of Ms. Grace A. Jardin and the Omnibus Sworn Statement not accompanied by duly notarized SPA, otherwise it shall be suspended in audit and strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management submit justification in writing why payments were made to M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services based on the self-serving documents issued by the latter instead of a duly executed joint agreement between R.S. Jardin & Associates and M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services, otherwise all payments made thereof shall be suspended in audit and strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 2 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management ensure that all contract agreements be supported with complete documentary requirements prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012 – 001 dated June 14, 2012. Further, we recommend that the lacking documents be submitted immediately to facilitate the review of the procurement.
observations.managementAction:
The Management thru its BAC submitted a reply and commented that they merely relied on the notarized Joint Venture Agreement and notarized Special Power of Attorney submitted by R.S. Jardin & Associates and M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services.


The Management thru its Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant commented in their reply that payments were paid to M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services because the latter was the one who requested for billing of the said consulting services and they were not informed by the BAC that it was a joint venture with R.S. Jardin & Associates.  More so, they merely rely on the invoice submitted not knowing that the contract was a joint venture.


The Management ensure to submit all contract agreements with complete documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC send invitation to COA and two observers at least five (5) calendar days before the date of procurement stage/activity and address the invitations to other two observers with particular identity in compliance with Section 13 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. Moreover, secure proof of receipt of the written invitations by the concerned observers.
observations.managementAction: The Management thru its BAC committed to comply with our recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to make a correcting entry on those procured properties that were recorded as outright expenses in the amount of P479,175.00 and record the same in the corresponding PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant committed to make a correcting entry on the said transactions and submit the corresponding JEV to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require:

The Municipal Accountant record all inventories as asset upon delivery and acceptance; and

The Supply Officer to conduct the periodic physical count of supplies and materials every semester and prepare and submit the corresponding Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) pursuant to Section 124, Chapter 7 of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant committed to adhere with our recommendations and informed that for CY 2022, procurements of supplies and materials shall be recorded using the Asset Method.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Development Council, the MPDC Office and the Budget Office to comply with the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01, that all development projects to be funded under the 20% DF shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU, and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
observations.managementAction: The Management informed that the purchase of said vehicles was authorized and supported by SB Resolution No. 74, series of 2020 dated June 22, 2020.  However, the Management committed that charges to 20% Development shall be made in accordance with the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE in the amount of P49,874.05 and P790,794.92, respectively, to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and reconcile the difference of P157.00 between the amount per Notes to Financial Statements and amount presented in the financial statements to ensure correct information and amounts presented thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already complied with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
Enforce collection and settlement of the long outstanding receivables and cash advances through issuance of demand letters.

Require the Municipal Accountant to submit a sworn Certification for unliquidated cash advances, and other receivables with no available documents but are not yet considered dormant as defined under COA Circular 2016-005.

Initiate the preparation and filing of the request for authority to write-off accounts with the COA in accordance with COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Send Demand letters to officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Municipal Engineer to strictly monitor the project implementations and ensure that projects are implemented and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, impose liquidated damages (LD) therein; and henceforth ensure that projects funded out grants from NGAs are completed in accordance with the approved plans within the target date of completion; and (b) Municipal Accountant to (i) impose and issue order of payment for LDs as determined and recommended by the Municipal Engineer and/or institute appropriate legal actions/charges against the contractor who abandoned the projects amounting to P4,800,000.00; and (ii) send demand letter to the contractor concerned to refund the unrecouped advances to contractors for the abandoned projects; and to cause the return of the unexpended balances of the Due to NGAs totaling P254,335.41 and/or make representations with the source agency for the possible reprogramming of the unexpended balances to other PPAs for proper presentation of the Due to NGAs account in the Financial Statements.
observations.reason:
The Management did not submit explanation for the deficiencies cited, hence this is reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

Also, the unexpended balances totaling P254,335.41 were still not remitted to the source agencies after project completion.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point Person, strictly observe the prescribed timelines for GAD Planning and Budgeting, as provided in the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and amended by Sections 4 and 5 of the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW)-DILG-Department of Budget and Management (DBM)-National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016, to enable its timely submission to the PPDO to check its alignment with the priorities of the Province and then to the DILG Provincial Office for final review and approval.
observations.reason: The Management still failed to comply with the audit recommendation to submit the reviewed and approved copy of the CY 2020 GPB of the Municipality hence, similar audit observations had been observed in CY 2021 transactions as discussed in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
Members of the Inventory Committee:

To fast tract the reconciliation of those property items included in the inventory report with the records being maintained by the Accounting and Property/Treasurer’s Office since its existence and condition have already been established. As provided under COA Circular 2020-005, the reconciliation shall be completed within ten (10) days from rendition of the RPCPPE by the Inventory Committee.

To exert their best efforts in completing the required physical inventory taking of properties owned by the LGU and update the RPCPPE with the data of those properties not included in the previous inventory report as the detailed procedures on how to conduct which are specified above.

Further, after the updated RPCPPE have been made in accordance with the prescribed format, reconciliation thereof shall be made with that of the accounting unit’s report to establish reliability and fair valuation of the PPE accounts within ten (10) days from the submission of completed RPCPEE.

Moreover, those missing PPES established during the physical count, demand letters are to be issued to those AO’s and cause the conduct of an investigation to determine who are responsible for the loss, among others.

Municipal Treasurer and Accountant

To maintain Property Card and Equipment Ledger Cards, respectively, for easy determination identification and reconciliation of records.

The management to follow the accounting and reporting guidelines in the recognition of the various components of the Road Network as stated in COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to ensure that the Road Network System of the Municipality is properly identified, valued and accounted for in the books.

Require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to retrieve all disbursement vouchers related to the above inventories in order to establish itemized records of the said inventories. Based thereto, validate who were the end-users thereof and require the submission of RIS and the same prepare the SSMI summarizing all the receipts and issuances thereof.
Accounting Office to prepare the JEV to derecognize from the books the inventories on the basis of the SSMI submitted by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.
Require the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office to maintain SLC and SC, respectively, for each item of stocks on the basis of the disbursement vouchers and to update and reconcile records regularly.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant give utmost priority in analyzing and determining the composition of the long outstanding Inter-Agency Receivables. Otherwise, comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in requesting for the write-off of the long outstanding receivables.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Restated as Observation No. 1 of Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following to the Management:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly signed certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible every beginning of the year for recording purposes.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV to record the RPT/SET receivables based on the certified list from the Office of the Treasurer and ensure that the recorded receivables are based on actual reference documents.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Restated in Observation No. 2 of Part II of this Report.

We recommended that the Management:
Enforce collection and settlement of the long outstanding receivables and cash advances through issuance of demand letters.
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit a sworn Certification for unliquidated cash advances, and other receivables with no available documents but are not yet considered dormant as defined under COA Circular 2016-005.
Initiate the preparation and filing of the request for authority to write-off accounts with the COA in accordance with COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:

Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the collections daily and intact, if practicable, and strictly implement controls over collections and remittances to avoid misappropriations, misuse and/or concealment of irregularities in the handling of cash.

Instruct the Collectors to religiously observe daily remittance of all their collections, if practicable, together with the issued official receipts, and preparation and submission of the corresponding RCDs to the Treasurer.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Restated in Observation No.  7 of Part II of this Report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management comply strictly with the prescribed controls over PPE as provided for in pertinent laws, rules and regulations.  We also recommended to the Management the following:

Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all PPEs of the Municipality, whether acquired through purchase or donation, including those constructed by administration and found at station pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

Update the property records based on the results of physical inventory, and reconcile with accounting records thru the Inventory Committee.

Undertake all other procedures prescribed under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to come up with the reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the updated ledger cards for each item of the PPE after the inventory taking pursuant to the Section 269 of the GAM for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: Physical count of PPE was conducted only for procurements for the current year.
observations.reason: Restated in Observation No. 3 of Part II of this Report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor required the Municipal Accountant to prepare a lapsing schedule to compute the depreciation expenses of all the recorded PPE and draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the computed amount of depreciation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor required an annual physical inventory of all the properties of the Municipality to be conducted by a committee consisting of representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, Municipal GSO or Municipal Treasurer and Supply Accountable Officer of the department or office concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor required the General Services Officer (GSO) to furnish the Audit Team copies of duly signed Purchase Orders (POs) not later than five (5) working days from its issuance in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009 and submit the Notices of Delivery (NODs) within 24 hours from the time of delivery and acceptance of the procured items as required under Section 114(d) of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the Sangguniang Bayan to update the Revenue Code of the Municipality pursuant to Section 186 and 191 of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 7160 to increase the revenue collection from local taxes that can be used to finance more beneficial Projects/Programs/Activities (PPAs).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal drivers to prepare a Monthly Report of Official Travels summarizing in chronological order their trips for the month and designate an officer to prepare the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for various types of motor vehicles showing the total distance travelled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle to facilitate the validation of the reasonableness of gasoline consumption of the Municipality pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor:

a. direct the OlC-Provincial Accountant to draw JEV to reverse the entry made in
writing off the unaccounted beginning balance of Land account totaling
P291,056,457.15, to retain the balance of the said account in the Province’s
books; and henceforth,
b. strictly observe the guidelines and procedures provided under COA Circular
No. 2020-006 for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of
Government Agencies.

We also reiterated our previous recommendation that the Provincial Governor
direct the Provincial officials concerned to speed up the transfer of the titles of
forfeited real estate properties, that were only found eligible upon inspection and
verification conducted by Management, in the name of the Province, and record
their costs in the books of accounts to prevent third party claims and facilitate
disposal of the property, if needed.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to:

a. prepare the necessary journal entry to reclassify the recorded completed
projects under CIP totaling P4,945,052.65 to its appropriate PPE and Expense
accounts in the amount of P4,908,010.78 and P37,041.87, respectively, and
subsequently provide the corresponding depreciation of P199,042.21 for the
reclassified PPE accounts;
b. correct the erroneous recording of partially completed project amounting to
P155,954,168.96, by debiting CIP and crediting Hospital and Health Centers
account by the same amount; and henceforth,
c. recognize partially completed projects in the Province’s books in accordance
with Section 50 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to:

a. draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the 3,712 vials of COVID-19
vaccines still on hand amounting to P4,165,963.86 by debiting the account
Drugs and Medicines for Distribution, and crediting Prior Period Adjustments
by the same amount; and henceforth,
b. OIC-Provincial Accountant to ensure that all supplies and materials received
from other government agencies/sourced agencies are properly and
completely accounted in the Province’s book, pursuant to Section 63 of PD No.
1445.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Officer-in-Charge
(OIC)-Provincial Accountant to:

a. instruct the accounting personnel in-charge of preparing the lapsing schedule
of PPE to start computing the depreciation of an asset for one month if the
PPE is available for use on or before the 15th of the month, while depreciation
shall be for the succeeding month if the PPE is available for use after the 15th
of the month;
b. prepare the necessary journal entry to correct the understatement of
depreciation amounting to P1,483,116.49 on PPE totaling P84,164,746.24;
c. assign personnel to trace back prior years’ acquisition of PPE to have a
complete details of the PPE accounts, so that the corresponding depreciation
could be computed for fair presentation of the affected accounts in the
financial statements; and
d. compute the new lapsing schedule of PPE to update the balance of
accumulated depreciation of each PPE account, and strictly comply with the
provision of PAG 3 of IPSAS 17 on the initial recognition of depreciation
expense.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor instruct
the OlC-Provincial Accountant to make formal representation to the SAs to
reconcile the difference between the Province’s and SAs’ records by furnishing
them the received copy of the Fund Utilization Report (FURs). together with the
related supporting documents. for the completed projects. Henceforth. prepare
the necessary adjusting journal entries for the reconciling items noted.

We also recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the OlC-Provincial
Accountant to:

a. draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the errors in recording the receipt
of LGSF-FA from the BTr. and collections from the NHA and individual lot
owners of various housing projects of the Province. amounting to
P94.768.596.00 and P17.162.856.10. respectively;
b. determine the cost of land under the Housing Projects of the Province. and
subsequently set-up Receivables from the NHA and beneficiaries of the
housing projects for the unpaid amortization duly supported with individual
ledger card for each lot owner; and henceforth.
c. train accounting staff on correct data/accounting entry. and ensure that he/she
understands the accounts and description used in recording the financial
transactions of the Province. as prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts
for Local Government Units (LGUs).

We reiterated our recommendation that the Provincial Governor direct the
Provincial Treasurer and Officer-in-Charge (OIC)-Provincial Accountant to exert
effort to locate the related supporting documents, and investigate to determine
the complete details of the mentioned investments of the Province for proper
valuation and disclosure of the same in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor direct
the:

a. Inventory Committee to prepare an accurate and reliable RPCPPE; and
subsequently reconcile the inventory report with the accounting and property
records to determine the details of the difference of P2,524,625,708.64 noted in
audit, and make necessary adjustment in the respective records of the
concerned offices, if warranted;
b. Property Officer to make certain that Property Cards are well-maintained,
complete and updated to facilitate the physical count of property and
equipment; and henceforth,
c. Inventory Committee to strictly adhere to the provisions of the above­
mentioned regulations for accurate and reliable reporting of PPE account
balances in the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor direct
the:

a. Inventory Committee to conduct semestral physical count of Inventories, and
submit a report thereon to the Auditor, in proper form, summarized according
to type of inventory and properly accomplished in all particulars, in
accordance with Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. Provincial Accounting Office to maintain a complete and updated supplies
ledger cards for proper monitoring. The balance per stock cards should
always reconcile with the ledger cards of the Accounting Office. Moreover,
exert efforts in tracing back the complete details of the unaccounted balances
of Office Supplies; Drugs and Medicines; Medical, Dental and Laboratory
Supplies and Other Supplies and Materials totaling P29,306,037.11, and non­
moving balance of Accountable Forms, Plates and Stickers of P435,000.00 for
fair presentation of the Inventories accounts; and
c. OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Provincial Accountant to explain and
account the discrepancies noted in the balances of Inventories reported in the
SLC, RPCI and Stock Cards; and henceforth, strictly comply with the
provisions of Paragraph 4, Section 114 and Section 124 of the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor create an Appraisal Committee to
assess the value of the unserviceable properties for disposal.

We also reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor:

a. direct the OIC-Provincial General Services Officer to coordinate with the OIC-
Provincial Accountant to facilitate the completion of all documentary
requirements needed to account all unserviceable and obsolete items; and
b. instruct the Provincial Disposal Committee to cause the immediate disposal of
all unserviceable assets following the guidelines set forth under COA Circular
No. 92-386; and henceforth, submit the complete documents to the Accounting
Office for derecognition of the same in the Province’s books.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to assign personnel for the retrieval of records and other supporting
documents that would establish the details of the transactions related to the
unaccounted beginning balances.

We also reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor
instruct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to:

a. review and walkthrough the process in recording the transactions which
frequently resulted in abnormal accounts’ balances to find solution on the
deficiency noted; and henceforth,
b. conduct periodic analysis of accounts to ensure that the financial transactions
of the Province are properly recorded in the books of accounts.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial Governor direct
the OIC-Provincial Accountant to:

a. take immediate action to reconcile the discrepancies of P9,758,883.24 and
P16,192,558.74 between the recorded balances of Due from/Due to LGUs
accounts of the Province and their reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from LGUs
reflected in the books of the concerned City/Municipalities, and subsequently
draw the necessary journal entries to present the true balance of the said
accounts in the financial statements of the Province;
b. strictly enforce collection and settlement for the receivables and payables
found to be valid; and
c. conduct periodic reconciliation of Provincial accounts with the City/
Municipalities accounting records to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies
between the two records.

We strongly reiterated our previous recommendations that the Provincial
Governor direct the:

a. OIC-Provincial Accountant and the Provincial Treasurer to strictly remit in full
the withheld taxes, premium contributions and loan amortizations to the
respective government agencies in accordance with the cited regulations so as
to avoid incurrence of penalties, surcharges and inconveniences to the
employees concerned;
b. OIC-Provincial Accountant to speed up and prioritize the determination of the
composition of the unaccounted balances amounting to P6,167,494.50, in
coordination with the other concerned government agencies, so that
appropriate disposition on said balances can be undertaken;
c. Provincial Accounting Office to ensure timely recording of transactions in the
books of accounts particularly the remittances made to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG
and PhilHealth to establish the correct balance of Inter-Agency Payables still
to be remitted to the said agencies; and
d. OIC-Provincial Accountant to determine the details of the abnormal balance of
the Due to GSIS (Consolidated Loan) account in the amount of P52,968.15.
Likewise, ensure that the employees’ share contributions and loan repayments
are already deducted from the salaries of the particular employees prior to
their remittance with the concerned agencies, to avoid misapplication of the
local funds.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization and the implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs. Likewise, the Municipal Mayor is advised to require the:
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related PPAs are made strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit the required supporting documents to the Audit Team to be able to arrive at a decision in audit and ensure that claims are duly supported with complete and proper documentation to establish the validity and propriety of transactions before processing of payment; (ii) ensure that accounting forms are properly accomplished and complete with essential information; and (iii) submit the lacking DVs to the Audit Team for the proper verification of the pertinent COVID-19 related expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Designate-GSO and  Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all Inventories and PPEs of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE in accordance with the prescribed format and with complete details, and henceforth ensure that the said physical count are strictly and timely conducted every year;         (b) Designate-GSO to: (i) prepare and maintain complete and updated Property and Stock Cards; and (ii) locate all the unserviceable properties and prepare and submit the duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable items; and (c) Accounting Unit to maintain an updated PPELCs and SLCs for each PPE and inventory accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to: (i) analyze and review the outstanding stale checks and prepare the necessary adjusting JEV to present the correct account balances of the Cash in Bank account; (ii) verify the validity and existence of the payable accounts, and determine whether these are still valid and enforceable; (iii) notify the concerned payees of their outstanding claims, if found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund and make the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the Payable accounts in the Statement of Financial Position; and (iv) cause the return of the unremitted long outstanding balances to the respective Source Agencies for proper presentation of the Other Payables account; and
Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees one month before the check becomes stale, to inform all payees holding the staled checks to return the same for cancellation and issuance of check replacements, and henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid the accumulation of stale checks. Cancelled stale checks shall be reported and attached to the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) at the period of cancellation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) institute appropriate legal actions/charges against the contractor who abandoned the projects amounting to P4,800,000.00; and (ii) make representations with the source agency for the possible reprogramming of the unexpended balances to other PPAs.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:
Municipal Engineer to strictly monitor the project implementations and ensure that projects are implemented and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, compute and impose liquidated damages (LD) therein; and henceforth ensure that projects funded out grants from NGAs are completed in accordance with the approved plans within the target date of completion;
Municipal Accountant to (i) impose and issue order of payment for LDs as determined and recommended by the Municipal Engineer; and (ii) send demand letter to the concerned contractor for the refund of the unrecouped advances for the abandoned projects; and
Municipal Treasurer to cause the return of the unexpended balances of the Due to NGAs totaling P214,012.69 to the respective Source Agencies for the proper presentation of Due to NGAs account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRM Council:                    (a) prepare the LDRRMFIP for the full amount of Continuing Appropriations under the LDRRMF; (b) ensure the optimal and timely utilization of the MDRRMF, including the unexpended balances from previous years, specially, during times of calamities to enhance the Municipality’s resilience and capacity to reduce vulnerabilities when exposed to hazards and calamities; and (c) refrain from utilizing the fund for purposes outside the mandate of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: (a) instruct the MSWMB and MESWMO Designate to hasten the integration of additional required revisions by the DENR-EMB to the SWMP for the eventual submission to DENR-EMB and approval of the NSWMC; and (b) henceforth, hasten the implementation of all Solid Waste Management-related programs/project/activities to enforce proper sorting, segregation, composting, recycling and disposal of waste materials.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to develop an effective and efficient strategy that would expedite the procurement and implementation of the identified development projects for the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits from said development projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (i) strictly observe the prescribed timelines for GAD Planning and Budgeting, as provided in the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016, to facilitate timely preparation and submission of the GPB to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and the subsequent review and endorsement by the DILG Provincial; and         (ii) ensure the accuracy of amounts indicated in the GPB and GAD AR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to monitor and remit promptly the amounts Due to BIR and adhere strictly to the provisions of Sections 4.114(c) and 2.58(a)(2) of BIR Revenue Regulations 2-98, as amended by Section 5 of Revenue Regulations 17-2003, to avoid incurrence of interests and/or penalties for delayed remittance.

We recommended for the settlement of the audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA). The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort in
reconstructing the SLs of the former Municipal Treasurer pertaining to her cash
accountability depending on the availability of reliable records in
accordance with Sections 111(1) and 114(2) of PD No. 1445 and submit the
same to the Audit Team for verification and audit.

We also recommended that the Management demand from the former
Municipal Treasurer to a) reconcile her cashbooks with accounting records;
b) surrender the same to the Municipal Accountant in compliance with Section
181(e) of GAAM, Vol. 1; and c) thereafter submit to the Audit Team the report
of her accountability and settle the remaining balance of such accountability
pursuant to Section 80 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal
Treasurer, the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned to make
representation with the bank to furnish the Municipality with copies of debit and
credit memos, and other supporting documents to facilitate recording of reconciling
items in the books of accounts for the fair presentation of the CIB-LCCA account in
the financial statements.

Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant should immediately record all valid
reconciling items after the preparation of the BRS pursuant to Section 3.3 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 to forestall the accumulation of
unadjusted reconciling items.

We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer a) monitor long
outstanding checks on a regular basis and notify the payees concerned to return the
stale checks for the issuance of replacement checks in accordance with Section
83(b)(4) of the LTOM; and b) regularly reconcile the cashbooks with the accounting
records so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and
corrected, in compliance with Section 63(F) of the LTOM. The Municipal
Accountant should also make the necessary adjustments in the books for the long
outstanding/stale checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record adjustment in  the
books  of  accounts  to  reflect  the  correct  balances  of  RPT  and   SET
receivables and deferred income from said taxes based on the certified  list
of taxpayers showing their cumulative taxes due and collectible provided  by
the OIC-Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor.

We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer ensure  that  the
RPT Account Registers or Taxpayer’s index card  are  updated  and  regularly
furnish the Municipal Accountant a certified list of taxpayers  showing  the
amount due and collectible for the year at the  beginning  of  the  year  to
serve as basis in establishing the  accurate  RPT  and  SET  Receivables  in
compliance with Sections 19(b) and 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor (a) thru,
the Municipal Accountant, regularly serve written notice to AOs demanding
settlement of their outstanding cash advances; (b) consider withholding the
salaries of AOs still connected with the Municipality who refuse to settle
their accounts without justifiable reason; and (c) institute the necessary
legal action against those who failed to settle their accountabilities, if
warranted.

The Municipal Accountant exert efforts to locate documents and previous
records to facilitate reconciliation and/or determination of the causes of the
unreconciled cash advance balances and thereafter effect the necessary
adjustment in the books of accounts, as appropriate.

In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant must
ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 are strictly observed in
the granting and liquidation of cash advances and see that all cash advances
are fully liquidated at the end of the year.

We also advised the Municipal Mayor to file a  request  for  authority  from
COA to write-off dormant unliquidated cash advances pursuant to the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the AO  concerned  provide  justification  on  the
deficiencies noted in the grant of cash advance  intended  for  confidential
expenses. Moreover, the  incumbent  Municipal  Mayor  should  ensure  strict
compliance with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND  Joint  Circular  No.
2015-01  on  the  future  entitlement,  release,  use   and   reporting   of
confidential funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in pursuance of the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005, (i) conduct regular and periodic
verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of  fund  transfers;
(ii) determine the source and implementing government entities concerned;
and (iii)  reconcile  the  balances  between  the  source  and  implementing
government entities concerned and effect adjustment in the books by
providing separate SL per agency and purpose of the fund transfer in
compliance  with  Section  111(1)  of  PD  No.  1445,  and  segregating  the
unsupported,  undocumented  and   unidentified   balances.   The   Municipal
Accountant should also prepare aging of dormant inter-agency fund transfers
on a quarterly basis to support the request for write-off.

Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor is  advised  to  file  a  request  for
authority  from  COA,  through  this  Office,  to  write-off  dormant   fund
transfers duly supported with the required documents, to facilitate the
cleansing of the books for the fair presentation of accounts in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the
General Services Office, submit to the Audit Team the duly approved PIP
containing, at the least, the specific assignments/duties of the members, the
cut-off date, and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the
inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the
physical inventory.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor monitor compliance by the
Inventory Committee, the GSO, and the Municipal Accountant with the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the procedural guidelines for
the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances.

After the cut-off date set by the Inventory Committee in the PIP, the
Municipal Accountant and the GSO should ensure that the proper accounting
and reporting procedures for all acquisition/receipt/issue/transfer/disposal of
PPE pursuant to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on property and
supply management are strictly followed. Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor
should likewise ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually and
