reports thereon are submitted accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
Municipal Engineer and the GSO, reconcile the local  road  inventory  report
and road map with those recorded in the RPI and prepare  the  journal  entry
voucher to update the Road  Networks  account  in  the  books  of  accounts.
Thereafter, the  Municipal  Accountant  and  the  GSO  should  maintain  the
relevant records required by COA Circular No. 2015-008.

The Municipal Mayor should ensure that the  physical  count  of  local
road networks is undertaken annually, and the reports thereon are  submitted
to the Audit Team and the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31  of
each year.

We recommended that a) the GSO and  the  BAC  Secretariat  immediately
submit the contract documents and POs for CYs 2020  to  2021  to  the  Audit
Team for the conduct of auditorial, legal and technical review; and  b)  the
Municipal Mayor enjoin the personnel concerned to strictly comply  with  the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the timely submission  of  copies
of government contracts and POs to the Audit Team.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
1.11Require the Inventory Committee to continue the physical inventory in order to identify items included in the FS and PPELC but not listed in the RPCPPE. Such then, the Property and Accounting Unit can proceed to the reconciliation process between PCs and PPELCs maintained by the MTO and Accounting Office, respectively. Such then the reconciled balances of PPE accounts be considered as the correct balance of the Municipality’s PPEs.
1.12  Direct the MTO/Property Unit to prepare/finalize the PCs to establish complete records for reconciliation between the MTO and Accounting Unit.

Recommendation:
We recommended the LCE to:
2.8 Create and Inventory Committee with adequate number of members and representatives from the Accounting and Property Division/Unit;
2.9 Instruct the Inventory Committee to:
2.9.1 Prioritize the completion of physical count of PPEs; and
2.9.2 Prepare the RPCPPE, reconcile the same with the Property Division/Unit and Accounting Unit records
2.10 Instruct the Accounting Unit to prepare the necessary adjusting entries, if any, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management to:
4.12 Consider requiring all department heads, as end-user for the inventories, to conduct physical count on a semestral basis using the RPCI and submit the same to the MTO for validation and confirmation of the reported stocks on hand.
4.13 Require all end-users/department heads to maintain stock cards for all stocks including donated goods and regularly update, monitor, balance and reconcile the stock cards against stocks on hand;
4.14Require all end-users/departments and MTO to prepare and timely submit to the Accounting Office the RIS and RSMI for all inventories issued including donated goods issued during the year.
4.15 MAO to effect necessary adjustments to correct inventory balances after the conduct thereof and reconciliation between his ledger cards and MTOs stock cards.

Recommendation:
5.6 We recommended the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify and record payables to its appropriate account in order to present fairly the Due to Officer and Employees as well as Other Payable account in the financial statements.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct:
6.23 The Inventory Committee to assign a unique Property Number for each PPE item following the required code format in the numbering system as provided in Section 5 of the Circular.
6.24 The Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit the same to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities in compliance with the pertinent provisions of Section 5 of the Circular.
6.25 Each office of the LGU to fill up the Inventory Count Form and submit the same to the Property Unit for verification. The form will then be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in the preparation of its PIP and will be considered as part of their inventory working paper.
6.26 Moreover, the Inventory Committee to record/document the physical count daily using the ICF per PPE sub-major account group and will be the basis in the preparation of RPCPPE after the physical count.
6.27 The Property Unit to prepare a List of PPEs Found at Station (Annex B of the Circular) and List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs (Annex C of the Circular) during physical inventory and submit these lists to the Accounting Unit for recording in the books of accounts of the LGU to establish the existence and proper accountability of the PPE accounts.
6.28 The LCE to ensure that the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE as provided in COA Circular 2020-006 are strictly observed to facilitate the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances.

Recommendation:
7.6 We recommended that the Management to instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer with the coordination of the Municipal Engineer to fast track the construction of the evacuation center and ensure that such constructions are based on the standards set by the NDRRMC. If funds are inadequate periodically, consider the preparation of the entire project’s program of works (POW) but to be implemented on a phase-by-phase basis.

Recommendation:
8.7 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Officer to fast track the implementation of PPAs specified in the LDRRMFIP through the preparation of a Purchase Request for the items/projects/activities listed in the said plan to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF for the benefits of the constituents.
8.8 Relative to the issue of lack of manpower, the Municipality could tap the services of other regular employees within the agency that can support and help the implementation of related PPAs.

Recommendation:
9.4 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the MDRRMO to immediately coordinate with personnel of MAO regarding the difference of                         P836,969.05. Any result thereof, can be a subject for a new revised budgetary program under the Special Trust Fund and be incorporated in the Revised LDRRMFIP.

Recommendation:
11.4 We recommended to the Management the following:
11.4.1 To establish responsibility center for GFPS under the Office of the Mayor.
11.4.2  The Municipal Accountant to establish a separate RC for GAD which shall maintain a subsidiary ledger (SL) for GAD for each account and may assign SL code to record all GAD expenses in order to facilitate GAD related reports.
11. 4.3 The Municipal Budget Officer to provide RC code in the Budget System as GAD related reports are also to be generated therefrom.

We recommended and the GSO designate/Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
a. cause the accomplishment of transfer documents of properties
donated to concerned agencies and submit copy thereof to the
accounting office for derecognition in the books.
b. cancel the Property Acknowledgement Receipt and issue Inventory
Custodian Slip to inventories/properties costing below the ₱15,000.00
threshold.

We further recommended that the accounting office effect the Proposed
Adjusting Journal Entries as shown in Annex E to correct balances of
affected accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a. the Municipal Treasurer initiate the process of disposing the
unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines under
NBC 425 dated January 28, 1992 to avoid further deterioration.
b. the Municipal Accountant record the eventual disposal of
unserviceable properties to reflect the actual properties owned which
are essential or useful to the LGU.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the provision of
Property Number and other vital information on all government properties
in accordance with COA Circular 80-124 dated June 18, 1980 and COA
Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended and Management agreed that the LDRRMO, in
coordination with the LDRRMC, strictly comply with the provisions of
COA Circular No. 2012-002 in the preparation of LDRRMF Investment
Plan ensuring that the projects and activities listed therein consist of the
current and prior years’ LDRRMF and to incorporate the same plan in the
AIP of the Municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed that the LDRRMO prepare
and submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF
using the prescribed format in the COA-Circular 2012-002 dated
September 12, 2012 with the utilization portion in accord with the PPAs
indicated in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended and Management agreed to support transactions with
complete documentation and to require the schools concerned to submit
liquidation documents such as: pictures before and after the construction
and statement of work accomplished, etc.

We recommended and Management agreed to adopt policies and internal
controls to regulate loan accommodations and other payroll deductions to
ensure strict compliance with the minimum monthly net take home pay of
₱5,000.00 pursuant to Section 49 of the General Provisions of RA No.
11518.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant analyze the Receivables accounts, Due from NGAs, Due from GOCCs, Due from NGOs/POs, and Other Receivables amounting to P48,237.99, P2,161,652.37, P990.92 and P12,854.05, respectively,   which has been dormant for 13 years and make the necessary verification against previous financial reports and schedules to determine the details of these accounts and confirm their balances, if warranted.  Otherwise, submit request of authority to COA for write off the accounts following the procedures set under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert the Due to Officers and  Employees and Notes Payable which remained dormant for more than ten years and no actual claim has been filed in the amount of P4,786.47 and P1,096.48, respectively, pursuant to Section 98 of PD 1445.

We recommended that Management:
Reclassify those cash advances granted to those officers and employees who are already dead, resigned, no longer connected with the LGU and those whereabouts are not known to “Other Receivables” account in the total amount of P4,172,123.84;
Require the Municipal Accountant issue demand letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and demand the immediate settlement thereof; and
Require for write-off of dormant accounts where collection is nil following the procedures set under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Inventory Team/Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of property, plant and equipment in order to finalize the preparation of the RPCPPE pursuant to Section 66 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System; and
Hire job order personnel, if necessary, to assist in the conduct of physical count of PPE for a speedy accomplishment and submission of the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose the related party transaction in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 20 paragraph 27 to ensure transparency and for the users to be able to fully assess the financial position and performance of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to record the reversion of stale checks in the General Fund amounting to P54,545.00 pursuant to Section 59, Chapter  3 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer religiously reconcile the recorded amounts reflected in her cashbook to avoid the possibility of occurrence of shortage or overage in compliance with Section 6.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Field Collectors to remit their collections daily to the Liquidating Officer, who will prepare the Report of Collections and submit the same to the Municipal Treasurer;
Require the Liquidating Officer to not issue new accountable forms to Field Collectors without remitting the previous accountable forms issued to avoid accumulation of collections in their hands; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the collections daily and intact to avoid possibility of theft, mishandling and misuse of the amount collected.
We recommended that the Liquidating Officer certify as to the correctness and completeness of the recorded transactions at month-end after the last entry in her cashbook.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer include checks in the preparation of the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms, pursuant to Sections 51, Chapter 2 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that the Management increase the fidelity bond of the Liquidating Officer and ensure that her bond is sufficient to cover her accountabilities in compliance with Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009. in the event that there is a loss and in order to comply with Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009.

We recommended that the Management strengthen and improve the existing controls on the disbursement of funds by adopting the ADA disbursement system wherein payment of salaries and wages are directly deposited to the respective ATM bank account of personnel, thus eliminating the risk of loss through theft or robbery and ultimately provide safeguard of funds pursuant to Section 2 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit the lacking documents as proof that they actually conducted the prescribed procedures on negotiated procurement; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of the RA 9184 and its IRR for all its procurement and the required transparency of government procurements in order to avoid sanctions and penalties and disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Management submit the lacking documents to support the payment of  the project, Improvement/Rehabilitation of COVID-19 Quarantine Facility, in the amount of P118,521.82 and ensure that all payments to contractors for the implementation of infrastructure projects be supported with the documentary requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009 -001 dated February 12, 2009 to ensure propriety and validity of the transaction.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC to submit justification in writing for allowing the documents submitted in the bid such as the Joint Venture Agreement containing a scanned signature of Ms. Grace A. Jardin and the Omnibus Sworn Statement not accompanied by duly notarized SPA, otherwise it shall be suspended in audit and strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA 9184.
We recommended that Management submit justification in writing why payments were made to M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services based on the self-serving documents issued by the latter instead of a duly executed joint agreement between R.S. Jardin & Associates and M Pulido Engineering & Urban Planning Services, otherwise all payments made thereof shall be suspended in audit and strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 2 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management ensure that all contract agreements be supported with complete documentary requirements prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012 – 001 dated June 14, 2012. Further, we recommend that the lacking documents be submitted immediately to facilitate the review of the procurement.

We recommended that the BAC send invitation to COA and two observers at least five (5) calendar days before the date of procurement stage/activity and address the invitations to other two observers with particular identity in compliance with Section 13 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. Moreover, secure proof of receipt of the written invitations by the concerned observers.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to make a correcting entry on those procured properties that were recorded as outright expenses in the amount of P479,175.00 and record the same in the corresponding PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Management require:
The Municipal Accountant record all inventories as asset upon delivery and acceptance; and
The Supply Officer to conduct the periodic physical count of supplies and materials every semester and prepare and submit the corresponding Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) pursuant to Section 124, Chapter 7 of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Development Council, the MPDC Office and the Budget Office to comply with the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01, that all development projects to be funded under the 20% DF shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU, and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE in the amount of P49,874.05 and P790,794.92, respectively, to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and reconcile the difference of P157.00 between the amount per Notes to Financial Statements and amount presented in the financial statements to ensure correct information and amounts presented thereof.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Health
Officer (MHO) to: (i) strengthen its campaign and widen the scope of its information drive to generate more demand for the vaccine and continue its vaccination roll out until the desired local herd immunity is attained; (ii) render a monthly report to the Accounting Office on the receipt, issuance and balances of vaccines and other inventories directly received from outside sources; and (b) Municipal Accountant in coordination with the MHO to determine and recognize in the books the full cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year end and to henceforth record future receipts and issuances of the same.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Accountable Officer to return immediately the remaining misappropriated funds in the amount of ₱383,166.60 without prejudice to the filing of appropriate charges against the said person; (b) MT to (i) monitor the enforcement of internal control procedures on collections as set forth in various regulations emanating from within the LGU itself and oversight bodies; and (ii) review and revisit the LTOM and attend training and seminars in relation to treasury operations.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct (a) the Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties owned by the Municipality; and (b) the Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Property Cards and PPE Ledgers Cards, respectively.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) ensure that all PPAs included in the 20% Development Fund are fully implemented in accordance with Section 5.0 of the DILG-DBM Joint Management Circular No. 2017-01; (b) Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to regularly monitor the status of project implementation so that necessary interventions may be introduced at the earliest time.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to continue the satisfactory compliance with the existing regulation on the appropriation and utilization of the 5% LDRRMF with adequate consideration of factors that would justify the keeping of standby funds.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) Municipal Engineer and BAC to strictly observe the guidelines provided in the Manual of Procedures for the Procurement of Infrastructure Projects specifically in the preparation of ABC of future projects, and (b) Municipal Engineer and BAC explain why they should not refund the ₱105,916.50 overstatement of the project cost.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the GAD Focal Person to continue its compliance in its mandate of promoting and providing a gender-responsive environment in the local government.
We recommended the LCE agreed to continue its satisfactory observance of BIR regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes.

We commended Management for its sustained satisfactory observance of the GSIS Act of 1997, and we recommended and the LCE agreed to continue the withholding and timely remittance of all premium contributions and loan amortizations of its employee members as well as the government’s share to the fund.

We commended Management for its sustained satisfactory observance of the National Health Insurance Act, and advised, for which the LCE concurred, to continue the withholding and timely remittance of all PHIC contributions of its employee members.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to continue adhering to the requirement of R.A. No. 9679 or the Home Development Mutual Fund Law of 2009.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. prepare and maintain PPELC for the properties recorded under the Special Education Fund; b. prepare a lapsing schedule to compute for the depreciation expenses of all the recorded PPE and draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the computed amount of depreciation; c. draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the misclassified PPE according to its classification, specification and nature; d. draw Journal Entry Voucher to drop the small value property items from the books by debiting Prior Years' Adjustment and Accumulated Depreciation of the respective PPE, and by crediting the respective PPE accounts. Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require an annual physical inventory of all the properties of the Municipality to be conducted by a committee consisting of representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, Municipal GSO or Municipal Treasurer and Supply Accountable Officer of the department or office concerned.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant already prepared the PPELC for the properties recorded under the Special Education Fund.
None
JEV was already prepared to correct the misclassified PPE according to its classification, specification and nature.
None
None
observations.reason:
The Municipality diverted their attention on the COVID-19 Pandemic.

The Municipality diverted their attention on the COVID-19 Pandemic.
The Municipality diverted their attention on the COVID-19 Pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify the amount due within twelve months after the reporting date as part of the current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant classified the amount due within twelve months after reporting date as part of the current liabilities in the submitted CY 2021 Statement of Financial Position.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies and materials as inventory following the perpetual inventory method and recognize as expense when it is already consumed or distributed. Also, maintain a Supply Ledger Card (SLC) for each inventory item; b. General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare and submit Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR).
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant recorded all the purchases of supplies and materials as inventory following the perpetual inventory method and recognize as expense when it is already consumed or distributed. Also, maintain a Supply Ledger Card (SLC) for each inventory item.
None
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant explained that he was not able to maintain Supply Ledger Card (SLC) for each inventory item due to numerous workload.
The Municipality cannot maintain stock cards and properly stock the inventories due to lack of storage room.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the amount and details of the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the Notes to the Financial Statements pursuant to Sections 5.1.12 and 5.1.16 of the COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant disclosed the amount and details of the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the submitted Notes to the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to submit all the lacking documents to the Auditor immediately to support and justify the payments for procured infrastructure projects and supplies and materials, for review and evaluation of the Audit Team; and b. Municipal Health Officer to ascertain that the requisition for medicines conform with the Philippine National Drug Formulary (PNDF) Volume I, prior to procurement, and strictly require from the suppliers the Certificate of Product Registration (CPR) from the Bureau of Food and Drugs (BFAD) and the General Services Officer to submit the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to the COA within twenty-four (24) hours from acceptance of the items delivered and strictly comply with the COA Circular Nos. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has recovered and had submitted the lacking documents. The payment for medicines were all supported with Certificate of Product Registration (CPR) from the Bureau of Food and Drugs (BFAD) and submitted its Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to the COA within twenty-four (24) hours from acceptance of the items delivered and strictly comply with the COA Circular Nos. 92-386.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to maintain an Inventory of all Local Roads and prepare Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System; b. General Services Officer to prepare Report on the Local Road Network System and submit the Report on the Local Road Network at the end of the accounting period; and c. Municipal Accountant to prepare a lapsing schedule to compute for the depreciation expenses of all the recorded Road Network Components and draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the computed amount of depreciation.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality created an Inventory Committee and conducted initial inventory of local roads.
None
None
observations.reason:
The preparation of Report on the Road Network System was not prepared because the inventory of local roads was not completed.
Inadequate information on road networks.
Inadequate information on road networks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMO to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF duly reconciled with the accounting records and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant on or before the 15th day after the end of each month as required under Section 5.1.5 of the COA Circular No. 2012-002 and the Inventory List of all procured, donated relief goods, supplies, materials and Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment at the end of the year in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-02.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO submitted the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF duly reconciled with the accounting records and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory List of all procured, donated relief goods, supplies, materials and Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment to the COA office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer and all concerned department heads to meticulously and judiciously prepare the Annual Procurement Plan in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and ensure that all necessary information required in the APP and its amendments, if any, are reflected therein to serve as a planning and control tool for efficient procurement.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer revised their APP format and all concerned included all the required details.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer and BAC Secretariat to: a. prepare the monthly utilization reports of BGCM and post the same in LGU's website and in at least three conspicuous public places in the locality at the end of every month; and b. comply with the posting in the GPPB online portal website as required by the GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 and GPPB Resolution No. 19-2020.
observations.managementAction: The BGCM utilization reports and payments for CY 2021 were prepared and posted in the LGU website and the GPPB online portal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRM Officer to prepare the 1) Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances for In-Kind Donations; 2) Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations; 3) Report on the Receipt and Distribution/Issuance of Donated Relief Goods; and 4) Report on the Receipt and Distribution/Issuance of Donated PPE; and submit the same to the NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy furnished the Audit Team within ten (10) working days after the end of each quarter pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO submitted all the required quarterly reports to the COA office within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to specifically allocate one percent (1%) of Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) for the LCPC fund and the members of the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children and maximize utilization of the allocated LCPC Fund to identify PPAs provided for in DILG MC No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC.
observations.managementAction: The allocated one percent (1%) of Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) for the LCPC fund in the CY 2021 budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit all the lacking supporting documents and justifications for allowing the disbursement and to ensure that all disbursement vouchers are supported by complete documentation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant immediately submitted all the lacking supporting documents. The Municipal Accountant undertake to see to it that all disbursement vouchers are supported by complete documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person and all other concerned officials to furnish the Audit Team a copy of the GAD Accomplishment Report for proper evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person submitted the copy of the GAD Accomplishment Report to the COA office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to adjust the book reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRSs) and ensure that all reconciling items are immediately recorded/adjusted in the Province's books.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Provincial Accountant secured copies of Credit Memos from the Province’s depository banks and prepared the identified reconciling items in the BRSs. The OIC-Provincial Accountant ensured that the reconciling items in the BRS are immediately recorded/adjusted in the Province’s books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to complete the inventory count of PPE accounts and submit the inventory report to the Audit Team summarized according to type of PPE and properly accomplished in all particulars, in accordance with Section 266 of the GAM for LGUs; b. Property Officer to ensure Property Cards are well-maintained, complete and updated and issue PARs; c. OIC-Provincial Accountant to trace back prior years’ acquisition of PPE to determine complete details so depreciation could be computed; and d. Provincial officials concerned to speed up transfer of titles of forfeited real estate properties and record their costs in the books.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee prepared and submitted an inventory report to the Audit Team but it was incomplete. Property Cards are now being maintained by the Property Officer with corresponding PAR and/or Inventory Custodian Slip. The OIC-Provincial Accountant established details of some unaccounted properties and provided depreciation where validated. Six forfeited properties were registered in the name of the Provincial Government and recorded in the books for CY 2021. The Province will pursue dropping claims over untied up properties and those not suitable for development. The PGSO prepared and submitted an updated Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and related documents to the Accounting Office for proper action.
observations.reason: Lack of available data for inventory accounts that are more than ten years outstanding in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. prepare the necessary journal entry to adjust depreciation for PPE acquired in the current year; b. assign personnel to trace back prior years’ acquisition of PPE to have complete details so depreciation could be computed; and c. compute the new lapsing schedule for PPE to update accumulated depreciation.
observations.managementAction: Adjustment on the understated depreciation on PPE acquired in the current year was made by the OIC-Provincial Accountant as validated by the Audit Team (JEV Nos. GJ-100-21-03-00097, 00098, GJ-200-21-03-0037 and 0038 dated March 31, 2021). Personnel was assigned to trace back prior years’ acquisition of PPE and retrieval of fixed asset records is in process. A new lapsing schedule for PPE was prepared to update accumulated depreciation as of December 31, 2020.
observations.reason: Lack of available data for acquisitions made prior to year 2002.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial Treasurer and OIC-Provincial Accountant to exert effort to locate the related supporting documents and investigate to determine the complete details of the mentioned investments of the Province for proper valuation and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Formal representations and communications with LLDA were made. It was discovered that part of the unidentified investments pertains to the Province's investment in LLDA recorded in the 2001 General Fund Trial Balance but not carried over; tracing and evaluation of records is ongoing and adjustments will be made where supported.
observations.reason: Necessary adjustments to be made are under evaluation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to conduct semestral physical count of inventories and submit RPCI in proper form summarized according to type of inventory; b. Provincial Accounting Office to maintain complete and updated supplies ledger cards and trace back the unaccounted balances totaling P31,354,647.24 and non-moving accountable forms P435,000.00; and c. OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Provincial Accountant to explain and account the discrepancies noted in balances reported in SLC, RPCI and stock cards.
observations.managementAction: The RPCIs for the two semesters were submitted on November 18, 2021 and February 9, 2022 due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The Inventory Committee conducted counts and submitted reports but noted deficiencies. The Provincial Accounting Office is tracing back unaccounted balances and has reduced unaccounted inventory balances from prior levels; adjustments and reconciliations are ongoing. The PGSO and Accounting Office are coordinating to address reconciling items.
observations.reason: Lack of available data; ongoing retrieval and reconciliation of prior records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. exert effort in retrieval of records/documents that would establish the details of Other Payables account; b. strictly comply with the provisions of IPSAS 1 for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements; and c. revert long outstanding accounts payable without valid claim to Government Equity where appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Transactions and documentation were traced where available; adjustments were effected where validated. The current portion of Other Payables was reclassified and presented under Current Liabilities in the financial statements as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. ensure accounting adjustments are valid, properly documented and the details secured to support adjustments in Journal Vouchers; b. review and walkthrough recording processes that frequently result in abnormal balances to find solutions; and c. conduct periodic analysis of accounts to ensure proper recording.
observations.managementAction: Evaluation of the identified abnormal balances is ongoing. Coordination with concerned departments was undertaken. Some adjustments were made where support was found. Periodic analysis and reconciliations are being implemented, and tracing back of unaccounted balances is in process.
observations.reason: Limited working hours and existing old files still being organized; difficulty retrieving detailed prior year records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. make formal representations to the Source Agencies to reconcile differences by furnishing them the received copy of the Fund Utilization Reports (FURs) and supporting documents for completed projects and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries for reconciling items; and b. Provincial Treasurer to remit unused balances to the Bureau of the Treasury and seek authority for reversion if necessary.
observations.managementAction: Formal representations were made to Source Agencies and FURs were submitted quarterly. The unutilized balance of P462,012.50 was returned to the BTr per JEV No. RCI-300-21-05-0190 and OR No. 7062358. Unaccounted balances of P146,666.03 were reverted to Government Equity per JEVs GJ-300-21-04-0019 and 00141 dated April 19, 2021. Journal Entry Vouchers to reclassify LGSF balances were drawn and subsequent collections are being recorded properly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. coordinate with City/Municipal Accountants to reconcile discrepancies and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries; b. strictly enforce collection and settlement for valid receivables and payables; and c. conduct periodic reconciliation of Provincial accounts with City/Municipalities.
observations.managementAction: Communication and coordination with concerned LGUs were made via letters, emails and other methods to reconcile the accounts. Evaluations and reconciliations are ongoing; adjustments were made for identified reconciling items.
observations.reason: Lack of available prior period data and documents necessary for full reconciliation; ongoing reconciliation efforts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant and the Provincial Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records to ensure accuracy in recording. Also, effect necessary adjustments to avoid accumulation of discrepancies.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of Cash in Treasury and Subsidiary Ledger of the Accounting Office was made. Reconciliation reports of Cash in Bank and subsidiary ledgers were prepared and reconciled; identified reconciling items were coordinated for adjustment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to analyze and identify Accounts Payable without valid documentation and those outstanding for two years or more; request SP authority for reversion to Government Equity where appropriate and prepare the necessary JEV to effect reversion.
observations.managementAction: Coordination was made and SP Resolution No. 94, s. 2021 was issued approving the reversion of some Accounts Payable. Reversion of long outstanding Accounts Payable to Government Equity under GF amounting to P23,956,194.67 was made by the OIC-Provincial Accountant per JEV No. GJ-100-21-04-00142 dated April 19, 2021.
observations.reason: Outstanding balances are related to Scholarship Programs which take more than two years before claims can be made; some reversion actions completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to maintain individual subsidiary ledgers per employee for withholding taxes and contributions to facilitate monitoring and reconciliation with the concerned agencies.
observations.managementAction: A program/system to record and monitor individual employee subsidiary ledgers was created by the MIS Office and data encoding of employees’ details into the system was prepared. Coordination with Accounting and Treasury was done to facilitate monitoring and reconciliation.
observations.reason: Incompatibility with systems used by stakeholders and ongoing data encoding; implementation in process.

observations.recommendation: We strongly reiterated that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. OIC-Provincial Accountant and Provincial Treasurer to remit in full the withheld taxes, premium contributions and loan amortizations to the respective agencies to avoid penalties; b. OIC-Provincial Accountant to prioritize determination of the composition of the unaccounted balances amounting to P6,167,494.50; c. Provincial Accounting Office to ensure timely recording of remittances; and d. OIC-Provincial Accountant to determine details of the abnormal balance of Due to GSIS (Consolidated Loan) amounting to P24,222.83 and ensure deductions from salaries prior to remittance.
observations.managementAction: The unremitted balance of Due to BIR as of December 31, 2020 was partly settled through a compromise agreement amounting to P4,283,235.98 per Check No. 77963477 dated February 24, 2022. The Province has undertaken actions to reconcile and remit outstanding contributions and taxes; coordination with concerned agencies is ongoing.
observations.reason: Inconsistencies and historical unremitted balances require tracing and verification; partial settlements made and reconciliation ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the concerned member of the Provincial Finance Committee in coordination with the OIC-Provincial Accountant to strictly comply with posting and reporting requirements pursuant to Section 6 of DBM LBC No. 135; and direct the OIC-Provincial Planning and Development Coordinator to immediately secure the PAMB and ECC from DENR to facilitate implementation of suspended projects and conduct project site inspection, securing all necessary permits to avoid delays.
observations.managementAction: Members of the Local Finance Committee and OIC-Provincial Accountant admitted delayed posting; posting was done in three conspicuous places and on the LGU website on February 16, 2022. Documents related to Fund Utilization Reports were furnished to the Audit Team. Lacking DENR documentary requirements were secured and resume orders for suspended projects were made by the Provincial Engineering Office on January 7 and February 10, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to: a. follow-up concerned Health Facilities for immediate submission of Liquidation Reports with supporting documents and submit to the Audit Team for post-audit; b. require RHU Municipality of Rodriguez and Montalban Infirmary to submit lacking documents to substantiate SRA grants; and c. ensure strict compliance with submission of financial and accomplishment reports to DOH-CHD pursuant to DBM-DOH JC No. 2, s. 2021.
observations.managementAction: The Province took actions; final Fund Utilization Report was received by CHD CALABARZON on May 12, 2022. The Province followed up health facilities for liquidation documents and required RHUs and infirmary to submit supporting payroll schedules and documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. Provincial Engineer to closely monitor and pursue timely implementation of priority projects under 20% DF; b. Bids and Awards Committee to strictly follow APP time schedule for advertisement, submission, receipt/opening of bids, award and contract signing; and c. OIC-Provincial Planning and Development Officer to conduct efficient planning through monitoring and evaluation to avoid delays and adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The RPBAC validated subject infrastructure projects and committed to strictly adhere to the APP and monitor procurement schedules. The PPDO committed to improve planning and monitoring for subsequent projects funded under the 20% DF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the GFPS-TWG to review the existing GAD Plan and Budget and consider realigning/recalibrating PPAs which cannot be implemented during pandemic to ensure gender-responsive interventions to COVID-19 and the new normal. Also, ensure effective and efficient implementation of GAD PPAs and judicious utilization of the GAD budget.
observations.managementAction: Management acknowledged lapses and committed to conduct a comprehensive re-orientation on GAD concepts focusing on planning and budgeting for GFPS-TWG members to enhance capacity for implementation and review of GAD PPAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the GFPS-TWG to quantify the targets and accomplishments relative to all GAD-related activities for easy monitoring and to facilitate evaluation of performance.
observations.managementAction: With guidance from DILG and COA, improvements were made in quantifying indicators and targets in the GPB 2022; Management will continue to improve documentation and implementation of gender mainstreaming.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-PDRRMO to: a. identify specific disaster-related projects/activities to be charged under Continuing Appropriations as approved by PDRRMC and submit to OIC-Provincial Budget Officer for supplemental budget; b. monitor efficient implementation of programmed projects; and c. strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2012-002 on allocation under 70% Mitigation Fund. Also, OIC-Provincial Accountant to reconcile reported Continuing Appropriations - LDRRMF in Notes to Financial Statements with Provincial Budget Office records.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Provincial Budget Officer, OIC-PDRRMO, OIC-Provincial Accountant and OIC-Provincial Administrator acknowledged deficiencies and committed to comply with recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-PDRRMO to monitor implementation of planned PPAs to guarantee public receive derived benefits and ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects to achieve objectives in mitigating disaster impact and enhancing preparedness and response capabilities.
observations.managementAction: The PDRRMO, OIC-Provincial Accountant and OIC-Provincial Administrator acknowledged deficiencies and stated PDRRMO will monitor implementation extent and ensure efficient execution; some unimplemented 2021 programs were intended for disaster response, contingency measures or COVID-19 vaccination program support.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization, implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs and reporting of the status of funds.

Likewise, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (a) BAC to: (i) ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related PPAs are included in the revised AIPs approved by the local sanggunian, and see to it that all procurements are strictly in accordance with the modes of procurement indicated therein; (ii) submit all the originals/authenticated copies of the lacking documents and proper justifications for non-compliance with the provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other COVID-19 related issuances on the procurement of COVID-19-related PPAs to arrive at a decision in audit; and  (iii) henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of the said Revised IRR to establish the validity and regularity of the procurement process and award of contracts/POs; and

(b) Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking disbursement vouchers and other lacking supporting documents for audit, to arrive at a decision in audit;(ii) check the completeness and propriety of supporting documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and other COVID-19 related issuances before processing claims for payments to establish the legality, validity and propriety of the disbursements; (iii) ensure that purchases of relief goods for distribution are recorded under their proper inventory accounts and that the appropriate expense accounts shall be recognized only upon its issuance; and (iv) prepare the necessary JEV for the proper recording of funds received from BGCM for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Lacking DVs were already submitted by the management.

Also, the management has already returned the fund received from BGCM to the Bureau of Treasury on February 9, 2021 per LBP check number 797293.
observations.reason: The Management still failed to fully comply with the audit recommendations hence, similar audit observations had been observed in CY 2021 transactions as discussed in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and Inventories of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE in accordance with the prescribed format and with complete details, and henceforth ensure that said physical count are strictly conducted annually;(b) Accounting Unit to prepare and maintain updated PPE Ledger Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards for each inventory account; and (c) Designate-GSO to: (i) maintain Stock Cards; and (ii) prepare and submit duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable items.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Unit and GSO already started the preparation of SLCs and Property Cards during CY 2021.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee failed to conduct actual physical count of all PPEs and Inventories hence, the RPCPPE was not prepared.

The concerned officials still failed to prepare the RPCPPE in the prescribed form and 
prepare and maintain proper and complete perpetual inventory records such as Stock Cards, PPELCs and SLCs for all the properties of the Municipality, due to the continued disregard of the concerned employees with the audit recommendation thus, reiterated in Part II of this Report.

Also, IIRUP of 103 unserviceable PPEs valued at P20,948,397.10  was still not submitted thus, said PPEs were not disposed of as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: (a) Municipal Accountant to: (i) analyze and review the outstanding staled checks and prepare the necessary JEV for the adjustments to present the correct account balances of the Cash in Bank account; (ii) strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances to ensure that no additional cash advance is granted to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made; (iii) cause the immediate settlement of cash advances to avoid accumulation thereof; and (iv) analyze the unreconciled balances of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, prepare a reconciliation statement therein and make the necessary adjustments in the books for fair and accurate presentation of the account balances in the financial statements; and (b) Municipal Treasurer to: (i) send written notices to the payees one month before the check becomes stale, to inform all payees holding the staled checks to return the same for cancellation and issuance of check replacements, and henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid the accumulation of staled checks. Cancelled staled checks shall be reported and attached to the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) at the period of cancellation; and (ii) withhold the payment of salaries of concerned Officers/Employees, if necessary, to enforce liquidation of their outstanding cash advances.
observations.managementAction:
Out of the unliquidated CA totaling P54,592.07 as at December 31, 2020, P47,879.67 was already liquidated during CY 2021.

The Accounting Unit already reconciled the amount of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds as at CY 2021.

Eleven (11) Stale Checks totaling P266,420.32 were  already replaced by the Municipal Treasurer and claimed by the payees.
observations.reason: Lack of initiative of the concerned Municipal officials to enforce full settlement of outstanding amount due from various employees and private individuals, hence this is reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC and the TWG to ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the Municipality are strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly during the bid evaluation and post-qualification phase, for proper evaluation and assessment of the eligibility as to legal, technical and financial capacities of the prospective bidders.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) BAC to (i) immediately submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that perfected contracts are properly and completely documented before submitting the same to the Audit Team; (ii) invite, in addition to the representative of the COA, at least two (2) qualified observers with knowledge, experience and expertise on the procurement at hand, in all stages of the procurement process, five (5) days before the activity; (iii) post in the PhilGEPS website and in any conspicuous places within the premises of the LGU copies of the Notices of Award, Approved Contracts and Notices to Proceed within the prescribed period; (iv) ensure that the submitted technical and eligibility  requirements were original or certified true copies; (v) submit copies of bid proposals of the other/losing bidders and attendance sheets duly signed by those who attended the pre-procurement  conferences; and (vi) submit perfected contracts, together with all supporting documents, within five (5) working days after its approval; (b) the Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the project implementation and ensure that construction schedules are strictly followed to ascertain that projects are implemented efficiently and effectively in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the contract duration; and (c) Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit the required supporting documents and proper justification for the deficiencies cited to be able to arrive at a decision in audit and henceforth, ensure that claims are duly supported with complete and proper documentation before processing claims for payments to establish the validity and propriety of transactions; and (ii) ensure that accounting forms are properly accomplished and complete with essential information.
observations.managementAction: Several lacking documents in the procurement activities of the Municipality were already submitted by the Municipal Engineering Office to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The Management has yet to submit the remaining lacking documents and proper justifications for the noted deficiencies in the procurement process.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRM Council:                    (a) prepare the LDRRMFIP which shall include Continuing Appropriations under LDRRMF; (b) ensure the optimal utilization of the MDRRMF, including the unexpended balances from previous years, specially, during times of calamities to enhance the Municipality’s resilience and capacity to reduce vulnerabilities when exposed to hazards and calamities; and (c) refrain from utilizing the fund for purposes outside the mandate of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that purchases of relief items, supplies and materials are recorded under the appropriate Inventory accounts and recognize the appropriate expense accounts only upon its issuance. The inventory accounts shall be properly monitored using the Supplies Ledgers Card (SLCs) pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-002.
observations.managementAction: The Management was able to implement the project, procurement of ambulance amounting to P1,499,500.00.
observations.reason: The Management has not yet submitted justification about the utilization of LDRRMF outside its purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: (a) instruct the MSWMB and MESWMO Designate to immediately submit the revised SWMP and coordinate with the DENR to fast track the review of the SWMP for the eventual approval of the National Solid Waste Management Commission; and (b) henceforth hasten the implementation of all Solid Waste Management-related programs/project/activities in order to enforce proper sorting, segregation, composting, recycling and disposal of waste materials.
observations.managementAction: The municipality has distributed MRFs designed to sort recyclable materials and biodegradable waste as part of the Municipal 10-year SWMP.
observations.reason:
The submitted revised SWMP of the Municipality was still not approved by the DENR-EMB.

The Management did not submit plausible explanation for their failure to submit the required reports within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the: (a) MPDC to plan the procurement and implementation of the identified priority projects for the optimal utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits derived from said development projects; and (b) ensure that all PPAs to be included in the expenditure program for 20 percent Development Fund are strictly in compliance with DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.
observations.managementAction: The Management had already implemented priority PPAs of 20 percent Development Fund such as, Rehabilitation of Nutrition Post in 2 GIDA, Installation of Water System in Balete, Purok 7 and Gawad Kalinga Housing Projects Area in Rumbang, Municipal Livelihood Support Fund and Establishment of Children's Park-Municipal Plaza during CY 2021.
observations.reason: The Management still failed to implement several priority PPAs of 20 percent Development Fund such as, Acquisition of Lots for Cemetery, Slaughter House and Solid Waste Management as of December 31, 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic and lack of design plans and the need for additional funding, thus, implementation were put on hold.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to monitor and remit promptly the amount Due to BIR and adhere strictly to the provisions of Sections 4.114(c) and 2.58(a)(2) of BIR Revenue Regulations 2-98, as amended by Section 5 of Revenue Regulations 17-2003, to avoid incurrence of interests and/or penalties for delayed remittance.
observations.managementAction: Out of the Due to BIR account amounting to P1,363,991.44 as at December 31, 2021, P639,159.99 was remitted to BIR during CY 2021.
observations.reason: Taxes withheld from CY 2020 and previous years amounting to P724,831.86 remained unremitted for more than one year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Require the Municipal Treasurer, the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned to make representations with the bank to furnish the Municipality with copies of debit and credit memos to facilitate recording of reconciling items in the books of accounts for the fair presentation of the CIB-LCCA account in the financial statements; b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to record all valid reconciling items immediately after the preparation of the BRS pursuant to Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 to forestall the accumulation of unadjusted reconciling items; c. Cause the submission of the disbursement voucher (DV) or payroll and its supporting documents pertaining to the debit memorandum of ₱293,820.00 for CY 2020 for recording in the books, otherwise the transaction has to be suspended in audit; and d. Thereafter, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adequately support all transactions in the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) with the required attachments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum Order No. 67, series of 2021, directing the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to follow the said audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of accounts the NDs which had become final and executory totaling ₱22,467,600.95 in compliance with Section 22.6 of RRSA.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant recorded in the books of accounts the subject NDs which had become final and executory in CY 2021. The Municipal Mayor also issued Memorandum Order to strictly enforce all COEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconstitute the Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, with at least one member each from the Municipal Accountant’s Office and the General Services Office, and temporarily relieve the members from their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed. The Inventory Committee, in coordination with the General Services Office, should be required to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing, at least, the specific assignments/duties of the members, the cut-off date, and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory, and to be approved by the Municipal Mayor, for submission to the Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a regular employee who will monitor compliance by the Inventory Committee, the OIC-GSO, and the Municipal Accountant in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the procedural guidelines for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances. After the cut-off date set by the Inventory Committee in the PIP, the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO should ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all acquisition/receipt/issue/transfer/disposal of PPE pursuant to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on property and supply management are strictly followed. Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor should ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually and the reports thereon are submitted accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor created an Inventory Committee through the issuance of EO No. 23, series of 2021. The Inventory Committee, in coordination with the General Services Office, submitted partial RPCPPE for CY 2021 to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Not Management failed to prepare and submit the PIP to the Audit Team. The physical count was only partial, and reconciliation of the RPCPPE with relevant records was not yet started. Lack of personnel in the GSO and the Municipal Accountant’s Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. thru, the Municipal Accountant, regularly serve written notice to concerned accountable officers (AOs) to demand settlement of their outstanding cash advances; b. consider withholding the salaries of AOs still connected with the Municipality who refuse to settle their accounts without justifiable reason. The Municipal Accountant exert efforts to locate documents and previous records to facilitate reconciliation and/or determination of the causes of the unreconciled and negative cash advance balances of various AOs and thereafter effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts, as appropriate. In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant must ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 are strictly observed in the granting and liquidation of cash advances and see that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year. We also advised the Municipal Mayor to file a request for authority from COA to write-off dormant unliquidated cash advances pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum Order No. 66, series of 2021. The Municipal Accountant exerted efforts and the negative cash advance balances of various AOs were already adjusted in CY 2021. The Municipality stopped granting additional cash advance to AOs who still have unliquidated balances. Moreover, they were able to reduce the cash advance balance by ₱914,648.87 from the previous year’s balance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the general ledger controlling account with complete and updated subsidiary records to effectively control and monitor any movement in the Land account. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor should review the discrepancies in their records for its reconciliation. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor facilitate the immediate titling of real properties owned by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant recorded SL for the Land account. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Municipal Assessor for the reconciliation of their records.
observations.reason: Not Funds for the immediate titling of real properties were not yet allocated by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. create or reconstitute the Inventory Committee who will conduct inventory of the local roads within the Municipality and submit the corresponding reports to the Audit Team for evaluation and verification; and b. enjoin the concerned Department Heads’ compliance with the requirements of COA and Report Circular No. 2015-008 to facilitate the transfer of the local roads accounts from the RPI to the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor created an Inventory Committee through the issuance of EO No. 23, series of 2021. The Report of the Local Road Network and Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network was submitted to the Audit Team on February 10, 2022.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee was not able to completely segregate the different road components in the report. Management was not able to timely furnish the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with the said reports for reconciliation in the books of accounts and property records.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the: a. Municipal Mayor (i) provide satisfactory explanations on the deficiencies noted in the hiring of consultant whose functions are mere duplication of the functions performed by existing regular plantilla personnel; (ii) assess the need to engage consultancy services and in case there is really a need for consultants, engage only experts in their respective fields that will be selected thru proper procurement process; and (iii) strictly observe the rules and regulations on the hiring of consultants; b. Municipal Budget Officer provide justification for certifying the existence of CY 2020 available appropriation for consulting services although the Executive Order for the appointment of the consultant was signed only on January 8, 2021; c. The BAC Chairman produce the lacking supporting documents on the procurement of consulting services, or a certification that such transaction did not undergo the proper procurement process, and henceforth, strictly abide by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184 and its R-IRR and other relevant laws on the procurement of consulting services; and d. The Municipal Accountant ensure that the DV is supported with complete documentary requirements for consulting services pursuant to CSC-COA-DBM JMC No. 2, series of 1979, and COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing and certifying the DV.
observations.managementAction: Management already cancelled the said claim to avoid the disallowance in audit. The Municipal Budget Officer provided justification for his certification in the Obligation Request. The BAC admitted that the transaction did not undergo proper procurement process. The Municipal Accountant installed measures to ensure the completeness of supporting documents required for consultancy services before certifying the DVs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to: a. comply with the required posting of information and publicity of government PPAs in the municipal premises and at the projects’ sites; b. provide the Audit Team with the list of government PPAs to be implemented at the beginning of the year with the complete information such as: project name; implementing unit; office or division if it is not the agency as a whole; brief description of the PPAs; contractor or supplier, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or approved budget; project duration including start and completion dates; and location; c. inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the projects or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the agency premises and at the project sites; and d. submit monthly monitoring report on the implementation of all Municipality’s PPAs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 and ensure that the cost incurred to date indicated in the report reconcile with the records of the Municipal Accountant.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed both the Municipal Engineer and the MPDC to comply with the said COA Circular through Memorandum Order No. 66, series of 2021. The MPDC and Municipal Engineer coordinated and submitted monitoring reports to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The required report was not yet submitted to the Audit Team. The information in the reports submitted were incomplete and monitoring reports were delayed and incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the: a. Municipal Mayor (i) ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% DF by promptly implementing the priority development projects as reflected in its duly approved local development plans and AIP, in accordance with Section 5 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; (ii) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the projects to maximize the developmental benefits of the intended beneficiaries; and (iii) consider realigning or reprogramming the unutilized/unimplemented PPAs to other viable development projects; b. Municipal Engineer (i) require the project engineer, officers and employees concerned to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs under their responsibility; (ii) assist the MPDC in identifying those projects not yet completed or started subject for possible realignment or reprogramming; (iii) provide complete information on the status of the projects funded by the 20% DF together with all the necessary details; and (iv) provide satisfactory explanation for the delayed or non-implementation of PPAs despite the considerable lapse of time; and c. MPDC (i) ensure close coordination with the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant for the proper monitoring of project implementation and the provision of all the necessary information in the Status Report of Government PPAs before submission to the Audit Team; (ii) conduct intensive review of the continuing projects funded under the 20% DF to determine whether the PPAs are still part of the vital needs of the Municipality otherwise reprogram them to other priority PPAs; and (iii) continue to identify development projects that are doable and more beneficial to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management installed measures for the immediate implementation of PPAs and the optimum utilization of the 20% DF. The Municipal Mayor reprogrammed projects which were not congruent to the current needs of its constituents. The Municipal Engineer monitored the implementation of all PPAs and coordinated with the MPDC for reprogramming. The MPDC coordinated with the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant for the preparation of Status Reports.
observations.reason: As at December 31, 2021, 58 PPAs were not yet completed. Delayed preparation of Program of Works (POW) and late approval of PPAs caused delays; reports submitted to the Audit Team were incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the creation of plantilla positions for the LDRRM Office and thereafter, instruct the Human Resource and Management Officer to facilitate the immediate hiring of LDRRM personnel in compliance with Section 12 of RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 04, 2014. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) update the DRRM Plan with the inclusion of a Business Continuity Plan; and (ii) explore feasible means, in view of funding constraints, to include in the LDRRM Fund the procurement of the aforementioned lacking equipment and tools, the construction of warehouse, and provision of adequate facilities for all evacuation centers to become compliant with the minimum standards on disaster preparedness.
observations.managementAction: Management installed measures and the plantilla positions in the MDRRMO were already filled up by the Management. The construction of warehouse and the procurement of lacking equipment and tools were already provided funds in CY 2022 Annual Budget. Most departments started collating data and consolidating for the formulation of the BCP/PSCP.
observations.reason: Most of the departments were not yet able to prepare and submit their own consolidation and required documents; implementation is ongoing and some items are funded in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The members of the BAC and the requesting unit concerned to provide information on the kind of rice procured in the said POs for further evaluation of the transactions. Moreover, the members of the BAC must ensure that the complete and detailed item description is included in the PO, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-010, and comply with the posting requirements of GPPB Circular No. 2020-001. Henceforth, the BAC members are advised to be updated and adhere to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on procurement. b. The LFC, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer, if necessary, to regularly prepare and submit the Monthly Reports on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of PPAs of the BGCM pursuant to Section 4.2 of DBM LBC No. 125; and c. The Municipal Accountant, the members of the BAC, and other offices concerned to submit the lacking required documents and rice distribution list to the Audit Team for further evaluation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum No. 65, series of 2021, requiring the BAC Chairman and its members to provide the Audit Team with relevant information on the kind of rice procured and to comply with the posting requirements of GPPB Circular No. 2020-001. The BAC members attended webinars to update them on procurement rules and regulations.
observations.reason: POs issued in CY 2021 were still lacking the complete specifications; distribution list was still incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The BAC and requesting units provide details including the kind of rice procured and explanation/justification on the high price of the rice and various medical supplies procured; ensure completeness of documentation (Mayor’s Permit, PhilGEPS registration etc.) and posting requirements; b. The LFC, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer, prepare and submit the monthly reports on BGCM fund utilization and status of implementation; and c. The Municipal Accountant and BAC to submit the lacking required documents and rice distribution list to the Audit Team for further evaluation pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum No. 65, series of 2021, requiring the BAC Chairman and its members to provide the Audit Team with relevant information on the kind of rice procured and to comply with the posting requirements. The Municipal Accountant submitted most of the lacking supporting documents. Management disclosed that the remaining unutilized funds were already refunded in May 2022.
observations.reason: POs issued in CY 2021 were still lacking the complete specifications and distribution list was still incomplete.
