We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant - Designate give utmost priority in analyzing and determining the composition of these long outstanding Receivables. Management should consider the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 and request for write-off of long outstanding receivables for which the LGU could not support its validity and propriety and could not establish rightful claims.

We recommended that Management:
Submit the identified lacking documents to support the public bidding conducted and strictly observe the documentary and procedural requirements of the Procurement Law to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance.
Comply with the standard format of bidding documents as prescribed under Government Procurement and Policy Board (GPPB) Resolution No. 06-2010 dated December 17, 2010 and supply correct and complete information thereof to maintain reliability and integrity thereof.
Require the contractor to post a Performance Security prior to the signing of contract in adherence to the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
Impose deduction of retention money from progress billings or provision of irrevocable standby letter of credit until 50% work completion and a satisfactory work is proven.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers are complete to prove the validity and legality of the claims and to avoid the suspension/disallowance of the transactions.

Require all officials and employees concerned to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances.
Stop granting additional cash advances to those officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances. The Municipal Accountant should issue a certification that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before granting additional advances.
Discontinue granting cash advances on account of infrastructure projects or other undertaking on project basis. Accordingly, Management should submit justifiable reason for granting cash advances for such purposes or Notice of Suspension shall be issued.
Immediately stop the practice of transferring cash advances from one accountable officer to another.
Discontinue granting of cash advances to elective officials for purposes other than travelling expenses.

We recommended that Management enforce the immediate settlement of the suspended transactions by the submission of complete supporting documents required in the Notices of Suspension. Otherwise, the appropriate Notice of Disallowance shall be issued immediately as the prescribed period to settle the suspended transactions have already lapsed.

We strictly recommended that Management immediately transfer the unexpended balance of the DRRMF to a special trust fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-002. The Special Trust Fund account shall be used to record transfers of the agency’s unutilized QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE of previous years, receipts of NDRRMF, DRRMF from other LGUs and other sources.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management maintain the required subsidiary ledgers for transfer of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer, receipts of DRRMF by transferring agency, DRRM from other LGUs and other sources by donor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately reconcile the Cash in Bank accounts appearing in the books of accounts at year-end against the balances per bank statements and those confirmed by the depository bank and make the necessary adjustments to present the correct balance of Cash in Bank account at year-end.

We recommended that Management immediately submit for audit all subject DVs together with the related supporting documents, as the corresponding Notice of Disallowance for the amount involved will be issued for non-submission thereof.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate submission of the required documents to justify the legality or validity of the disbursements. Also, for future disbursement of funds, the required supporting documents should accompany the Disbursement Voucher before payment and to comply with the proper disbursement procedures to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the procedural requirements in resorting to Negotiated Procurement as a result of Two Failed Biddings prescribed by the procurement law to ensure that the principles of transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency, and economy in the procurement process are carried out.
Submit the identified lacking documents to support the procurement activities conducted and strictly observe the documentary requirements of the Procurement Law and other related COA Issuances to preclude the occurrence of irregular transactions, to wit:
Proof of posting of invitation or request for submission of price quotation in the PhilGEPS website, website of the procuring agency, if available, and at any conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the procuring agency for a period of seven calendar days,
Performance and warranty securities,
Proof of posting of Notice of Award in the PhilGEPs website, the website of the procuring entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place the premises of the procuring entity,
Approved Procurement Plan,
Statement of the prospective bidder that it is not blacklisted or barred from bidding by the Government or any of its agencies, offices, corporations or LGUs,
Sworn affidavit of the bidder that it is not related to the HOPE by consanguinity or affinity up to the third civil degree,
BAC Resolution recommending award of contract to Lowest Calculated Responsive Bid (LCRB)
Inspection and acceptance report signed "Inspected by" by the authorized agency inspector and signed "Accepted by" by the authorized end-user to whom the item was delivered or the property officer if item is for stock
Agency's offer for negotiations with selected suppliers, contractors or consultants, and
Evidence of invitation of observers in all stages of the negotiation.

We recommended that the Accounting Office immediately submit all paid disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents, official receipts, journals and financial reports for the period January to June 2016. Accordingly, Management is enjoined to strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-006 on the submission of accounts for post-audit.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to stop using the cash advances for payroll fund to pay other expenses. Cash advances for salaries and wages should be equal to the net amount of the payroll for a pay period.
Strictly comply with the documentary requirements required in the liquidation of the cash advances. Accordingly, immediately submit the pertinent supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 in order to preclude the occurrence of irregular transactions.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that that all disbursement vouchers must specify the true nature of the expenses to facilitate proper classification and posting of expenses and in order to fulfill the provision of PD 1445 that claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management fast tract the preparation of its Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan for submission and review by EMB Regional Office No. 02 and for approval by the National Solid Waste Management Commission.

We recommend that Management strictly follow the guidelines on the entitlement, release, use, reporting and audit of Confidential Fund (CF) and/or Intelligence Fund (IF) prescribed in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management direct the concerned personnel to immediately settle their respective outstanding cash advances to comply with Section 89 of PD No. 1445; otherwise, effect the withholding of their salary equivalent to the unsettled amount, if they are still in the active service and institute appropriate legal action against those officials and employees with unsettled accountability.   Likewise, locate the other officials who were transferred to other agency/ies and demand the immediate liquidation thereof.
The Local Chief Executive, with the assistance of the Municipal Accountant, committed that those cash advances will be liquidated the soonest possible time as they have done before which reduced the unliquidated cash advances by 75.75%   during the year.

For the above, we recommended to the Local Chief Executive to:
require the concerned accountable officers to remit their collections intact and within the prescribed period in accordance with existing rules and regulations;
investigate the causes of infractions and subject the erring accountable officers to appropriate administrative sanctions; and
require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to enhance her reviewing functions over the flows of accountable forms and ensure that daily collection reports by collecting officers are extensively checked and verified to eliminate the non-reporting of official receipts and non-remittance of collections.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to be more careful in handling petty cash to ensure that all replenishments are fully accounted and supported by proper vouchers to avoid double replenishment; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer  to strengthen mechanism in recording petty cash transactions of the  Municipality in order to  avoid such lapses.

We reiterated our previous year recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
require the conduct of a complete physical count of its properties, prepare the report thereon and reconcile its results with the records maintained at the Accounting Office; and
monitor its progress, by setting a timetable within which to finish the count.

We recommended that management minimize the expenditures representing payroll costs related to Solid Waste Management Program.  To attain the socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes, the management should ensure that only qualified projects are programmed and implemented under the 20% Development Fund.
The Local Chief Executive commented that those JOs are used/utilized  not only for the solid waste management program of the municipality but also for the implementation  of the clean and green program projects and the maintenance  of  drainage systems and  de-clogging of canals which are all generally correlated..

We reiterated our previous year recommendation that the Management refrain from using DRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not directly related with disaster preparedness, mitigation, and response and rescue operations to have sufficient funds for calamity purposes.

We reiterated our previous year recommendation that the MSWMB involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation on the existing environmental laws; intensify the information campaign on the “no segregation, no collection policy and the “no-littering policy” among the residents; and exercise strong political will to implement the laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under Republic Act No. 9003.

We recommended that the management stop the practice of paying expenditures which are not included in the priority expenses chargeable against SEF as provided under Section 1 of R.A. 5447, Section 272 of R.A. 7160 and Deped-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 01, s. 1998 dated April 14, 1998, No. 01-A, March 14, 2000, No. 01-B, dated June 25, 2001.

We reiterated our previous year recommendation that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) assist the Sangguniang Bayan in the formulation of GAD Code that will support the LGU’s efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s human rights, women’s economic empowerment and gender-responsive governance towards the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment; and
b.  the Municipal Mayor ensure that GFPS structures and compositions will be in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We reiterated our previous year recommendation that the GAD Focal Point System Executive Committee and Technical Working Group:
review the GAD Plan and Budget to determine programs, projects, and activities that are responsive to gender gaps or issues faced by their women and men employees, as well as their clients and constituents.; and
utilize the GAD Fund only for programs, projects, and activities that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01

We  recommended  that the municipality strictly follow  COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 regarding the information and publicity on the PPAs of the Government Agencies to promote good governance through transparency and accountability to the people and to enable this Office to comply with the submission of the Quarterly Report of the PPAs to the Regional Director for monitoring compliance.

Replace the existing tarpaulins posted in the particular areas where the projects are located.  Follow strictly the design and format of the tarpaulin signboards as prescribed in Section 2.2.3 of the circular and avoid displaying  or affixing pictures associated with the political leaders of the implementing agency.
The Local Chief Executive informed us that it was not his order to include his pictures in the tarpaulin but the contractor who posts the name of the project/s.  He had already instructed the concern contractor to replace the signage.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
enforce the strict implementation of municipal ordinances relevant to the operation of three wheeled public conveyances; and
consider the necessity of hiring additional traffic personnel to man the critical or accident-prone areas and provide the necessary communication equipment and facilities for improved information dissemination.
We recommended that Management continue remitting the due to GSIS in  compliance with Section 3.4 of R.A. 8291.
We recommended that management continue remitting the withheld taxes from different sources in compliance with the regulations set by the BIR.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require all those named liable in the Notice of Disallowance to refund the amount disallowed and to settle immediately those requirements stated in the Notice of Suspensions to avoid its eventual maturity to disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare JEV to adjust the Cash in Bank – LCCA and Accounts Payable due to stale checks.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to make the necessary adjustment of the cashbook balance and to notify the payees for replacement of the stale checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to restore and retain the account Due from NGAs totaling P43,713.90 in the books of accounts of the Municipality, until the authority to write off is filed and granted.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor file a request for the authority to write-off the dormant Due from NGAs in the total amount of P43,713.90, in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for inventory items, in compliance with Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the JEV to record and account for all the property and equipment acquired out of Trust Fund, and to furnish the Municipal Treasurer with the subject JEV in order to include those properties in the property records of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to exert extra efforts to account and record all parcels of land owned by the Municipality of Rizal and to reconcile their records based on the available documents.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to initiate action for the titling of the parcels of land owned by the Municipality to protect its interest against third party claims and illegal disposition.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to initiate the appropriation of available funds for the cost of titling of the parcels of land covered only by Certificates of Tax Declaration named to the Municipality of Rizal.
Likewise, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Assessor to look into the possibility for the revaluation of the parcels of land owned by the Municipality in accordance with the policies and guidelines on the measurement of cost after recognition prescribed under paragraphs 43, 45, and 51 of PPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to make the appropriate classification of the PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 prescribing the Revised Chart of Account for Local Government Units.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
require the Municipal Treasurer to maintain complete Local Road Network Property Cards;
require the Municipal Accountant to record the beginning balance of the local road network in the general ledger and its component in the Local Road Network Ledger Card and to disclose the required information on Local Road Network System  in the Notes to Financial Statements;  and,
instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects in compliance with COA Circular No.  2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately provide and maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for the special accounts in compliance with Section 313 of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 110 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and submit to the Audit Team the financial statements and trial balances with the necessary supporting schedules of each special account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accountant the Monthly Reports of Checks Issued, Cash Disbursements, and the Report of Collections and Deposits within the time frame required under Section 6.03 of COA Circular No 95-006.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to turn over the paid disbursements vouchers, payrolls, and receipts and other documents evidencing the transactions to the Auditor within ten (10) days from date of receipt of said documents, pursuant to Section 6.04 also of COA Circular No. 95-006.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit immediately trial balances and financial statements to the Office of the Auditor, in compliance with Section 70 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Management direct the Inventory Team to conduct complete physical count of all PPE and require the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer to maintain updated PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively, to facilitate its reconciliation with the RPCPPE. Should there be any discrepancy between these records, the same should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected PPE account balances for its fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position.
We also recommended that Management prepare the IIRUP for unserviceable properties for eventual disposal to decongest the space occupied by these assets and generate additional income therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement to the Auditor together with copies of bank statements, debit/credit memos, cancelled and paid checks and all other supporting documents necessary to facilitate its timely verification; and (b) supply the necessary information, nature, and description of reconciling items for better appreciation and to determine its proper disposition.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate liquidation of cash advances totaling P87,774.47.  We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) strictly monitor and fully observe the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances; and (b) see to it that no additional cash advance is granted to officials/employees unless their previous cash advances are fully settled and that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of each year.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 for the proper accounting and control of fuel consumption. We also recommended that the concerned drivers prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels to COA to fill up/provide relevant information on the Driver’s Trip Ticket.

We recommended that the Municipality allocate at least 1% of their annual IRA for child protection program as required under RA No. 9344 and to create their LCPC and formulate and implement a comprehensive plan specifically to strengthen, develop and protect children in the Municipality.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board develop a comprehensive Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan (ESWMP) that will ensure the long-term management of solid waste, as well as integrate the various solid waste management programs and strategies of its barangays through consultations with the various sectors of the community. Ensure the viability and effective implementation of solid waste management programs embodied in the ESWMP and monitor its implementation.
We recommended that the BAC faithfully comply with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR to ensure competition, efficiency and transparency in the procurement process.

We recommend that the Management strictly observe the provisions of RA 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods to promote efficiency, competition and transparency in the procurement process. Comply with the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 on the complete documentation of disbursements to establish their validity, legality and propriety.

We recommended that the BAC and the Municipal Engineering Office ensure that detailed engineering activities are conducted prior to bidding and award of infrastructure projects. We also recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR to enhance efficiency and transparency of government procurement. We further recommended that all claims against government funds be supported with complete documentation to establish the validity and propriety of the disbursements.

We recommended that the Local School Board (a) prioritize the expenditures chargeable against the SEF in accordance with Sections 100 and 272 of RA No. 7160; and (b) see that the SEF is utilized solely for priority activities that contribute to the attainment of its objectives for the benefit of public elementary and high school students.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund to enhance the resilience of the constituents should disaster and calamities arise in the future.  We also recommended that the LDRRM Council decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the 20% Development Fund to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator ensure that the programs, projects and activities under the 20% Development Fund are specifically itemized to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation.  Identify, first and foremost, the programs and projects that must be prioritized for inclusion and consideration in the Annual Investment Plan.  For those which are inconsistent with the governing rules and regulations, initiate amendments to address the needs of the constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor exert efforts to enforce the settlement of suspensions and disallowances. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly monitor the settlement of suspensions and disallowances.

We recommended that management effect the transfer of its Public Infrastructure accounts to the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts of the LGU pursuant to the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards and COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following department heads for the compliance with the requirements of the COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015:
a. Municipal Accountant
1.  Prepare the Journal Voucher to record the beginning balance of the local road    network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card (Annex D of the Circular), respectively;
2. Support the JV with the Registry of the Public Infrastructures for the Roads, working paper on the distribution of costs for the road components, and working paper on the 	determination of the depreciated replacement cost for road components with no available 	cost per registry;
3.  Keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and
4. Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year.
b. Municipal General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer
Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and
2. Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. (Annex E of the Circular)
c. Municipal Engineer
Provide the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Property Officer to facilitate the submission of the updated list of inventory of insurable properties with the GSIS so that the necessary insurance coverage for all the assets can be applied with the GSIS General Insurance Fund.

We recommended  that the Bids and Awards Committee prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report for the 1st semester of CY 2017 to GPPB website on July 14, 2017; submit the CY 2018 Annual Procurement Plan-Common-use Supplies and Equipment to DBM on November 15, 2017 and prepare the APCPI for CY 2016 during the 1st quarter of CY 2017. The same shall be done on each ensuing semesters and years thereafter.

We recommended that the municipal mayor will require the Municipal Planning and Development Council to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund so that all its programmed development projects could be implemented immediately to ensure that intended beneficiaries avail of the maximum benefits that will be derived therefrom.

We have recommended the municipality to formulate more realistic and doable socio-economic programs and projects and prioritize their implementation according to the needs of the constituency to avoid constant reprogramming and reversion. The Executive Committee must ensure the decisions of the council to faithfully carry out and implement so that the public may receive the entire benefits that they may be able to derive from the projects.

Require coordination between the Planning, Engineering, Treasury, Accounting, DSWD, DILG, and other concerned divisions/offices to fast track the implementation of BUB projects especially so that these projects are intended to provide benefits to the municipality’s grassroots population.
Require the BUB focal person to regularly monitor the status of proposed BUB projects as to funding and implementation.

We recommended to the municipality that all construction/improvements, repairs and maintenance of municipal properties/facilities have to be supported with estimate of work, labor costs, drawings/plans specifications and program of work to prevent wastage of materials and losses of government resources.

We recommended that management should:
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the monthly Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Fund and submit to the COA Auditor thru the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) every 15th day of the month.
LDRRM Officers must strictly comply with the accounting and reporting requirements stipulated in RA 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002, dated September 12, 2012, to ensure that the financial information is appropriately taken up in the books and properly presented in the financial statements with adequate disclosures.

We recommended that the municipality ensure strict compliance to the provisions of Republic Act 9003 in proper segregation, collection, transport, storage, treatment and disposal of solid waste. Ensure the implementation of segregation at source of solid waste, enforcement of “no segregation, no collection’ policy, creation of Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) in every barangay, and the development of disposal facility in the municipality.

We recommended that other basic services/ priority expenditures enumerated in Section 272 of the LGC should be considered in allocating funds of SEF so as not to deprive the constituents of the benefits from the said funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor will direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the amount of P236,118.17 to the Bureau of Internal Revenue and submit to the Office of the Auditor the proof of its remittance as soon as possible.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

create an Inventory Committee that will be responsible for the conduct of the
physical inventory count and furnish the Audit Team with the said report;
require the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and
reclassify the PPE; and

require the GSO to reconcile with the Municipal Accountant records.

complete subsidiary ledger of all accounts and facilitate a monthly reconciliation
of the Subsidiary Ledgers against the General Ledger controlling account.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to record the First
Consolidated Bank (FCB) account in the Cash in Bank - Local Currency,
Current Account.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal
Treasurer, the Municipal Accountant and other officials concerned to make
representations with the bank to furnish the Municipality with copies of debit
and credit memos to facilitate recording of reconciling items in the book of
accounts for a fair presentation of the Cash in Bank account in the financial
statements.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to submit the documents
supporting the transactions to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transmit the
identified unrecorded disbursements to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate
recording thereof in the book of accounts.

We recommended Ms. Drilon and Ms. Bungalso to submit the respective
Disbursement Vouchers of their cash advances together with the liquidation
documents te the Municipal Accountant within thirty (30) days from receipt
hereof, otherwise the Municipal Mayor is hereby instructed to issue an order for
the suspension of their salaries in accordance with the mentioned regulations
and Section 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

Henceforth, strict compliance by the Municipal Treasurer with regards to the
transmittal of the check disbursements and its supporting documents within
the prescribed period to the Municipal Accountant must be enjoined and
strictly monitored. The Municipal Treasurer must impose administrative
disciplinary

41
action against the Disbursing Officer/s who fails to submit a complete report of
disbursement including its supporting documents.

We also recommended the Management to improve the agency’s record
keeping and tracking system to ensure that every bank transaction and all other
financial information are properly documented and duly recorded in the books.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit the necessary
Journal Entry Vouchers and supporting documents to establish the Iegality and
propriety of those entries/adjustments, otherwise, reverse the undocumented
entries. Henceforth, strict compliance and observance of highest standards of
honesty is enjoined and see to it that all claims against government funds are
supported with complete documents in compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D.
1445.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to permanently relieve the Senior
Bookkeeper of his duty in the maintenance of General Ledgers and preparation
of Financial Statements. Assign the Financial Statement preparation to the
Municipal Accountant, so that he can personally attest to the accurateness of the
Municipality’s Financial Statements. Likewise, implement job rotation on the
employees as a good practice of internal control.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer and Special Disbursing Officer
to submit all the mentioned RDs together with the supporting documents to the
Municipal Accountant for recording.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to forward the same to the
Audit Team for post audit, otherwise the transaction will be suspended/
disallowed in audit.

In case of failure to render his/her accounts within thirty (30) days from
receipt hereof, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to suspend the payment
of the concerned employees salary and file administrative disciplinary action in
accordance with the above-cited regulations.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal
Accountant to fast track the completion of the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to forward the original copies
of the bank statements and cleared checks to the Audit Team as reference in
audit.

‘We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the bank for
the prompt printing of bank statements. Further, it is suggested that the
Municipal Treasurer utilize the Land Bank iaccess option for monitoring and
printing of bank transactions, which will then be forwarded to the Municipal
Accountant for timely reconciliation of the bank depository accounts.

We also recommended the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from
withholding the bank statements and cleared checks that may have come into
her custody. Likewise, she should also forward all cancelled checks to the
Municipal Accountant, who will also forward it to the auditor.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to serve a written notice to
the concerned accountable officers on their outstanding cash advances as
mandated by Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2012-004. Settlement of the
accountable officer’s account is hereby enjoined within 15 days upon receipt
hereof.

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to suspend the salaries of the
concerned accountable officers upon failure to settle their obligations.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up the
implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities funded
out of the 20% DF as per Annual Investment Program and that closer
coordination among the officials involved in planning, execution and
monitoring be undertaken to achieve the desired level of outcome.

b. Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Officer to immediately look
into the unimplemented developmental projects as embodied in the Annual
Investment Program. Identify causes of non-implementation and develop
feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement developmental
projects,

c. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be very prudent, meticulous,
conscientious and precise in evaluating the timely and perfect
implementation of each and every project. Likewise, he should also conduct
regular inspection of the projects so that accomplishment of the contractor is
monitored and evaluated based on the required accomplishment against the
number of days stipulated in the contract.

62
d. Should there have been delays incurred in the project implementation,
Liquidated Damages must be imposed from the contractors concerned. Such
amount shall be computed and deducted from any money due or which may
become due the contractor under each contract and/or collect such liquidated
damages from the retention money or other securities posted by the
contractor, whichever is convenient to the Municipality, in accordance with
Item 8.3, Annex “E” of the Revised L.LR.R. of R.A. 9184.

e. Reappropriate the unexpended balance of the completed projects of the 20%
DF to finance development PPAs identified to be funded under the 20% DF
for the ensuing year.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer
to transfer the remaining balance of the 20% DF to its separate bank account.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
to reconcile their books on the prior years’ 20% DF that remained in the bank
account of GF-Proper and submit the said report to the Audit Team for
validation.

Henceforth, practice the transfer of the entire amount of 20% DF to its
separate bank account as released by the DBM and validated deposit slip should
be attached in the disbursement voucher of 20% DF transfer.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to immediately forward the
mentioned documents to the auditor for post audit. The Municipal Accountant
should ensure that transactions recorded in the books are properly documented.

We recommended the Management to require a Board of Director’s
Resolution before the transfer of fund to provide bases for monitoring of fund
utilization and to ensure that funds were properly used for its intended purpose.

‘We recommended the Municipal Accountant to initially take up the fund
transfers in the books as Due from LGUs and subsequently be recorded as
Subsidy - Others after liquidation was submitted and evaluated. The Municipal
Accountant should immediately submit the liquidation documents covering the
transaction for CY 2016 to the Audit Team for post audit.

We also recommended the ABC Secretary to submit all of the minutes of
the the Board of Director’s Resolutions covering the mentioned disbursements
for validation. He should submit a certify copy of the Fidelity Bond Documents
of the Liga Treasurer and Liga’s Bank Statement in accordance with Section 505
of the Local Government Code.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to issue the accountable forms
to bonded accountable officers duly authorized to issue the same. We also
recommended the Municipal Treasurer to recover all Accountable #51 issued to
Ms. Gleizle C. Magbanua.

We recommended the MSWDO to strictly adhere to the rules and
regulations in the granting and utilization of funds release to NGOs/POs. We
also recommended the MSWDO to refrain from entering into a MOA with
NGOs/POs as the same is not among her listed powers and duties enumerated in
Section 483 of the Local Government Code.

Henceforth, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to initially take
up the release of grants to NGOs/POs as Due from Non-Government
Organizations/People's Organizations and subsequently recorded as Donations
upon submission of the liquidations and required reports.

We recommended the HRMO to submit the required documentary
requirements and deduct the 5 days annual forced/mandatory leave for the
second claim, and direct the former Mayor to refund the amount received for
the first claim.

‘We recommended the Municipal Budget Officer to submit an explanation
on the funding source of the claims.

We recommended that the:
a. Management strictly adhere to the Procurement Law.

b. Municipal Accountant return all liquidations of the Disbursing Officer that
is not covered by an original Obligation Request/s and those that is not
included in the original list of expense/s to be paid out of the cash advance.

¢. Municipal Budget Officer indicate the nature of transaction in the Obligation
Request to determine the appropriateness of the claim.

d. Municipal Treasurer make the necessary claims payable through checks in
accordance with Section 93 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 which states that:

Section 93. To whom warrants or checks payable. Warrants chargeable to
revenue or trust funds of the national government or checks drawn against
the Treasury Checking Account for Agencies maintained with any
government depository shall be made payable either directly to the creditor to
whom the money is due or to a disbursing officer for disbursement.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to mandate the Municipal
Engineer to immediately submit a complete list of all on-going PPAs and those
that are to be implemented for year 2016 and the ensuing year, containing all
necessary information relative thereto, otherwise, he should be subject to
disciplinary action in accordance with Section 4.0 of the same Circular, to wit:

Xxx. Failure of the concerned agency to comply with any of the
provisions of this Circular shall be subject of administrative action
provided under Section 127 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 without
prejudice to the filing of a criminal action, if warranted by existing
laws.

We recommended the management to impose liquidated damages to the
defaulted Contractors in compliance with Section 68 of the Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to create an Inventory Committee
and enjoin the concerned department heads’ compliance with the requirements
of COA Circular No. 2015-008 and submit the corresponding reports to the
Audit Team for evaluation and verification.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary entry
to remove the inventoriable items from the PPE. Further, we recommended the
Municipal GSO to issue an Inventory Custodian Slip to the Accountable Officer,
who is the actual custodian of these items. The Municipal GSO should make a
separate database/lists of these items for monitoring purposes.

We reiterated our recommendation last year that the Municipal
Accountant reclassify the asset. The Management should assess the useful life of
the assets to determine the related amortization or any impairment in its cost to
reflect its carrying amount.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to record the depreciation for
those PPE and ensure recognition of the monthly depreciation for all the PPE of
the Municipality for fair presentation in the financial statement.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to apply the change in
accounting policy to determine the impact on the Financial Statements and
consequently disclose it to the Notes to Financial Statements. We also
recommended the Municipal Accountant to fill out the acquisition dates of the
PPE for the determination and accuracy of accumulated depreciation as at year-
end.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to reconstruct the
Subsidiary Ledgers of each bank accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the cashbook of
all bank accounts of the Municipality against the Subsidiary Ledgers. We also
recommended the Municipal Treasurer to require all Accountable Officers to
reconcile their respective records with the subsidiary ledgers of the Accounting
Office.

The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant should appoint one
focal person per office, who will be tasked to monitor and do the reconciliation
of the two records. Both the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
must identify the discrepancies between the cashbook and the general ledger so
that necessary adjustments can be effected soonest to bring the two records in
agreement.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to send a written notice to the
concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes stale. Moreover, we
require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the
books for the long outstanding/stale checks in accordance with Section 59,
Volume I of the NGAS Manual.

‘We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the previous SDO to
reconcile her accountabilities against the records of the Municipal Accountant
and settle any remaining balances.

We recommended the SDO to transmit her cashbook to the Audit Team for
post audit.

We recommended the Management to increase the fidelity bond of the
accountable officers to ensure full bond coverage of their cash advances,
otherwise grant cash advances within their maximum accountability.

‘We recommended the Management to strictly abide by the provisions laid
down by the Local Government Code and COA Circular No. 98-002.

We recommended the Management to fully comply and implement all the
audit findings contained in the AAR of CY 2015, and inform this Office on the
status of the mentioned findings.

We recommended that the concerned officials:

Abide with their mandates as provided under Sections 319 and 323 of Republic Act No. 7160;
Always uphold public interest over and above personal interest.; and
 Perform and discharge their duties with the highest degree of excellence, professionalism, intelligence and skill; and shall enter public service with utmost dedication to duty.

We recommended that Management observe strict implementation of the municipality’s local disaster program aimed at addressing the community disaster preparedness and understanding of disaster contingency planning at all levels, thus, enhancing the capacities of the municipality and its constituents to effectively understand, prepare, respond and recover from the impacts of natural and man-made disasters.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular Nos. 97-002, 2009-002 and Section 4 of PD 1445 in order to forestall the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances. 

Stop granting additional cash advances to those officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances. 

Discontinue granting cash advances on account of infrastructure projects or other undertaking on project basis. 

Require all officials and employees concerned to immediately settle all unliquidated cash advances.

Submit the supporting documents of cash advances granted. In addition, Management should clearly indicate the specific legal purpose of the cash advances to be made on the face of the DV and Obligation Request. 

Require the concerned municipal officials to review the disbursement transactions and affix their signatures on the spaces provided in the DV.

Management also sent demand letters to concerned employees and individuals and significant liquidations have been achieved.

Management is studying its available options for the unliquidated cash advance of the deceased former municipal mayor.

The deficiencies having such financial impact, we recommended that Local  Chief Executive -

a.)require the conduct of a complete physical count of its properties, prepare the report thereon and reconcile its results with the records maintained at the Accounting Office; and

b.)monitor its progress, by setting a timetable within which to finish the count.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive-

a.)require the Municipal Treasurer to keep and maintain Stock Cards for each category of supplies/materials to be used in recording the items and quantity of supplies/materials received and issued.  Likewise, instruct her to prepare the SSMI to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant and to establish the quantities of supplies/materials still on hand as at year end and report to the Municipal Accountant in the SSMI, for proper adjustment in the books;

b.)instruct the Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain the Supplies Ledger Card (SLC) to be used in recording the quantity and amount of supplies/materials purchased and issued during a given month. Require him to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the amount of supplies/materials on hand as of December 31, 2015, based on the submitted SSMI of the Municipal Treasurer, in order to reflect in the books the actual amount in the inventory; and

c.)provide a stock room for storage of supplies and materials purchased for stock, which could accommodate the same prior to issuance to the end users.

We recommended that management utilize the 20% Development Fund in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 to contribute in the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management, for the benefits of the intended beneficiaries, and to avoid the sanctions provided under RA No. 7160 and other applicable laws.

We recommended that the Management refrain from using LDRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not directly related with disaster preparedness, mitigation, and response and rescue operations to have sufficient funds for calamity purposes.

We recommended that the MSWMB and the MENRO-

a.)exert effort to expedite the establishment and completion of the Sanitary Landfill;
 
b.)involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation on the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign on the “no segregation, no collection policy and the “no-littering policy” among the residents; and 

c.)exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under Republic Act  No. 9003.

We recommended that-

a.)The GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) assists the Sangguniang Bayan in the formulation of GAD Code that will support the LGU’s efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s human rights, women’s economic empowerment and gender-responsive governance towards the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment; and

b.)The Municipal Mayor ensures that GFPS structures and compositions will be in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System Executive Committee and Technical Working Group-

a.)review the GAD Plan and Budget to determine programs, projects, and activities that are responsive to gender gaps or issues faced by their women and men employees, as well as their clients and constituents.; and 

b.)utilize the GAD Fund only for programs, projects, and activities that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01

We recommended that the municipality follow strictly COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 regarding the information and publicity on the PPAs of the Government Agencies to promote good governance through transparency and accountability to the people and to enable this Office to comply with the submission of the Quarterly Report of the PPAs to the Regional Director for monitoring compliance

Lastly we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit a written explanation on why the BAC Members and other concerned Municipal Officials should not be held liable for their failure to recognize the inappropriateness of the documents submitted.

Lastly, we recommended that the Local Development Council explain why it included non-developmental PPAs in its of list priority projects despite the recommendation of the audit team to use 20% DF only for developmental projects.

Accountant to turn-over all the
Bank Statements, cleared checks
and other necessary documents,
properly inventoried and
transmitted to the Bank Recon
Team to facilitate the preparation
of the BRS.

c. Investigate the issue at hand and
file administrative and criminal
charges in relation to Article 218
of the Revised Penal Code for
failure of the accountable officer
to render accounts, if warranted.
